steps.m: to point to or indicate something

steps.n: The root letters combine with a shadda (doubled letter) to make a word that asks 'which' or 'who'
Adding damma shows this word is the subject being addressed
Adding يٰٓ at the beginning makes this a call for attention
Adding ها makes this a way to call out to someone
This is the basic form of the word that connects descriptions to people or things
Adding ن at the end makes it refer to many people instead of just one
Adding ال at the beginning and a shadda makes it point to specific people. This word describes the believers who are being addressed in this verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding alif after the hamza changes the meaning to actively choosing to believe or have faith

steps.m: they believed (referring to those who believe in يٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ)
steps.n: The وا at the end shows that a group of people (all men or mixed group) did this action in the past. This word describes the believers being addressed in the phrase 'O you who believe'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Adding وا changes it from 'he' to 'they'

steps.m: to bind or make an agreement

steps.n: Root letters got vowels to make a noun meaning one specific contract/agreement
Makes the word plural to show multiple contracts
Added ال to make it definite and ِ at end to show it follows a preposition
Added preposition ب meaning 'with/by'. This word is the object of the command أَوْفُوا۟ (fulfill) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
The middle letter gets a shadda to show emphasis and completeness of the action
Adding hamza at the beginning makes it causative - showing someone made something happen
Changing the vowels to show this happened to something rather than someone doing it
Adding ت at the end shows this refers to a feminine thing (like a rule or permission)
Added fatha to show the basic vowel of the particle ل
Added ك with damma to show it refers to multiple people being addressed

steps.m: for you all (object of أُحِلَّتْ - was made permissible)
steps.n: Added م to complete the plural pronoun كم. This word shows who the things were made permissible for in أُحِلَّتْ (was made permissible)

steps.m: to be silent, unclear, or without speech

steps.n: Added vowels and ي to create an adjective describing something that cannot speak
Added ة to specify one instance of something that cannot speak, which came to refer specifically to animals/beasts

steps.m: beast, animal (subject of أُحِلَّتْ - was made lawful)
steps.n: Added ُ to show this word is the subject of the verb أُحِلَّتْ (was made lawful) in this ayah

steps.m: to be blessed with comfort and abundance

steps.n: Root letters for 'blessing' formed into a noun meaning 'livestock' - animals that are a blessing from God
Changed from single animal to plural form (like saying 'cow' to 'cows') by adding letters at start and middle
Added the definite article to specify these particular livestock
Added kasra (i sound) at the end to show this word belongs to or is owned by something
Added kasra under إ to help with pronunciation
Added a shadda (double letter mark) on ل to make the word mean 'except'. It introduces something that will be mentioned next as an exception.
This is a relative pronoun connecting to يُتْلَىٰ (is recited). It refers to the things being recited or explained.

steps.m: to follow in sequence or recite

steps.n: Changed from basic root form to past tense 'he' form by adding vowels and turning و into ا at the end
Changed to passive form by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. The ى at the end shows it's happening to something
Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's a long vowel sound
This is a common preposition in Arabic meaning 'upon' or 'on'. Since it's a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.
The ى changes to ي with sukun when adding a suffix. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect meaning.
The suffix كُمْ shows this is addressing multiple people (you all)
Root letters got vowels to make a word meaning 'not' or 'other than'
Added fatha at the end to show this word is the object of the verb أُحِلَّتْ (was made lawful)
Added vowels (َ) to make it 'he made lawful' in past tense
Added مُ prefix and ِ vowel to show who does the action, then doubled the last ل with shadda
Added ى at the end to make it a relative adjective (like saying 'pertaining to making lawful')
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and form a noun meaning the act of hunting or the prey itself
Added ٱل (the) at the beginning and doubled the ص with shadda because it comes after ٱل
Added kasra at the end because this word is possessed by محِلِّى (those who make permissible) in the ayah
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation: fatha on أ, damma on ت, and sukun on م
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it
Added damma vowels to create a plural noun describing people in a sacred state (ihram)

steps.m: those in a sacred state (while you are)
steps.n: Added tanween (double damma) to show this describes the state of the people being addressed in وَأَنتُمْ (and you are)
Added kasra vowel mark to help with pronunciation
Added shadda to make this an emphasis word. This shows that what comes after it (Allah) is being emphasized as important.
Added emphasis by doubling the ل letter

steps.m: Allah (as the object being acted upon by يَحْكُمُ - He judges)
steps.n: Changed the first letter to hamzat al-wasl (ٱ) and added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being judged
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changes past tense to present tense. This word shows what Allah does, as it comes after 'Indeed, Allah' (إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ) in the ayah.

In this ayah, مَا connects with يُتْلَىٰ (is recited) to mean 'that which is recited'. It's referring to the exceptions that will be recited or explained later in the Quran about which animals are not permissible to eat.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make past tense verb
Added يُ at start to show present tense, and changed vowels to match present tense pattern

steps.m: to point to or direct attention to something

steps.n: Root letters أيي combined with vowels and doubled middle letter (shadda) to make the question word 'which/what'
Added damma (u sound) at the end to show it's asking 'which one?' as a question
Added ها to make it into a way of addressing people
Added يا (ya) at the start - this is how we say 'Hey!' or 'O!' when calling out to someone in Arabic
This is a special word that connects descriptions to people or things
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Adding alif after hamza changes the meaning from just feeling safe to actively putting trust in something (believing)
Added plural ending وا to show many people believing. This word describes 'those who' (الَّذِينَ) in the phrase 'O you who believe'
This is a negative particle that tells us not to do something. Here it's telling us not to do تُحِلُّوا۟ (violate/make lawful). It's like adding 'don't' before a verb in English.
Added a fatha mark (َ) on the ل to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'laa'
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changed from past tense 'he made lawful' to a command 'make lawful' by changing middle vowel and doubling the last letter
Adding تُ changes it to 'you' (2nd person)

steps.m: you all make lawful (after لا meaning 'do not')
steps.n: Added وا at end to show multiple people are addressed
Added vowels and ي to make singular noun for one ritual/symbol

steps.m: rituals, symbols (object of تُحِلُّوا - do not violate)
steps.n: Single ritual/symbol (شَعِيرَة) becomes many rituals/symbols (شَعٰٓئِر) - like changing 'flag' to 'flags' but in a special Arabic way. These are what we shouldn't violate.
Added emphasis by doubling the ل sound
Changed to show possession or connection by adding kasra at the end. Also changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word.
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it. This word negates ٱلْهَدْىَ (the sacrificial animals) in this ayah.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and form the noun 'month'. A month marks/divides time, which connects to the root meaning of marking or making visible.
Added shadda to strengthen pronunciation after adding ال prefix
Added ال (the) at the beginning to make it specific
Added fatha at the end to show this word is receiving the action of the verb تُحِلُّوا (you all make lawful)

steps.m: to make something off-limits or sacred

steps.n: Root ح-ر-م with alif makes حَرَام meaning 'forbidden' - like turning 'forbid' into 'forbidden'
Added 'ال' to make it specific and 'َ' to show it describes الْبَيْتَ (the House/Ka'bah). Together they form الْبَيْتَ ٱلْحَرَامَ meaning 'the Sacred House'
This is a negative particle that negates what comes after it
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This word negates 'the sacred month' (ٱلشَّهْرَ ٱلْحَرَامَ) in this ayah.

steps.m: to guide or show the way

steps.n: Changes from 'guiding' to mean a specific type of offering that guides people to worship. This word refers to animals offered as sacrifice during Hajj.
Adding the definite article ال makes the noun definite, specifying this is 'the' sacrificial animal being referenced

steps.m: the sacrificial animal (which is being violated)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is receiving the action of تُحِلُّوا (violate/make lawful). The verse is saying not to violate the sanctity of these sacrificial animals.
This is a negative particle that makes the thing after it forbidden or prohibited. In this ayah, it's used with other similar phrases to list multiple sacred things that should not be violated.
The و connects this prohibition to the previous one, creating a list of sacred things that should not be violated

steps.m: to decorate or adorn with necklaces

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun for a single necklace by changing the first vowel and adding ة
Changed from one necklace to many necklaces by adding letters in the middle of the word
Added 'the' at the beginning and the accusative case ending because it's the object of the verb تُحِلُّوا (do not violate)
This is a negative particle that negates the word ٱلْقَلٰٓئِدَ (the garlanded animals) in this ayah
Added و at the beginning to connect this prohibition with the previous ones in the verse
The alif is elongated (آ) to emphasize the negation. This is a pronunciation change that doesn't affect the meaning

steps.m: to head toward or seek something

steps.n: Connected the root letters and added a shadda to show 'he did the action'
Changed from past tense 'he headed' to present continuous 'heading' by adding alif at start
Added plural ending ين with kasra before it to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: house/dwelling (object of آمِّين - those heading towards)
steps.n: Changed root into a noun by adding fatha and sukun. This word is the object being aimed for by آمِّين (those heading towards).

steps.m: the Sacred House (Ka'bah) (object of آمِّين - those heading towards)
steps.n: Added ال to make it definite and َ to show it's being acted upon. In this context, البيت refers specifically to the Ka'bah in Mecca.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding alif turns the verb 'forbade' into a noun meaning 'something forbidden'

steps.m: the forbidden, the sacred (describes الشَّهْرَ - the month)
steps.n: Adds 'the' at beginning and accusative case ending because it's describing 'the month' in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present tense, with sukun and kasra vowels for present pattern
Added ت after first letter to show intensive or thorough seeking

steps.m: they are seeking earnestly (referring to those coming to the Sacred House)
steps.n: Added ون ending to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: to give extra or do more than required
extra favor, blessing (what is being sought)
steps.n: Changed the root into a noun showing the result of giving extra. This word is what the people are seeking (يَبْتَغُونَ) in this verse.
The ًا ending makes it mean 'seeking a favor/blessing' instead of just 'favor/blessing'
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or its source. Here it connects 'those coming' (ءَآمِّينَ) with 'their Lord' (رَّبِّهِمْ)
The م gets doubled (shown by the ّ mark) because it comes after a long vowel sound in ءَآمِّينَ. This makes it easier to pronounce smoothly.
Root 'r-b-b' becomes 'lord/master' with kasra showing someone owns/possesses it (like 'my lord')
Added 'their' (هِمْ) at the end and doubled the first letter (رّ) because it connects to the previous word
Adding ان makes it a noun showing strong/complete pleasure
Added و to connect this word to what came before it in the verse
Added ًا to show this is something being sought (connected to يَبْتَغُونَ - 'they seek' in the verse)

steps.m: when (connects to حَلَلْتُمْ - you are free)
steps.n: Basic conditional/temporal particle that introduces a time condition. Here it connects to حَلَلْتُمْ (you are free) to show when something should happen

steps.m: and when (connects to حَلَلْتُمْ - you are free)
steps.n: Adds و at the beginning to connect this condition with the previous statements. This shows this is another part of the larger set of instructions.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action in past tense

steps.m: you all made permissible (after إِذَا meaning 'when')
steps.n: Added م to change 'I' to 'you all'. This word comes after إِذَا (when) in the ayah, showing this happens at a specific time.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
The ص changes to صط after اِ due to a common Arabic sound rule
Added فَ at beginning to show this command comes after something else

steps.m: so hunt, all of you! (plural command)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to make the command for a group instead of one person
This is the basic negative word that prevents something from happening. In this ayah, it's used with other similar phrases to list things that should not be violated.
Added و with fatha to connect this prohibition to the previous ones in the list of sacred things that should not be violated.

steps.m: to cause to commit a crime/sin
he caused (someone) to commit a sin
steps.n: Root letters connected with short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make past tense verb meaning 'he caused'

steps.m: he causes (someone) to commit a sin
steps.n: Changed to present tense with يَ at start and different vowels

steps.m: he surely causes (someone) to commit a sin
steps.n: Added نَّ for emphasis

steps.m: let not the hatred (of شَنَـَٔانُ) cause you all to commit sin
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show who is being affected. This word is connected to شَنَـَٔانُ (hatred) which is what's doing the causing.

steps.m: to hate intensely or detest strongly

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
The ان ending makes it a noun that shows strong feelings
Added damma at the end to show this word is doing the action in the sentence - it's what should not drive you to transgression
Changes verb 'to stand' into noun meaning 'people who stand together'

steps.m: of a people (who prevented you from the Sacred Mosque)
steps.n: Added two dots under the word (tanwin kasra) to show it means 'of a people' instead of just 'a people'. Like saying 'book' vs 'of a book'.
This is a connecting word that introduces what comes next. Here it connects to the word تَعْتَدُوا۟ (you transgress) to show what should not happen.
Added the fatha mark (َ) above the first letter to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'anna'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Adding shadda to middle root letter creates Form II verb with intensified meaning
The وا shows multiple people did the action

steps.m: they forcefully blocked you all (from الْمَسْجِدِ الْحَرَامِ)
steps.n: كُمْ shows this was done to multiple people. The next words tell us they were blocked from the Sacred Mosque.
This is a preposition that shows movement away from something
Added kasra at the end because the next word starts with ال. This helps connect the words smoothly when speaking. The word connects to ٱلْمَسْجِدِ (the mosque) to show what someone is away from.

steps.m: to prostrate, to put one's forehead on the ground in worship

steps.n: Adding مَ at the beginning and ِ in the middle creates a noun meaning 'the place where prostration happens'

steps.m: the place of prostration (connected to الحَرَامِ)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific (the), and the ِ ending shows it's connected to the word الحَرَامِ (sacred) that comes after it, forming 'the Sacred Mosque'

steps.m: to prevent access or make something off-limits

steps.n: Added alif after second letter to make it into a word that describes something that is completely forbidden or sacred
Added ال to make it specific and ِ to show it describes المسجد (the mosque) in this ayah

steps.m: that (connects to صَدُّوكُمْ - they prevented you)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that links two ideas together. Here it connects the idea of people's hatred (شَنَـَٔانُ) with what they did (preventing access to the mosque). It's like saying 'because' or 'that' in English.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Added اِعْتَ to show someone deliberately going beyond proper limits or boundaries
Changed to present tense for multiple people being addressed (you all)
Added تَ at start and changed pattern to show people helping each other back and forth (mutual action)
Added 'wa' (و) at the beginning which means 'and'

steps.m: and (all of you) help each other!
steps.n: Added 'و' (and) at start + 'وا' at end to make it a command for a group

This is a preposition that shows something is on top of, over, or involved with something else. In this ayah, it connects the verb 'cooperate' (وَتَعَاوَنُوا۟) with what to cooperate in (ٱلْبِرِّ - righteousness).

steps.m: to do good, to be kind and righteous

steps.n: Adding the kasra changes it from a verb (to do good) into a noun (goodness)
The kasra shows this word follows عَلَى (upon/for) in the ayah
Adding ال makes it refer to specific righteousness - in this case, the good deeds and kindness that believers should help each other with

steps.m: to protect oneself, to be careful, to avoid bad things

steps.n: Changed to noun form showing the quality or state of being protective/careful

steps.m: the state of being careful/protective (of what's right)
steps.n: Adds 'the' (ال) at the start to make it specific

steps.m: and the state of being careful/protective (of what's right) (connected to ٱلْبِرِّ)
steps.n: Connects this concept with ٱلْبِرِّ (righteousness) mentioned earlier in the verse
Starting with the basic negation particle لا which means 'not'
Added و at the beginning to connect this prohibition with the previous one in لَا تُحِلُّوا (do not violate)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start and ا after first root letter to show people helping each other

steps.m: cooperate! (all of you) (with عَلَى ٱلْبِرِّ - upon righteousness)
steps.n: Added ُوا۟ to make it a command for a group. This word is followed by عَلَى ٱلْبِرِّ which tells what to cooperate upon.

This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or against something else. In this ayah, it connects the verb 'cooperate' (وَتَعَاوَنُوا۟) with what to cooperate in (ٱلْبِرِّ - righteousness).

steps.m: to do something wrong or harmful

steps.n: Changed the word from an action into a thing by adding إِ at the start and a sukun in the middle
Added ال to point to a specific sin, and ِ at the end because it comes after the word على (upon/on)

steps.m: to cross boundaries, go beyond limits

steps.n: Root letters kept but vowels changed to make it a noun meaning 'the act of crossing limits'
Added ٰن to show intensity of the quality
Added kasra at end to show it's being acted upon by another word
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added letters at start (ٱتَّ) make it mean 'to protect oneself' instead of just 'protect'
Changes from 'he protected' to 'you all protect!' by adding -ū ending for a group command
Adds و to connect with previous commands in the verse. This is one of several commands given to believers about proper conduct.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word that became the name for God in Arabic.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of وَٱتَّقُوا۟ - fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: The word 'Allah' gets a special mark (shadda) on the ل to make it stronger, and gets a small 'a' sound (fatha) at the end because it's receiving the action of 'fear/be mindful of'

steps.m: indeed/verily (emphasizes that اللَّهَ is شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ)
steps.n: This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. It also causes the next word (اللَّهَ) to end in َ instead of ُ. It's like adding 'truly' or 'definitely' at the start of a sentence in English.
Changed the root letters into a noun form that means 'one who is worshipped'
Added an alif to make it a specific type of deity
Added 'al' to make it specific/definite, like saying 'the god' instead of just 'god'

steps.m: Allah (as the object of fear/being mindful in وَٱتَّقُوا۟)
steps.n: The word 'Allah' comes from 'al-ilah' (the god), but is a unique name. The fatha at the end shows Allah is who we should be mindful of
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
This form makes an adjective showing a strong quality. Here it describes how intense Allah's punishment is.
The ُ shows this word owns or describes what comes after it (العِقَابِ - punishment), forming the phrase 'intense in punishment'
The pattern عِقاب shows the result or consequence that follows an action. Here it means the punishment that follows wrongdoing.
Adding ال makes it specific (like 'the' in English). The ِ at the end shows it's connected to Allah's attribute of being شَدِيدُ (severe/strong) in this ayah.

steps.m: to make something off-limits or sacred

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the vowels and doubled the middle letter to show this happened to something rather than someone doing it

steps.m: it (feminine) was made forbidden (referring to the forbidden foods and animals mentioned after)
steps.n: Added ت to show this refers to feminine things (like the forbidden foods mentioned in the verse)
This is a preposition that shows something is happening above or affecting someone/something. The root letters combine to mean 'highness' or 'elevation'.
When adding a suffix, the ى changes to يْ to connect with what comes after it

steps.m: upon you all (object of حُرِّمَتْ - forbidden)
steps.n: Added كُمُ which means 'you all' when attached to the end of words. This word connects to حُرِّمَتْ (forbidden) to show what was forbidden to you all.
Changes from verb 'to die' into adjective 'dead'
Added ة to make it refer to a specific dead thing rather than just the quality of being dead

steps.m: the dead thing/carcass (subject of حُرِّمَتْ - was forbidden)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and ُ to show it's what was forbidden in the verse
The root letters د-م-و become the simple word 'blood' by dropping the و and adding a fatha mark
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the د with shadda
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and', which also changes the alif to a different form (ٱ)
Added damma at the end because this word is a subject of the verb حُرِّمَتْ (was forbidden)
و connects this word to words before it, meaning 'and'
Basic noun form of the root letters for 'meat' with short vowels added
Added و with fatha to mean 'and'
Added damma at the end to show this is one of the subjects of the verb حُرِّمَتْ (was forbidden)
This is the common noun form for pig/swine
Added ٱلْ to make it specific
Added kasra at the end because this word is possessed by لَحْمُ (meat) in the phrase لَحْمُ ٱلْخِنزِيرِ (meat of pig/pork)
This is a particle meaning 'what' that connects to أُهِلَّ (was dedicated) to ask about what was dedicated
Added و with fatha at beginning to connect this phrase to what came before
Added alif at end to show elongated vowel sound, this is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning

steps.m: to call out loudly, to raise one's voice

steps.n: Added vowel marks to make it 'he called out' in the past tense
Combined repeated letters with shadda (ّ) which shows emphasis
Changed to passive voice to show this is about what was announced/proclaimed, not who did it. In this ayah, it refers to animals that were sacrificed while announcing names of false gods.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make a noun meaning 'other than'
Added لِ at start to mean 'for/to'
Added kasra at end to show this word is connected to what comes after it
Added ال to specify this is THE one true deity, and the hamza was dropped because this is a special word used so often. The shadda shows emphasis.
Added kasra at the end to show this belongs to or follows after the word غَيْرِ (other than). The alif at the start changed to a waṣla (ٱ) because it connects to the previous word.
Added the preposition بِ which shows how something is done

steps.m: with him/it (referring to what was dedicated to other than اللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added a small alif (ۦ) after هِ to help with pronunciation. This word refers back to what was dedicated to other than Allah mentioned earlier in the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb
Changed from 'strangled' (verb) to 'one who was strangled' (feminine noun) by adding mu- at start and -ah at end
Added definite article ال to make the word definite
Added damma to indicate nominative case

steps.m: and the strangled one (feminine) (subject of حُرِّمَتْ)
steps.n: The letter و (meaning 'and') was added to connect this word to other forbidden items in the list
Forms passive participle indicating something that receives the violent beating/striking
Adding ة makes it feminine
Added definite article to specify this particular type of prohibited animal mentioned in the verse about forbidden foods
Added nominative case ending ُ to show this is part of a list of subjects of حُرِّمَتْ (forbidden things)

steps.m: and the beaten-to-death animal (feminine, subject of حُرِّمَتْ)
steps.n: Added conjunction prefix وَ meaning 'and' to connect this to the previous items in the list of forbidden foods
Added shadda to show intensive falling
Added مُتَ prefix to show something doing the action to itself (reflexive) - like falling down on its own
Added ة to make it feminine and refer to a specific instance

steps.m: the one that has fallen down (feminine)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: the one that has fallen down (feminine subject)
steps.n: Added ُ to show it's the subject

steps.m: and the one that has fallen down (feminine subject)
steps.n: Added و to connect it to the previous items in the list

steps.m: to hit or strike with horns

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: one who was hit by horns
steps.n: Changed from a verb (hit) to describe the animal that got hit by horns

steps.m: female one who was hit by horns
steps.n: Added ة to make it feminine

steps.m: the female one who was hit by horns
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ن

steps.m: the female one who was hit by horns
steps.n: Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is a complete subject word

steps.m: and the female one who was hit by horns
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect it to the previous words in the list of forbidden things
This is a relative pronoun that means 'what' or 'that which'. It's used to refer to things rather than people.
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it in the verse
Changed the final ا to آ, which is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning
Basic meaning of the root letters
Added fatha after each letter to make it past tense. This word describes what the predator (السَّبُعُ) ate in the verse.
Base noun meaning 'wild animal/predator'
Adding vowels to make the basic noun meaning 'predator'
Added the definite article ٱلْ to specify a particular wild beast
Added a shadda to emphasize the 'س' sound, making it stronger
Added the nominative case ending to show this is the subject
Added shadda to emphasize the exception. This makes the word stronger in excluding things from what came before.
Added alif at the end to complete the exception particle. In this ayah, it introduces an exception to the prohibited foods mentioned before - things that were properly slaughtered are allowed.

steps.m: that which (connects to ذَكَّيْتُمْ - you slaughtered)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it connects to the verb ذَكَّيْتُمْ (you slaughtered), showing what was slaughtered.
The doubled middle letter shows this is done thoroughly and properly according to ritual requirements. Final و changes to ى due to being a weak letter.
Adding تُ at the end shows 'I' did this action in the past
This is a combination of two particles: و (and) and ما (what/that which)

steps.m: and what/and that which (that was sacrificed on stone altars)
steps.n: The و adds the meaning of 'and'. In this ayah, this word refers to animals that were sacrificed on stone altars (ٱلنُّصُبِ) as offerings to idols.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: it was slaughtered (what was sacrificed on النُّصُبِ - stone altars)
steps.n: Changed vowels to show this happened to something rather than someone doing it. This word refers to animals sacrificed on stone altars mentioned right after it in the ayah.
This is a basic preposition showing something is on or above something else. In this ayah, it's talking about what was slaughtered upon the stone altars.

steps.m: stone altars (objects used for pagan worship)
steps.n: Root letters form a noun meaning stone altars - like turning 'set up' into 'the thing that is set up'

steps.m: the stone altars (objects of pagan worship)
steps.n: Adding definite article makes the noun definite

steps.m: upon/on the stone altars (referring to sacrifices made on these pagan altars)
steps.n: The kasra here shows this word follows عَلَى (upon/on) in the ayah, referring to sacrifices made on these altars
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Added تَسْتَ at the beginning to show 'seeking to do' something, and changed the pattern to show 'you' are doing it now (present tense)
Added وا at the end to show that multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: divining arrow (a tool used for fortune-telling)

steps.n: Changes from single divining arrow to plural by adding أ at start and ا after ل
Makes the word definite by adding ال, showing these are specific divining arrows
Added preposition ب to indicate 'with/by'
The word gets a kasra (i) ending because it comes after the word bi (with/by). It is the tool used for fortune-telling mentioned in the verb tastaqs'imu (seek fortune-telling).
Basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
Added ل with kasra to change from 'this' (near) to 'that' (far). The alif is elongated with a small alif above to show distance.
Added كُمْ at the end to show this is directed to multiple people being addressed

steps.m: to disobey/rebel against what is right

steps.n: This form turns the action of disobeying into the noun describing the act itself. In this ayah, it's describing 'that' (ذٰلِكُمْ) referring to the forbidden acts mentioned before.
Root letters put together to make the common word for 'day'
Adding ال makes it specific to 'this day', and the final fatha shows it's answering 'when?' about the completion of religion mentioned in the ayah
Past tense verb meaning 'he lost hope' - refers to the disbelievers (كَفَرُوا) in this ayah who gave up hope
Added ي to create the basic relative pronoun form
Added ين at the end to make it mean 'those who' (plural) instead of 'one who' (singular)

steps.m: those who (referring to كَفَرُوا۟ - those who disbelieved)
steps.n: Added 'the' (ال) to make it mean 'those who'. The ل doubles (gets shadda) because it blends into ذ when spoken - like saying 'allatheena' instead of 'al-theena'. Points to كَفَرُوا۟ (those who disbelieved).
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed from 'he rejected' to 'they rejected' by adding وا at the end to show multiple people did the action
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects 'your religion' (دِينِكُمْ) to 'they have despaired' (يَئِسَ), showing what they despaired from.

steps.m: to follow a path or way

steps.n: Adding the kasra (ِ) and connecting the letters turns this into a noun meaning the system or way that someone follows
Adding kasra at the end shows this religion belongs to someone
The كُمْ shows this belongs to multiple people being spoken to

steps.m: so/then (connecting to previous statement about disbelievers)
steps.n: Basic connecting particle that shows one thing follows from another
Added لا which makes the statement negative. In this ayah, it's connected to تَخْشَوْهُمْ (fear them) that comes after it, making a complete phrase meaning 'so do not fear them'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Changes from 'he feared' to 'you fear' by adding تَ at start to show you are doing it now
Adds و suffix to indicate masculine plural subject

steps.m: you all fear them (referring to the disbelievers mentioned in the previous part of the verse)
steps.n: The هُمْ shows who is being feared (them). This connects to the previous part of the verse talking about those who disbelieved.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he feared' to 'fear!' by dropping the last letter and adding alif at start to make a command
Adds 'and' to connect this command with previous phrase
Makes the command plural - addressing multiple people
Adds 'me' to the command - now means 'fear me' instead of just 'fear'
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning a specific 24-hour period
Added ال to make it specific and َ at the end to show it's describing when something happened. In this ayah, it's telling when the disbelievers despaired (يَئِسَ)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start changes the meaning from 'to complete' to 'to make something complete'. This form is used when someone causes an action to happen.

steps.m: I completed (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Adding تُ at the end shows that 'I' did the action in the past. In this ayah, Allah is speaking about completing the religion.
Added fatha to show the basic meaning of direction or purpose

steps.m: for you all (related to دِينَكُمْ - your religion)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show this is directed at multiple people. This connects to the word دِينَكُمْ (your religion) that comes right after it in the ayah.

steps.m: a complete way of life and beliefs
steps.n: The kasra (ِ) and long ي together make this into a noun meaning a complete system of beliefs and practices

steps.m: a complete way of life and beliefs
steps.n: Added fatha (َ) to connect with the next change

steps.m: your (all of you) complete way of life and beliefs
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show this belongs to all the people being spoken to
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb
Added 'أ' at start to mean 'making something/someone complete' instead of just 'completing'

steps.m: I completed (referring to completing the religion in this ayah)
steps.n: Adding تُ shows that Allah (I) is the one who completed. This refers to Allah completing the religion mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: and I completed (connected to أَكْمَلْتُ - I perfected)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this to the previous statement about perfecting the religion (أَكْمَلْتُ)
This is a preposition that comes from the concept of being high/upper. Adding ى at the end makes it mean 'upon'.
When adding a suffix, the ى changes to يْ to make pronunciation easier
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to. This word shows who receives the completion mentioned in أَكْمَلْتُ (I have perfected).

steps.m: to bestow comfort, blessing, or favor

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun meaning one specific blessing or favor
Added kasra to show this blessing is connected to something that comes after it
Added ى to show this is my blessing. This blessing is being completed/perfected by Allah when He says أَتْمَمْتُ (I have completed).
Added vowels to make it into a past tense verb showing a feeling or state. The و at the end changes to ي because it's a weak letter.
Adding تُ shows that 'I' did the action in the past
Adding وَ connects this word to what came before it
Added fatha to the letter lam to show its pronunciation

steps.m: for you all (receivers of أَكْمَلْتُ - I have perfected)
steps.n: Added كُمُ at the end to show this is directed to multiple people. This word connects to أَكْمَلْتُ (I have perfected) in the ayah, showing who received the perfection.
This pattern makes the action into a noun
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) called an alif khanjariya, which is a stylistic variation in writing but doesn't change the meaning
Adding ال makes it refer specifically to Islam as the religion of submission to Allah

steps.m: to follow a way or path
steps.n: The meaning evolved from 'debt/obligation' to 'judgment/religion'
Adding the kasra (ِ) sound on the first letter turns this from an action into a system or way

steps.m: a way (of living/believing) (object of رَضِيتُ - I am pleased with)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is receiving the action of being pleased with in the phrase 'I am pleased with Islam as a way'
Starting with the basic question word مَن (whoever). Added kasra at the end because it's followed by a word starting with a hamza (ٱضْطُرَّ)

Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. In this ayah, it connects to the previous rules about forbidden foods, meaning 'then whoever (is forced to eat these things)...'
Basic meaning of being forced or compelled due to harm/necessity
Adding vowel marks makes it a proper past tense verb meaning to harm or force
Added ٱ at start and ط between first two letters to show being affected by the action. The ط comes from the original ت changing due to the strong ض sound.
Basic preposition showing location or being inside something
The ى at the end makes the word connect better with what comes after it. Here it connects with the word مَخْمَصَةٍ (hunger) to show being in a state of hunger.
Basic meaning refers to having an empty stomach from hunger
Changes from verb 'to be hungry' to noun meaning 'state of hunger'
Added ٍ (kasratin) at the end to show this word is receiving the action, like saying 'in/during severe hunger'
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make a noun meaning 'other than'

steps.m: other than (describing what is forbidden in relation to اللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is describing what is being acted upon. Here it's describing what is forbidden - specifically things dedicated to other than Allah.
Added vowels to make a basic past-tense verb
Forms active participle on pattern فاعل indicating 'one who does the action'
مُ and ت together show someone doing the action to themselves

steps.m: one who deliberately leans away (being described in the sentence)
steps.n: Adding the two dots below (tanween) shows this describes someone or something in the sentence

steps.m: to commit a wrongdoing or harmful act

steps.n: Root meaning 'do wrong' becomes noun meaning 'sin' by adding vowel marks
Added tanween (double kasra) to show this is any sin, not a specific one
Added لِ to show movement or inclination towards something
The ل gets a shadda (double letter) because it comes after a word ending in ن or م
This is a word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English

steps.m: then indeed, for certainly (connects to previous statement)
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this is connected to and follows from what was said before, like saying 'then' or 'so' in English
Changed to the specific name for the One God by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object being acted upon)
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize the ل and fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon. The hamza at the start is written as ٱ when connecting to previous words.
Added vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past
Changed the vowels and added و to show this is describing someone who forgives a lot

steps.m: very forgiving (describing Allah as a main quality)
steps.n: Adding the two dots (tanween) on top makes it mean 'a very forgiving one' instead of 'the very forgiving one'

steps.m: to show kindness and care for others

steps.n: The ي makes it mean 'full of mercy' instead of just 'showing mercy'
The ٌ shows this is being used as a complete noun/name
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: they ask you (about what is allowed for them)
steps.n: Added ك to show you are being asked. The question is about what foods are allowed, as shown in the rest of the verse.
This is the interrogative particle that asks questions like 'what'
Added ذا (meaning 'this') to make the question more specific. This combination is commonly used to ask 'what' about something specific.

steps.m: what (asking about what is permitted) (object of يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ)
steps.n: The alif is elongated to show emphasis in the question. This word is being asked about in the phrase 'They ask you what...'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding أ at the beginning shows causing something to happen

steps.m: it was made lawful (in response to يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ - they ask you)
steps.n: Changed to passive voice with damma at start and kasra in middle, plus shadda for emphasis. This shows the action being done to something rather than someone doing it. This answers what was made lawful when they asked.
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the preposition ل
Added هُ (him) to show who the preposition is directed to
Added م with sukun to change 'him' to 'them'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

steps.m: Say! (command to one male) (response to يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ - they ask you)
steps.n: Changed to a command form. The middle و is dropped because it's a weak letter. This command is a response to 'they ask you' in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Combined the doubled letters with a shadda

steps.m: it was made lawful (in response to يَسْـَٔلُونَكَ - they ask you)
steps.n: Added أُ at the beginning and changed the vowel to ِ to make it passive voice, showing the action was done to something rather than by something
Added fatha to the letter lam to show it's a preposition meaning 'to' or 'for'
Added كُم which means 'you all' to show who the action is for. This is connected to أُحِلَّ (was made permissible) in the ayah to show what was made permissible for them.
Added 'u' sound at the end to connect smoothly with the next word
Root letters with extra ي (shadda) to make it mean 'very pure/good'
Added -aat ending to make it plural, meaning multiple good/pure things. This word receives the action of being made lawful in this ayah.
Added ٱل (the) to make it specific. The ط gets a shadda because it comes after ٱل.
Added U mark (ُ) at the end to show this word is doing the action
This is a basic particle that connects to the verb عَلَّمْتُم (you taught) to ask about what was taught
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before
Doubling the middle letter makes it mean 'to teach' instead of just 'to know'
Added sukun to prepare for adding past tense ending
Added تُ to show 'I' did the action in the past
Basic preposition meaning 'from'. The َ (fatha) at the end helps connect it to the next word.
The ّ (shadda) on م adds emphasis to the word, making the 'm' sound stronger.
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone/something that hunts
Changes from single hunter/predator to plural by adding وا in the middle - a common way to make plurals in Arabic

steps.m: the hunting animals (referring to trained hunting dogs and birds)
steps.n: Makes the word specific and adds the genitive case ending ِ because it comes after مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah

steps.m: to train (especially dogs), to hunt with trained animals

steps.n: Added مُ at the beginning to show who does the action (like saying 'trainer'), with kasra under ل to show it's a present/ongoing action
Added shadda and fatha to show this is someone who does this as a regular job or skill
Added kasra to show connection to the word مِنَ (from) in the verse
Adding masculine sound plural ending
Added fatha at end to show this word describes الجوارح (hunting animals) mentioned earlier in verse
Doubling the middle letter shows intensive teaching or training, like when training hunting animals
Adding تُ at start makes it 'you' (second person)
The ending changed from َ to ُ to show this is a regular present tense statement, not a special type of sentence
Adding ون shows multiple people (you all) are doing the teaching
The هُنَّ at the end refers to the hunting animals (feminine plural) being trained
This word combines two parts: مِن meaning 'from' and مَا meaning 'what'
When مِن comes before a word starting with م, the ن changes to match the م and gets a shadda mark. This is just for easier pronunciation.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter shows he taught a lot or taught many people

steps.m: he taught you all (object of أُحِلَّ)
steps.n: Adds second person masculine plural object pronoun suffix. This word is connected to أُحِلَّ (was made permissible) in the ayah.
Changed the word to become the proper name of God by adding ال and merging letters
Added ُ at the end to show Allah is doing the teaching. The ّ was already there (not added). The first letter changed to a special connecting alif.
The root أكل becomes a command by dropping the first letter and adding a 'u' sound (ُ) at the end
وا shows this is a command to a group of people

steps.m: so eat! (all of you) (connected to أُحِلَّ لَكُمُ - what was made permissible for you)
steps.n: فَ connects this command with the previous statement about what was made permissible, showing this is a result or consequence
Starting with the two separate words that combine to form this word: مِن (from) and مَا (what)
The ن of مِن combines with the م of ما, shown by the shadda (ّ) on the م. This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: from what (of what عَلَّمَكُمُ taught you)
steps.n: The final alif is elongated (آ) to help with pronunciation when continuing to the next word. This word connects to عَلَّمَكُمُ (taught you) in the ayah.

steps.m: to grasp or hold onto something

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters together
The أَ at the beginning makes the action more direct and forceful - changing from just holding to actively catching or seizing

steps.m: they (feminine) caught (referring to hunting animals in this ayah)
steps.n: The نَ at the end shows this was done by multiple feminine subjects (in this case, referring to trained hunting animals mentioned earlier in the verse)
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of or affecting something else
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for connecting with the next part
Added كُمْ which means 'you all' to show who is being affected
Changed the root into a command by adding marks (sukun and damma) - like saying 'Remember!' to one person
Added hamzat al-wasl (ٱ) which helps pronunciation when starting the word
Added وا to show this command is for multiple people

steps.m: and remember! (command to all of you)
steps.n: Added و to connect this command with the previous one (فَكُلُوا۟) in the ayah
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters

The root سَمَوَ (meaning height/elevation) became the noun 'name' (ٱسْمَ). This is a common word that doesn't follow normal patterns. It's the object being mentioned in the command 'remember/mention' (وَٱذْكُرُوا۟)
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.
Added kasra at the end to show possession, connecting it to the word ٱسْمَ (name) in the phrase 'name of Allah'
This is a preposition showing something is on top of or upon something else
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for connecting to the next part
Added هِ which means 'him' or 'it' to show what is being acted upon

steps.m: to guard against harm or evil
to protect oneself from harm (especially by being mindful of Allah's commands)
steps.n: The root و-ق-ي becomes ٱتَّقَى by adding ٱ at the start and changing و to ت with a shadda. This makes it mean 'to protect yourself' instead of just 'to protect'

steps.m: all of you protect yourselves! (by following Allah's commands)
steps.n: Changes from 'to protect' to 'all of you protect!' - makes it a command for many people

steps.m: and all of you protect yourselves! (connected to وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ - and remember)
steps.n: Connects this command with the previous command to remember Allah's name
Changed to the proper name for God by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object being feared in وَٱتَّقُوا۟)
steps.n: Allah gets a fatha mark at the end because it's the object being feared in وَٱتَّقُوا۟ (fear Allah)
Added kasra (ِ) under the alif to show correct pronunciation
Added shadda (ّ) to strengthen the word, making it emphasize what follows. In this ayah, it emphasizes that Allah is swift in reckoning (سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ)
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding shadda and replacing initial hamza with lam

steps.m: Allah (as the object of وَٱتَّقُوا۟ - fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being acted upon. Also changed alif to wasl alif since it comes after another word.

steps.m: quick, fast (describing Allah's quality of swiftness in this ayah)
steps.n: Root meaning 'quick' becomes an adjective meaning 'fast/quick' - like changing 'run' to 'runner'

steps.m: quick, fast (describing Allah as the subject of the sentence)
steps.n: Adds the ُ ending to show this word is the subject in the sentence
Changed the word from an action (counting) into the result of that action (calculation), by adding ِ on the first letter and ا after the second letter
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to mean 'the', and ِ at the end because this word is being described by سَرِيعُ (swift)

steps.m: to spend/pass time, to have a day

steps.n: Connected the letters with vowel marks to create the basic noun meaning 'a day'. The middle letter و stays as a consonant here.

steps.m: a day (object of time when أُحِلَّ occurred)
steps.n: The َ (fatha) at the end shows this word answers 'when?' about the permitting (أُحِلَّ) that happened
Adding ال makes it refer to 'this specific day' being discussed. Still shows when the permitting (أُحِلَّ) happened.
Added vowel marks to make it 'he made lawful' in the past tense
Changed to passive form by adding أُ at start and doubling the middle letter with shaddah, showing something was made lawful rather than someone making it lawful
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added كُ to show this is directed at multiple people

steps.m: for you all (connected to أُحِلَّ - was made lawful)
steps.n: Added م with damma to complete the word. This word connects to أُحِلَّ (was made lawful) showing who something was made lawful for.
Root letters made into a noun by adding vowels and doubling middle letter (shadda), changing from 'being pure' to 'something pure'
Added feminine plural ending to show multiple pure things
Added definite article to specify these particular pure things
Added damma to show this word is the subject of the verb أُحِلَّ (was made permissible)
This pattern creates a noun meaning the thing itself (food) rather than the action of feeding
Added وَ (wa) at the beginning to mean 'and', connecting this with the previous items being discussed as permissible foods
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this word is the subject doing the action of being permissible (حِلٌّ) for them
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that describes or specifies something
Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'

steps.m: those who (referring to people who were given the Book)
steps.n: Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people. In this ayah, it refers to the People of the Book who were given scripture before.
Changed to passive voice by changing vowel pattern to show receiving action
Added plural marker وا to show multiple people received the action. This word describes those who were given the scripture, as mentioned in the phrase أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتٰبَ (they were given the Book).
Changes from verb 'to write' into noun 'book'
Adds 'the' to show we're talking about a specific book
The َ at the end shows this book is receiving the action in the sentence (it was given)
The kasra changes this from a verb into a noun meaning 'something permissible'
Added double damma (ٌ) to show this is a noun that is the subject of the sentence
This is the basic particle that shows direction or purpose
The shadda adds emphasis to the meaning

steps.m: surely for you all (object of أُحِلَّ - was made lawful)
steps.n: Added the plural 'you' pronoun كُمْ to show who the action is for. This word connects to أُحِلَّ (was made lawful) in the ayah, showing what was made lawful for them.
Changes the verb 'to feed' into the noun 'food'. This word طَعَام is the subject of حِلٌّ (permissible) in this ayah, showing what is made permissible.
Adds 'your' (plural)
Adds 'and' at the beginning
The kasra vowel changes this from a verb into a noun meaning 'state of being permissible'
The tanween marks this as an indefinite noun - 'a permissible thing' rather than 'the permissible thing'
This is the basic letter that shows something is for someone or goes to them
The shadda (ّ) and fatha (َ) make the meaning stronger, like saying 'definitely for'
Added هُمْ which means 'them' when talking about a group of men or a mixed group
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start to show someone receiving protection, like changing 'protect' to 'protected'
Added ات to make it feminine plural, referring to multiple women
Added ال to make it specific/definite
Added 'and' (و) at the start, and changed the ending to show this is the subject of the sentence
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what group something belongs to. Here it connects the word before it to ٱلَّذِينَ (those who)
The extra vowel at the end helps connect this word smoothly to the next word
Added alif at beginning to show causing or entering into a state of trust/belief
Changed to active participle (doer) form by adding مُ prefix and changing vowels
Added ات to make it feminine plural
Added ال for 'the' and kasra at end because it follows مِنَ (from/of)
Added مُ at start to show someone/something receiving the action, made into a passive participle meaning 'protected'
Added ات to make it feminine plural, referring specifically to women who are protected through marriage or chastity
Added ال with proper vowel marks to make it definite (specific protected women)

This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts from or comes from
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly to the next word ٱلْمُؤْمِنٰتِ (the believing women)
Starting with the basic relative pronoun and adding kasra and ي to make it singular

steps.m: those who (referring to the People of the Book أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتٰبَ)

Changed to passive voice through vowel pattern change
The letter ي changes to و due to being passive voice

steps.m: they were given (refers to those who were given the scripture)
steps.n: Added plural marker وا to show multiple people were given something

The pattern فِعَال is commonly used to create nouns from verbs, especially for things that result from the action. In this ayah, كِتاب is in the accusative case because it is the object (thing being given) in the phrase 'those who were given the Book'.

steps.m: the book (being acted upon in أُوتُوا)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it specific ('the book') and َ at the end shows it's receiving the action of being given in the phrase 'those who were given the Book'
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or which group something belongs to. In this ayah, it's used to specify which group of women is being talked about.
Added kasra to show this word is being possessed by what comes after it
Added كُمْ to show this means 'before you all'
This is a conditional word that connects two actions. Here it connects to the word ءَاتَيْتُمُوهُنَّ (you gave them), showing when something happens.
The long alif (آ) is used instead of regular alif (ا) at the end of words to show it's a complete pause point in reading.
The root letters are modified to create the basic past tense form
Added hamza with fatha at start and alif to make Form IV verb, which means 'to give' rather than just 'to come'
Changed final ى to ي with sukun to prepare for attachments
Added past tense plural masculine 'you' ending
Added و to mark masculine plural subject
Added هُنَّ to show the feminine plural recipients of the action
This root relates to payment or compensation for work or service
Changes the word into a plural form meaning multiple payments or rewards
Adds fatha to show this is the object receiving the action of giving (ءَاتَيْتُمُو)
Adds هُنَّ to show these wages belong to multiple females

steps.m: to protect, guard, or make something pure/chaste
steps.n: This is the basic meaning showing the concept of protection and purity
Added مُ at start and kasra on middle letter to show who does the action, specifically referring to someone who guards marriage's purity

steps.n: Added ين to show this refers to multiple men. In this ayah, it refers to men who seek marriage properly, protecting its sanctity, as opposed to those seeking illicit relationships.
Forms a noun meaning 'other than' from the root meaning 'to change'
Added vowel marks to create a noun meaning 'other than' from the basic idea of being different
Added fatha at the end because it describes the word before it - showing how to be chaste (not doing bad things)
The root is transformed into an active participle meaning 'one who commits fornication'
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Added alif after first letter to show action between two people
Added مُ at start and changed middle vowel to kasra to show who does the action
Added ين at the end to make it plural, and changed alif to alif with bar (ٰ) for writing style
Added fatha to show proper pronunciation
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
This is the basic concept of taking or adopting something

Added kasra at the end to show it's being used after a preposition or as part of a possessive phrase
Added ي at the end to show possession - means 'my'
Added the long vowel mark (ٓ) to show proper pronunciation
Basic noun form showing one who is a secret companion

steps.m: secret companions (being described by another word)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show this word is being described by another word in the sentence
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people. It introduces a conditional clause in this ayah, meaning 'whoever' or 'anyone who'.
The و connects this clause to what came before. This word starts a new condition - 'and whoever rejects faith'.
This is the basic concept of rejecting or denying something, especially faith
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.n: Changed to present tense with sukun at end to show this is part of a conditional statement (whoever rejects...)

steps.m: to trust, feel secure, or have faith

Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Adding tanween damma makes it an indefinite noun meaning 'a deed'

steps.m: his deed (subject of حَبِطَ/has become worthless)
steps.n: Added pronoun suffix to show possession. This word is the subject of حَبِطَ (has become worthless) in this ayah.

steps.m: and he (referring to whoever rejects faith)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it, showing that this is an additional statement about the person who rejects faith.
This is a basic preposition that shows location. In this ayah, it's showing that someone will be located in the afterlife.

steps.m: to come later, to be last
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Changes from basic root to mean 'someone/something that is last', like changing 'late' to 'later'
Added feminine ending -ah to make it mean 'the afterlife' instead of just 'last'

This is a basic preposition showing where something comes from or what group it belongs to
Added َ (fatha) at the end to help connect this word smoothly to the next word ٱلْمُؤْمِنٰتِ (the believing women). This shows we're talking about women who come from the group of believers.

steps.m: to lose or be at a loss
steps.n: This is the basic past tense form
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does the action
Makes the word plural to show multiple losers
Makes the noun definite
Adds final fatha because it comes after the word مِنَ (from/of) in the ayah

steps.m: to point to or direct attention

steps.n: Making the word nominative case

steps.m: O you! (calling out to a group)

Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: they believed (refers to ٱلَّذِينَ - those who)

The weak letter و is dropped and replaced with sukun due to being between two vowels

Changed from a verb (praying) to a noun meaning the act of prayer itself, with the ة ending making it a noun
Added ال (the) at start and doubled the ص with shadda because it comes after ال

This is the basic root meaning

Adding vowel marks creates basic past tense verb meaning 'he restricted'
Added tanwin kasra at the end because it follows the word مِنْ (from/of). The tanwin also shows this is referring to any general difficulty, not a specific one.

steps.n: Adding alif makes it about two people agreeing with each other

Basic preposition that shows how something is done or what is used

steps.m: with it/by it (referring to ميثٰق - covenant)
steps.n: Added pronoun ه to show what the preposition connects to. Here it refers back to the covenant (ميثٰق) mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: with it/by it (referring to ميثٰق - covenant)
steps.n: Added small alif (ۦ) and elongation mark (ٓ) to help with pronunciation. These don't change the meaning.
This word connects to what comes after it, showing when something happened in the past. Here it introduces when 'you said' (قُلْتُمْ) something.
Added damma and sukun to make basic command form. The middle weak letter و drops out in this pattern.
Added تُ at the end to change from a command ("Say!") to past tense ("I said")
Added مْ at the end to change from 'I said' to 'you all said'

steps.m: to hear or listen to something

steps.n: Adding vowels turns the root letters into a complete past tense verb meaning 'he heard'
Changed the final vowel to sukun to prepare for adding 'we'

steps.m: we heard (what was said after قُلْتُمْ)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word comes after قُلْتُمْ (you said) to show what was said.
Added vowels to connect the letters. The middle و changes to ا because it's between two vowels.
Added أ at the start and made the word shorter by putting a sukun on ع - this makes it ready to connect with 'we' or 'you' later
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word pairs with سَمِعْنَا (we heard) in the ayah.
Added و at the start to connect this word with سَمِعْنَا (we heard), meaning 'we heard and we obeyed'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Added اِتَّ at the beginning to show doing the action of protecting to yourself (like saying 'self-protect')
Changed to a command by adding وا at the end, telling multiple people to protect themselves
Added و at beginning to mean 'and', and changed spelling of alif to match Uthmani script
Changed from 'a god' to 'The God' by adding ال and merging letters. The word Allah is unique and refers only to the One True God.
Added the fatha at the end to show Allah is being mentioned or described. Also added shadda to emphasize the L sound and changed alif to wasl-alif for smoother pronunciation.
Added kasra (ِ) under the alif to show correct pronunciation
Added shadda (ّ) to strengthen the meaning. This makes the word emphasize what comes after it. In this ayah, it emphasizes that Allah is All-Knowing.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for the one true God. The hamza is dropped and the first lam gets a shadda.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of اتَّقُوا - fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being acted upon by the verb 'fear/be mindful of' (اتَّقُوا)
Adding vowel marks to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to know'
Changed from past tense 'knew' to a special form showing Allah's perfect and complete knowledge
Added tanween to show this is indefinite, emphasizing the vastness of Allah's knowledge
Added feminine ending ات to make the word feminine
Added kasra at the end to show this word is affected by the preposition before it

steps.m: with/of that which (is in الصُّدُورِ - the chests/hearts)
steps.n: Added the preposition ب at the beginning to show 'with' or 'of'. This word connects to الصُّدُورِ (the chests/hearts) in the ayah.
Added vowels to the root letters to make the word mean 'a chest' (where feelings come from)
Changes short vowels from 'a' to 'u' and adds 'u' sound in middle to make it plural (like box → boxes)
Added ٱل (the) and doubled the first letter with shadda
Added kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by ذَاتِ (contents/nature) in the phrase 'contents of the hearts'

steps.m: to point to or indicate something

steps.n: Root letters combined with fatha and shadda to make a word that asks 'which one?'
Added the calling particle يا with elongated alif to show we're addressing someone
Added a small u-vowel mark (damma) to show we are calling out to someone directly
Added ها to show we are addressing a group of people
This is the basic form of the word that means 'who'. We start with this because it's a special word without a root.
Adding ين at the end makes it refer to many people instead of just one
Adding ٱلّ at the beginning makes it mean 'those who' instead of just 'who'. The َ at the end shows this word connects to the next word ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed).

steps.m: to cause to feel secure, to make safe
steps.n: Adding long alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Adding وا shows multiple people did the action. This word connects to كُونُوا (be!) which comes right after it in the ayah.
The small circle (۟) is just a pronunciation helper that shows this is a plural word
Added damma and sukun to make basic command form
Added damma at end to show nominative case
Added وا to make it a command to multiple people
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
The doubled middle letter (shadda) and alif show this person does this action thoroughly and consistently

steps.m: those who consistently maintain/establish (plural, referring to believers in كُونُوا۟)
steps.n: Makes the word plural to match with كُونُوا۟ (be!) which is addressing multiple believers
This is the base word 'Allah', the proper name of God in Islam. The shadda shows emphasis on the ل.
Added لِ at the beginning to show something belongs to or is for Allah, and ِ at the end to connect it with other words in the sentence.

steps.m: to witness or testify to something

steps.n: Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed from past tense verb 'witnessed' to noun 'witness' by adding ي in the middle
Makes the word plural by changing vowels and adding اء at the end
Added final vowel mark to show this is the object of the command 'be!' (كُونُوا) in the verse
Root meaning 'being fair' becomes a noun meaning the idea of fairness/justice
Adds 'al' (ال) at the start to mean 'the' justice instead of just justice
Added بِ at the beginning to show how something is done, and added ِ at the end because it follows the word بِ. This word describes how to be قَوَّامِينَ (upholders/maintainers) - with justice.
This is the basic negative particle that means 'not' or 'no'
Added و at the beginning to connect this negative phrase with what came before. This word negates يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ (let [something] cause you) in this ayah.

steps.m: to cause/drive someone to do something

steps.n: Added vowels to make it 'he caused' in past tense
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: he definitely causes/drives (someone to do something)
steps.n: Added emphasis by adding نَّ at the end
Added كُمْ to show who is being affected (you all). In this context, with لا before it, it becomes a prohibition.

steps.m: to hate intensely or have strong dislike

Changed the verb into a noun showing the intense feeling or result of hatred. This word is what should not drive you (يَجْرِمَنَّكُمْ) to injustice.
Added the final damma to show this word is doing the action in the sentence
Changes from verb root meaning 'to stand' into noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Added the -in sound at the end (tanween kasra) to show this word is connected to 'hatred' in the ayah
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows something being physically or metaphorically on top of something else
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end to show this is the standard written form
Added a small alif (ٓ) above to show this word connects to the next word أَلَّا. Together they mean 'against that you' or 'from that you'
Starting with the first part أَنْ which shows something is about to happen
Adding لَا which means 'not' to negate what comes after

steps.m: that not (before تَعْدِلُوا۟ - you be just)

Added vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added ت at beginning to make it 'you' and changed vowels to make it present tense
Added وا at the end to show plural 'you all'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to a command form telling multiple people to act fairly
Adds a silent mark to show where the word ends

steps.m: he/it (refers to being just/fair from previous word اعْدِلُوا)
steps.n: This is a pronoun meaning 'he' or 'it' (masculine). In this ayah, it refers back to being just/fair (from the command اعْدِلُوا meaning 'be just'), explaining that justice is closer to righteousness.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
This form shows comparison between things. In this ayah, it's saying that being just is closer to being God-conscious (التَّقْوَىٰ).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed to a noun form showing the quality or state of protecting oneself (from wrong). The و changes to ى at the end.
Added ال to make it definite and doubled the ت with shadda
Added لِ to show purpose or direction, and added alif maksura at the end
Added reflexive pattern to show doing action to oneself
Changed from regular verb to command - telling one person to protect themselves
Changed from command to one person to command to many people by adding وا at the end
Added connecting word at beginning
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.
Added shadda to show emphasis on ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added fatha at end to show Allah is the object being discussed in this sentence
This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger. Here it emphasizes that Allah is All-Aware. The shadda (ّ) makes the 'n' sound stronger to show this emphasis.
Added ال to make it specific and changed the first letter, making it a unique name for The One True God

steps.m: Allah (as the object being feared/revered in وَٱتَّقُوا۟)
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize the ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added fatha to show it's the object of the verb 'fear/revere'
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who is deeply aware/informed (of بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ - what you do)
steps.n: The ي between second and third root letters with kasra before it creates an intensive active participle, showing someone who deeply possesses this quality. In this ayah, it describes Allah's complete awareness of people's actions.

steps.m: All-Aware, Most Informed (of بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ - what you do)
steps.n: Adding the double dots (tanween) at the end shows this is describing Allah as the One who knows everything
Basic preposition showing how or by what means something happens
Added مَا which means 'what' to ask about or refer to something. Here it connects to the verb تَعْمَلُونَ (you do) to mean 'with what you do'
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Changed to present tense with ت prefix showing 'you' as the doer
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: to promise or commit to something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this is a past-tense verb. In this ayah, Allah is the one making the promise.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel patterns

steps.m: Allah (the one true God) (subject of وَعَدَ - promised)
steps.n: Added ال (the) and emphasized the middle letter to show this is the one true God. The ُ at the end shows this is the subject doing the promising in the verse.
This is the base form of the word that points to someone or something. It's a special word that doesn't come from a three-letter root.
Added ال at the beginning and doubled the ذ letter to make it specific. Like changing 'who' to 'the one who'.

steps.m: those who (referring to the believers in ءَامَنُوا۟)
steps.n: Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many people. This word connects to ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) later in the verse.
Adding alif after the first letter changes the meaning from just trusting to having deep religious faith
Adding 'وا' shows that many people (they) did the action. This word is the subject of وَعَدَ (Allah promised) in this ayah.

steps.m: to work, to act, to do deeds

steps.n: Added vowels to make past tense verb. This word is connected to ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) describing what the believers did.
Added وا to show multiple people (they) did the action, matching the plural in ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed)
Added و with fatha to connect this action with the previous phrase about believing, showing these people both believed AND did good deeds
Changes from verb (to be good) to noun (one who is good)
Adding 'ات' makes it plural feminine, here referring to good actions or deeds. This word is the object (what is being done) in relation to the verb عَمِلُوا (they did/performed).
Adding the definite article ال makes the word definite
The word gets a kasra (i) sound at the end because it's receiving the action 'they did'. Also, the alif is written slightly differently but means the same thing.
Added fatha to show this is connected to what follows
Added هُم at the end to show this is for a group of people. This word connects to مَّغْفِرَةٌ (forgiveness) in the ayah to show they will have forgiveness.
Added مَ at start and ة at end to make it mean the act itself, like how 'write' becomes 'writing'
The doubled م shows completeness or emphasis of the forgiveness

steps.m: to give a reward or payment for work done

Added the nominative case ending to show this is a complete noun in the sentence
Added ي between ظ and م to show this is a lasting quality. This word describes the reward (أَجْرٌ) mentioned in the ayah.
Added tanween dammah to show this is indefinite, meaning 'a great reward' rather than 'the great reward'
Added ين to show we're talking about multiple people
Added ال to make it specific, like saying 'the ones who' instead of just 'ones who'
Added و at the beginning to connect this with what came before. This word is connected to كَفَرُوا۟ (they disbelieved) in the ayah.

Adding the shadda (ّ) on ذ makes the denial stronger and more emphatic, like saying 'completely reject' instead of just 'reject'

Added vowels and shadda to form the question word

Adds ها to make the word address a group of people
This is a special word used to point to someone or something. Adding ي makes it specifically about connecting one thing to another, like saying 'the one who...'
Adding ين makes it refer to many people instead of just one person
Adding ال makes it point to specific people we're talking about, not just any people

steps.m: those who (describing the believers who are addressed)

Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This word describes 'those who' (الَّذِينَ) from earlier in the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Added alif at start to help pronunciation, changed vowels to make it a command to one person
Added وا to make it a command to many people

Changed to the unique name for the One True God by adding ال and merging letters with shadda
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection, and changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes in middle of speech
This is the basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'
The ى changes to يْ when adding a suffix

Adds the final vowel mark to complete the plural form

steps.m: Allah (as the object being acted upon)

Added ل at the beginning to add emphasis, like saying 'definitely' or 'surely'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what comes before it

This is the unique name of the One True God in Islam. The ال at the beginning and the emphasis (shadda) on the ل show this is THE one and only deity. The ُ at the end shows Allah is the subject of قَالَ (said) in this verse.

Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Added مِ at start and changed و to ي with alif after second letter to make a noun meaning 'contract' or 'promise'
