Final voweling for grammatical case, alif changed to elongated form
Adding vowel marks to make a basic past tense verb
These are two different words - 'banaa' means to build, while 'bani' means sons/children. They just look similar but aren't related.

This is a proper name that comes from Hebrew. It was given to Prophet Jacob (يَعْقُوب) after he struggled with an angel, and means 'one who struggles with God'. His descendants became known as the Children of Israel (بَنِي إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ).

steps.m: to send forth or raise up

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added 'wa' (and) at start and 'na' at end to change from 'he sent' to 'and we sent'
Basic preposition meaning 'from' or 'of'
Added the pronoun هُمُ meaning 'them' to show who is being referred to. This refers back to the Children of Israel (بَنِىٓ إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ) mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: to double or make into two parts

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added ٱ at start and sukun marks to make it an imperative verb
Adding vowel marks to make the basic number word

steps.m: to examine closely, to investigate, to lead

steps.n: The pattern creates a noun for someone who performs the action professionally or as a key characteristic. A نَقِيب investigates and oversees affairs of people under their leadership.

steps.m: a leader (one of the twelve leaders of بَنِىٓ إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is describing what was appointed - twelve leaders were chosen. This connects to ٱثْنَىْ عَشَرَ (twelve) in the ayah.
This is the basic concept of speaking or saying something
Added short vowels (fatha) to make the past tense verb 'he said'
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change in Arabic words with و in the middle.
Added و at the beginning to connect this word with what came before it. This word is followed by اللَّهُ (Allah) who is doing the saying.
Changed the first letter's vowel and connected the letters to make the word for 'deity'

This is a unique word transformation where إِلَه becomes ٱللَّه, adding ٱل (the) and changing the word to become the proper name of The One True God. The ُ at the end shows this word is doing the action in the sentence - here Allah is the one who took the covenant.

Added ي at the end to show that Allah is speaking in first person. This connects to the next part where Allah tells the Children of Israel what He will do for them.
This is a preposition that shows accompaniment

steps.m: with you all (referring to the Children of Israel)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being addressed. In this ayah, it refers to the Children of Israel who are being addressed.

steps.m: if (introduces the condition for what follows)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces the first part of an if-then statement. Here it introduces the conditions that will lead to Allah's promise of forgiveness and paradise.
Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis to this important divine promise. This emphasizes that Allah will definitely fulfill His promise if the conditions are met.

Adding أ at the start makes the verb causative - instead of just standing, it means to make something stand/establish. This verb takes الصلوة (prayer) as its direct object in the accusative case, showing what is being established.
The middle weak letter و drops out when making a command form, which serves as the base for past tense
Adding fathas creates the basic past tense verb pattern
تُم at the end shows that multiple people (you all) did this action in the past

steps.m: to perform ritual prayer, to connect with God

steps.n: Basic verb form - added vowels (fatha) to make 'he prayed' in past tense

steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start, changing middle vowel to kasra, and adding ُ at end
Added fatha to show the basic meaning of direction or purpose
Added كُمْ to show this is directed at multiple people. This word is receiving the action of يُبَيِّنُ (he makes clear) in this ayah.

steps.m: much, many (describing something that has increased)
steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters with vowels to show something has this quality strongly

steps.m: much (of what you used to hide from the Book)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word is used as an adverb - tells us HOW MUCH was hidden
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added ما which makes the meaning more general - 'what/whatever'
The ن merges into the م, creating a doubled م (shown by the shadda mark). This is just a pronunciation change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The middle weak letter و drops out, and the vowels change to match the common pattern for this type of verb
Added تُمْ at the end to show that multiple people (you all) did this action in the past. This word connects to تُخْفُونَ (you hide) in the ayah, showing who was doing the hiding.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding تُ changes it from 'he hid' to 'you hide'
Added plural marker ون to show multiple people doing the action
This is the basic form of the preposition meaning 'from'
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word (ٱللَّهِ). This combination means 'from Allah'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb

This pattern is often used for things that result from an action - a book is the result of writing. The word كتاب is in the genitive case because it comes after أهل in a possession relationship, where أهل (people) possess or own الكتاب (the book/scripture).
The ال (al) makes it 'the book'. The ِ (kasra) at the end shows it's connected to another word. The long 'a' sound is written with ٰ instead of ا.
Added fathas to make past tense. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. The و returns at the end in present tense.
Added و with fatha to connect this word to what came before it
This is a basic preposition that shows moving away from something or passing over it. Here it connects to the verb يَعْفُوا۟ (he pardons/passes over) to show what is being pardoned or passed over.
Forms an adjective meaning 'much/many' by adding ي between the last two root letters

steps.m: much/many (describes what is being hidden from the Book)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this word is describing something indefinite

steps.m: certainly/indeed (emphasizes جَآءَكُمْ - has come to you)
steps.n: This is a particle that adds emphasis to the past tense verb that follows it (جَآءَكُمْ). It shows that something has definitely happened or is completed.
Changed root letters to past tense by adding vowel marks and turning middle letter ي into ا
Added extra alif to emphasize the length/duration of coming
Added كُم at the end to show the action was directed at multiple people
This is the basic form of the preposition meaning 'from'
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the next word (ٱلْكِتٰبِ - the book)
Added shadda on م for emphasis and smooth pronunciation when connected to previous word
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ل, making this the unique name for The One God
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word
Changed the verb into a noun by adding damma on first letter and making the middle letter long. This pattern is common for turning actions into things.
Added double-damma (tanween) at the end to show this is any light, not a specific one. In this ayah, it refers to a light that has come from Allah.
Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'something written'
Adding double dammah makes it 'a book' instead of 'the book'
Adding وَ connects this word to what came before it
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to show strong action
Added مُ at start to make it mean 'one who does' instead of 'he did', with some vowel changes
Added emphasis by doubling the م sound (shown by ّ ), making it mean 'very clear' instead of just 'clear'
Added tanween to show this describes the 'book' (كِتٰبٌ) mentioned in the ayah
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. This shows Allah is doing the guiding right now.
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'with' or 'by'
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'with him/it'. This word is the object that Allah guides with in يَهْدِى (guides).
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'

steps.m: Allah (the One True God who guides those who seek His pleasure)
steps.n: Changed from general word for deity to the specific name of the One True God. The ال makes it definite (like 'the') and the shadda emphasizes the L sound. This word is the subject (doer) of يَهْدِى (guides) in this ayah.

This is a special word (called a relative pronoun) that refers to people in general - like saying 'whoever' or 'anyone who'. In this ayah, it connects to the verb اتَّبَعَ (followed) to mean 'whoever follows'
Added kasra (ِ) at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it. It's like adding a tiny bridge to the next word.
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show past tense

steps.m: he followed for himself (subject of يَهْدِى)
steps.n: Added pattern VIII (اِفْتَعَلَ) to show the person is doing the action for themselves. This word is what Allah guides in this ayah.
The two ت letters combine with a shadda, showing emphasis in the following action

steps.m: to be pleased with or approve of something

steps.n: Root letters combined with vowels to make noun meaning 'pleasure'
Added ان at the end to make the word mean 'great' or 'complete' pleasure/approval
Changed alif to alif maqsurah for writing style, no meaning change
Added هُ meaning 'His' and َ to show this is the object being acted upon. This word is what Allah guides people to in the phrase 'Allah guides with it'.
Changed the vowels to make it mean multiple paths instead of just one path
Added fatha at the end to show this word is receiving the action of 'guides to' in the phrase 'guides to paths'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added alif after second root letter to make it a noun meaning the state of peace
Added ال at beginning to make it specific and doubled the س with shadda
Added kasra at the end because this word is possessed by سُبُلَ (paths), making it 'paths of peace'

steps.m: to exit or make something/someone exit

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added hamza at beginning to make the verb transitive (affecting something else)
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added هُم at the end to show who is being made to exit (them)
Added وَ at beginning to connect this action with what came before
Basic preposition meaning 'from'. Shows something coming or moving away from somewhere.
The م gets doubled (gets a shadda) because it comes right before the word ٱلظُّلُمٰتِ (the darknesses) - this is a common rule when these words are next to each other
Added damma and sukun to make the basic noun form
Added the feminine plural ending ات to show multiple instances of darkness
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ظ with shadda because it comes after ال
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word مِنَ (from) in the ayah. Also changed alif to alif madd for proper writing.
This is a preposition that shows direction or movement towards something. In this ayah, it connects to ٱلنُّورِ (the light) to show movement from darkness towards light.
Changes from the root meaning 'to shine' into the noun 'light' by adding damma (u sound) on the first letter
Doubles the middle letter to show the light is very strong or intense
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific light (the light)
The kasra shows this word follows إِلَى (to/towards) in the ayah
Changed the verb into a noun meaning the concept of permission
Added بِ at the beginning to show this describes how something happens. This connects to Allah's guidance mentioned earlier in the verse.
Added ِ to show this word is connected to what comes after it (Allah)
Added هِۦ at the end to show this is Allah's permission, referring back to Allah mentioned earlier
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The final ي changes to ى because it's a weak letter at the end.
Changes from past tense 'guided' to present tense 'guides' by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel
Added و to connect this word with يُخْرِجُهُم (brings them out) in the previous part of the verse
Added هم meaning 'them' to show who is being guided. This refers back to مَنِ ٱتَّبَعَ (those who follow) from earlier in the verse.
Basic form of the word that shows direction or movement towards something
Added the small alif (ٰ) which extends the sound but doesn't change the meaning. This word points to the path (صِرٰطٍ) that follows it in the ayah.
The pattern فِعَال is used for nouns meaning paths or ways
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) because it comes after إلى. The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this style.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who seeks to be straight
steps.n: Added مُسْتَ at the start to mean 'one who seeks/tries to be' straight - like adding 'seeker of' before a word
Added ي in the middle to make it a proper noun meaning 'one who is straight' rather than 'one who seeks to be straight'
Added emphasis (shadda) on the م
Added tanwin kasra to show this describes the path (صِرٰطٍ) mentioned in the ayah
This is a basic emphasis word that shows something is definite or certain
Added لَ at the beginning to make the certainty even stronger
Added a shadda on the ل to make the emphasis even stronger, showing this is absolutely certain

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth/faith

steps.n: Added fatha (َ) after each letter to show this happened in the past. This word is connected to قَالُوٓا۟ (they said) in the ayah, showing who did the saying.
This is a special demonstrative pronoun used to refer to a group of people. It's considered a relative pronoun in Arabic grammar.
Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it mean 'he said' in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas
Added وا to show many people (they) did the action
Added pronunciation marks (ٓ and ۟) that don't change the meaning
This is a special word that makes what comes after it more emphasized and changes its ending. Here it emphasizes that what follows about Allah is definitely true.
Changed the root letters into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'

steps.m: Allah (the one true deity being acted upon)
steps.n: Added ال to specify THE one true deity, doubled the ل with shadda, and added fatha at the end to show Allah is being acted upon in this sentence. The hamza from إِلَه is dropped when ال is added.

steps.m: He (referring to Allah in إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ)
steps.n: This is a personal pronoun that refers back to Allah mentioned earlier in the ayah. It's used to emphasize that it is specifically Allah being talked about.
Added vowels and ي to show this describes someone who has been anointed (like with oil in a special ceremony)
Adding ال makes this a specific title referring to Jesus, not just any anointed person
Added ُ to show this is the subject of هُوَ (he is) in this sentence

steps.m: to build or establish a family line

steps.n: Changed root letters into past tense verb by adding short vowels (fatha) between them
These words are not directly related - 'ibn' (son) comes from a different root than 'bana' (to build). They just look similar.
Changed to a connecting alif and added a mark to show this is the one doing the action
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Aramaic. In this ayah, it identifies Jesus's mother specifically.
Added damma and sukun to make it a command form
The و (weak letter) is dropped because it comes between a damma and sukun
This is a combination of two particles: فَ (then/so) and مَن (who/whoever)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form

steps.m: he possesses/controls (in response to فَمَن - so who?)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, changing vowels, and adding ُ at end. This word answers the question 'who?' (فَمَن) in the ayah.
Basic preposition showing source, origin, or separation from something
Added connecting alif to link smoothly with the next word اللَّهِ (Allah). This is a helper letter for pronunciation.
Changed to the proper name for God by adding ال and merging letters. The first letter changes to ا and the ل gets a shadda to show the merger.
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being acted upon by مِنَ (from) in this ayah. The alif at the start is written as a wasl alif (ٱ) because it connects with the previous word.
Basic noun pattern showing a single thing
Added tanween alif to show this is the object being possessed in the phrase 'who possesses anything'
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition - in this ayah, it connects 'if He willed' with what would happen as a result. It shows that one action depends on another.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas.
Adding أَ at the start changes it from 'want' to 'cause to want'

steps.m: that (introduces what إِنْ أَرَادَ wants to do)
steps.n: This is a particle that introduces what someone wants/intends to do. Here it connects 'if He willed' (إِنْ أَرَادَ) with what follows (to destroy)

steps.m: to destroy or cause to perish

steps.n: Basic past tense verb form with fatha vowels
Added alif at beginning to show causing something to happen
Changed to present tense subjunctive after أَن (that). The يُ at start shows it's happening now/future, and the َ at end shows it depends on أَن.
Added ي between س and ح and vowels to show someone who has been anointed

steps.m: the Messiah (object being acted upon in يُهْلِكَ - destroy)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and َ to show it's receiving the action of destruction

steps.m: to build or construct (especially family relationships)

steps.n: The root meaning 'to build' becomes 'son' by adding alif at the start - like building a family tree
Changed to 'son of' form by adding ٱ at start and -a at end because it connects to Mary's name after it
This is a proper noun (name) borrowed from Hebrew/Aramaic. In this ayah, it refers to Mary the mother of Jesus as part of the phrase 'son of Mary'.
Added damma and shadda to create the noun 'mother' from the root meaning of source/origin
Added 'his' (هُۥ) at the end to show it's his mother. The fatha (َ ) helps connect it smoothly. This mother is being destroyed in the sentence.
Adding conjunction prefix وَ meaning 'and'
This is a question word asking about who can do something. It's the subject of the verb يَمْلِكُ (possesses/controls) in this ayah.

Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This connects to the previous statement about Allah being the Messiah, son of Mary, asking who then could prevent Allah if He willed to destroy them.
Basic preposition showing location or containment
Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. This word shows where the possessing happens in relation to the verb يَمْلِكُ (possesses/controls).
Added short vowels (fatha and sukun) to make it readable as 'ard' meaning earth
Added 'the' at the beginning and kasra at the end because it comes after the word فِى (in)

steps.m: to gather or bring things together

steps.n: Adding ي makes it mean 'all together' instead of just 'gather'

steps.m: all together (describing how something in the ayah happens)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word describes how something happens, like saying 'quickly' or 'completely' in English
Added kasra at the end to show possession or connection
Added laam with kasra at beginning to show purpose or direction. The first laam of Allah merges with this new laam.
Added و at beginning to connect this phrase to what follows (مُلْكُ - dominion/kingdom). This shows that the dominion belongs to Allah.

steps.m: to own or rule over something

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun by adding ُ and ْ marks. This makes it mean the thing itself (ownership/kingdom) instead of the action (to own/rule).
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject - it's telling us what belongs to Allah in this ayah
Adding ا turns the root into a noun meaning sky/heaven
The word for 'sky' becomes plural by adding -aat at the end, like how we add 's' in English to make things plural
Adding the definite article makes the noun definite. This word is the object of مُلْكُ (dominion/ownership) in this ayah.
The root letters أ-ر-ض become the noun 'earth' when you add the fatha mark
Two changes happen: 1) ٱلْ makes it 'the earth' specifically, and 2) the ِ at the end shows it follows the word فِى (in/on)
Added وَ (and) to connect this to the previous phrase about السَّمٰوٰتِ (the heavens). The ِ still shows it follows فِى (in/on)
This is a relative pronoun used for things (not people). In this ayah, it refers to whatever is between the heavens and earth.

steps.m: and what/and that which (connecting to previous statement)
steps.n: The prefix وَ connects this to the previous statement about Allah's dominion over the heavens and earth

steps.m: to be between or separate things

steps.n: Changed the action of separating into a word that describes the space between things
Added a fatha at the end to show this word describes position/location

steps.m: between them both (referring to السَّمٰوٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: Added هُمَا at the end to show we're talking about two things (the heavens and earth)
Added basic past tense pattern with fatha marks to connect the letters

Changed to present tense by: 1) adding يَ at start to show 'he', 2) changing middle vowel to sukun, 3) changing last vowel to damma, 4) adding final damma to show it's happening now. This verb tells what Allah creates (مَا meaning 'whatever' in this ayah).
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to things (not people). In this ayah, it refers to everything that exists between the heavens and earth.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense. The middle letter ي changes to ا because it's a weak letter.
Added يَ at the beginning to show it's happening now, and changed the ending to ُ to match.
Added long ا to show the extended nature of wanting/wishing
This is a unique proper name that doesn't come from a root. It's the personal name of God in Islam.
Added shadda on ل to show it's pronounced strongly

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of يَخْلُقُ - creates)
steps.n: Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of يَخْلُقُ - creates)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this part to what came before it, like saying 'and'

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of يَخْلُقُ - creates)
steps.n: Changed the alif to a waṣla form (ٱ) because it connects with the previous word in pronunciation

steps.m: upon/over (preposition showing authority over كُلِّ شَىْءٍ)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition showing position or authority over something. Here it connects to كُلِّ شَىْءٍ (all things) to show Allah's power over everything.

steps.m: upon/over (preposition showing authority over كُلِّ شَىْءٍ)
steps.n: Added an alif symbol (ىٰ) at the end to show the long 'aa' sound. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.
The doubled ل shows completeness, while the ُ on ك makes it into a noun
The ِ at the end shows this word is describing شَىْءٍ (thing) in the phrase 'of every thing'
This root represents the basic concept of a thing or entity
Connected the letters and added vowel marks. The ي gets a sukoon (ْ) to show it's silent.
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this thing is owned by كُلِّ (every/all) in the phrase كُلِّ شَىْءٍ (every thing).

steps.m: to determine, measure, have power over

steps.n: Adds ي to make it mean 'someone/something that has complete power' - like turning 'power' into 'powerful'
The tanwin (double damma ٌ) shows this is describing Allah's attribute of having complete power over everything mentioned in the ayah
The middle letter و changes to ا when we add the past tense pattern. This is a common change with middle-weak verbs.
Adding ت at the end changes 'he' to 'she' for past actions
Added و ('and') at the start. The ـتِ ending shows it's connected to what comes next.

steps.m: to guide back, to lead to repentance

steps.n: The root ه-و-د becomes يَهُودُ (Jews) with يَ at start and ُ ending to show they are the ones doing the action
Adds ٱلْ to specify we're talking about the Jews as a specific group (subject of قَالَتِ - 'said')
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Added shadda and alif to create intensive form showing someone who does this action frequently or strongly

steps.m: Christians (followers of Jesus who supported him)
steps.n: Added ى to create a special plural form for followers/adherents. The word came to specifically mean Christians because they were followers/supporters of Jesus.
Added و (and) and ال (the) at the beginning
Removed ال (the) from the word, making it general 'Christians' instead of 'the Christians'
This is a pronoun meaning 'we'. In this ayah, it's the subject of أَبْنٰٓؤُا۟ (sons/children), making the phrase mean 'we are the children'.
Changes the root into a noun meaning 'son' - something that comes from (is built from) parents
Changes 'son' to 'sons' by adding alif and changing the word pattern to make it plural
Adds damma (ُ) to show this is the subject of the sentence
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters
Added a small line under the last letter to show 'of' (possession), and doubled the middle letter with a shadda
Added vowels and combined the letters with shadda to show strong emphasis
Changes verb into noun describing someone who receives the love
Changes from one beloved person to many beloved people using a special plural form for important/honored people (like prophets, saints)
Changes ending to show this word is the subject/doer in the sentence
Shows these beloved ones belong to Him (Allah)
Adds 'and' to connect this to the previous word
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changed to a command form. The middle letter و drops out because it's a weak letter, and the vowels change to match the command pattern.
Starting with the question word ما (what), shortened to مَ when combined with other particles
Added لِ (for/to) at the beginning to change 'what' into 'why' (literally: for what)

steps.m: then why? (in response to their claim of being God's beloved children)
steps.n: Added فَ (then/so) to connect this question to the previous statement, showing it's a response to their claim
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Adding shadda doubles the middle root letter ذ to show the punishment is intense or repeated
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added كُم to show the punishment is happening to multiple people being addressed
Connects the letters together to form the basic noun for one sin
Changes vowels and adds و to make it plural, showing multiple sins
Adds بِ at beginning to show these sins are the reason for something (in this case, punishment mentioned in يُعَذِّبُكُم)
Adds كُم to show these sins belong to the people being addressed (the Jews and Christians who claimed to be God's children)

steps.m: rather (contrasting with what was said before about being children of Allah)
steps.n: This is a particle that introduces a contrasting statement. In this ayah, it's used to contrast with the previous claim of being Allah's children by saying 'rather, you are just human beings'.
This is the base form of the second person pronoun, not derived from a root
Added ت to make it a complete singular pronoun
Added م with damma to make it plural masculine. This word is used to address multiple men or a mixed group.
Added fatha vowels to connect the root letters and form a noun meaning 'human being'

steps.m: a human being (in contrast to أَنتُم 'you' in this ayah)
steps.n: Adding the two dots on top (tanween) makes it mean 'a human being' instead of just 'human being' - this shows contrast with 'you' in the verse
Starting with the two separate words: مِنْ (from) and مَنْ (who)
When مِنْ comes before a word starting with م, its ن combines with the م to make a stronger م sound (shown by ّ)

steps.m: to create or form something from nothing

steps.n: Adding the three fatha marks (َ) changes this from a basic concept into a past tense verb showing that Allah did this action. In this ayah, it's talking about how Allah created everyone.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, putting sukun on first root letter, kasra on middle root letter, and damma at the end. In this ayah, Allah is the one who forgives.
This is a basic interrogative/relative pronoun meaning 'who' or 'whom' depending on its position in the sentence. Here it means 'whom' because it's the object of the verb يَغْفِرُ (he forgives).

steps.m: for whom (object of يَغْفِرُ - he forgives)
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show this is about who receives the action of forgiving
Added fatha vowels and the weak letter alif replaced the root letter ي
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense and ُ at end to show he is doing the action
Added long ا to show extended duration of wanting/wishing
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the action stronger/more intense
يُ at start shows it's happening now to someone
The ُ shows this is a normal present tense verb
Added وَ (and) at start + changed from passive (being punished) to active (he punishes)
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun used to refer to people
Added fatha (َ) on م to show proper pronunciation
The root letters get vowels (fathas) and the middle letter ي becomes ا to make 'he wanted' in past tense

steps.m: he wants/wishes (object of لِمَن - for whom)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end. This word shows who Allah wants to forgive in this ayah.
Added long alif to show continuous/extended wanting
This is the base word meaning Allah, the proper name of God in Islam
Added ل at the beginning which combines with ال to become لل with a shadda, showing possession or belonging
Added vowel marks to show proper pronunciation: kasra under first ل, fatha on second ل, and kasra at the end
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what follows (مُلْكُ meaning 'dominion/kingdom'). The complete meaning is 'and to Allah belongs the dominion...'
Changes the concept of ruling/owning into the abstract noun meaning sovereignty or dominion. This word is connected to اللَّهِ (Allah) showing that the dominion belongs to Him.
Added vowels to connect the letters. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
The word for 'sky/heaven' gets 'waat' added at the end to make it mean 'many heavens'
The prefix 'ال' is added to make the word definite. It's like adding 'the' before 'heavens' in English, making it specific.
The kasra at the end shows this word is possessed by مُلْكُ (dominion) in the phrase مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ (dominion of the heavens)
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation: fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter
Adding the definite article makes the noun definite
Adding 'و' connects this word to what came before it
Adding kasra shows this word is possessed by Allah (اللَّهِ) in this context, as part of the phrase 'to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth'

steps.m: what/that which (connects to بَيْنَهُمَا - what is between them)
steps.n: This is a particle word with no root - it's used to refer to things in a general way. In this ayah, it refers to what exists between the heavens and earth.

steps.m: and what/that which (connects to بَيْنَهُمَا - what is between them)
steps.n: Adds و with fatha to connect this phrase to the previous mention of heavens and earth
This root became a preposition that shows something is in the middle of other things
Added fatha and sukun to form the preposition. This shows the space or separation between things.

steps.m: between them both (referring to السَّمٰوٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: Added هُمَا which shows this refers to exactly two things (the heavens and earth mentioned in the ayah)
This is a preposition with no root
Changed ى to ي with sukun to prepare for adding the pronoun

steps.m: to him/it (connected to المَصِيرُ - the destination)
steps.n: Added pronoun هِ meaning 'him/it'. This shows the destination (المَصِيرُ) is returning to Allah.

steps.m: and to him/it (connected to المَصِيرُ - the destination)
steps.n: Added و with fatha to mean 'and', connecting this phrase to what came before
Added مَ at the beginning to make it mean 'the place/destination where something ends up' instead of the action of going somewhere
Added ُ to show this is the main subject
Added ٱلْ to point to a specific destination
Added vowel marks to make it into a noun meaning the people who belong to or inhabit a place
Added يٰٓ at the beginning to show we're calling out to these people, and َ at the end to show it's connected to what comes next (الْكِتٰبِ - of the Book)
Changed the verb 'write' into a noun 'book' by adding long alif after second letter and kasra on first letter
Added ال to show this refers to a specific book - the divine scripture
This is already the final form - no changes needed from previous step
This is a particle that emphasizes that something has definitely happened. In this ayah, it emphasizes that a messenger has certainly come to them.
The root letters changed to make 'he came' - the middle letter yaa turned into alif which is common for hollow verbs

steps.m: he came to you all (subject of يُبَيِّنُ)
steps.n: Added كُمْ (meaning 'to you all') at the end to show who he came to. The long آ shows emphasis. This word is connected to يُبَيِّنُ (he explains) later in the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to the pattern that shows someone who regularly does the action of sending/delivering
Added نَا meaning 'our' to show the messenger belongs to us. This messenger is doing the action of 'coming' in the verse.
Added fatha vowels and doubled the middle letter to show basic past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. This verb shows what our messenger (رَسُولُنَا) is doing.
Added fatha to the basic preposition ل to show it's connected to what follows

steps.m: for you all (clarifies يُبَيِّنُ - he makes clear)
steps.n: Added the plural 'you' pronoun كم to show who the action is for. This connects to the verb يُبَيِّنُ (he makes clear) in the ayah, showing who he makes things clear for.
Basic preposition showing something is above or has authority over something else, like saying 'the book is on the table' or 'he has authority over them' in English

steps.m: upon/on/over (connected to كُلِّ شَىْءٍ - every thing)
steps.n: Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end to show this is the end of the word. In this ayah, it shows Allah's power over all things.
Changed from basic root to noun meaning 'a pause/break' by adding vowel marks
Added ة at the end to show one specific instance of pausing
Added tanwin kasra at end to show this is indefinite and being acted upon by عَلَىٰ (upon)
This is a preposition that shows origin, source, or partitive meaning. The shadda is added due to assimilation with preceding ن sound.

steps.m: to send or be sent as a messenger
steps.n: This form makes it a plural noun meaning 'messengers'
Added damma vowels to make it a broken plural form meaning multiple messengers
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific messengers, and doubled the ر with shadda because it comes after ال
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word مِّنَ (from) which requires this ending. Also changed the ا to ٱ which is just a writing style.
This is a particle that introduces what someone might say or do. Here it connects to تَقُولُوا (you say) to show what the People of the Book might say.
Added basic past tense form with fatha vowels. The middle و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb.
Changed from past tense 'he said' to command form 'say!' by adding damma to first letter. The middle alif changes back to original و.
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' are doing the action
Added وا at end to show multiple people. The word comes after أَن (that) which requires the subjunctive mood ending وا instead of ون.
This is the basic form of the word that can be used to ask questions like 'what?'
When مَا comes before a past tense verb like جَآءَنَا (came to us), it changes from asking 'what?' to saying 'not' or 'did not'
These are the three basic letters that give us the core meaning of coming or arriving
Added fathas and the middle letter ي changes to ا because it's a special kind of word where middle letters can change. This shows the action happened in the past.
Added a long alif to emphasize the action of coming
Added نَا at the end to show who he came to - us!
This is a preposition showing that the 'gap/period' comes from 'the messengers'. It connects these two words to show their relationship.
Added a small ن (called nun ghunna) at the end to help with smooth pronunciation when connecting to the next word
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed the middle vowel to kasra and added ي to show this describes someone who does this action
Added tanween kasra to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a' instead of 'the')
This is the basic negative particle that negates what comes after it
Added و at the beginning to connect this negation with what came before it

steps.m: to warn or alert of danger
steps.n: This is a broken plural form meaning multiple warnings
Changes from basic root to mean 'someone who does the warning'
Added the tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this is describing 'any warner' in general. This describes رَسُولُنَا (our messenger) in the ayah.
This is an emphatic particle that shows something definitely happened or will happen. In this ayah, it emphasizes that a messenger has indeed come to them.

steps.m: then certainly/so indeed (connects to previous statement about no bearer of good news coming)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this statement with what came before. It shows that because they said no bearer of good news had come to them, now indeed one has come.
Basic past tense verb with fatha marks

steps.m: he came to you all (referring to the People of the Book being addressed)
steps.n: Added كُم at the end to show the action was directed at multiple people being spoken to. In this ayah, it refers to the People of the Book (أَهْلَ ٱلْكِتٰبِ) who are being addressed.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb

steps.m: one who gives good news (describes رَسُولُنَا)
steps.n: Changed the word to show who does the action, like changing 'teach' to 'teacher'. This describes رَسُولُنَا (our messenger) in the ayah.

steps.m: to warn or alert someone about danger
steps.n: This is a broken plural form meaning multiple warnings

steps.m: one who warns (someone who tells others about danger)
steps.n: Added ي between the last two root letters and vowel marks to create a word for someone who does the warning
The و connects this word to what came before it
This is the unique proper name of God in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning of divinity/worship but is a special word that only refers to the One God.
Added shadda on ل to show it is doubled in pronunciation
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence
Added و at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement, plus the helper alif that always comes before the definite article ال

steps.m: upon/over (showing position in time during فَتْرَةٍ)
steps.n: Basic preposition showing position or authority over something. In this ayah, it shows when the messenger came - during a break in messengers.

steps.m: upon/over (showing position in time during فَتْرَةٍ)
steps.n: Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end to show this is a fixed spelling for this word. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with reading.
Added damma and doubled the middle letter to create a word meaning 'all' or 'every'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to the word after it (شَىْءٍ meaning 'thing')
Added vowels to the root letters to make the basic word for 'thing'
Adds the genitive case ending because it follows the word كُلِّ (every/all) in the ayah
This form makes it a noun meaning the act or result of determining
Root letters connected with vowels to make an active participle meaning 'one who has power'
Added ي to make it mean 'very powerful' instead of just 'powerful' - like adding 'very' or 'all' in English

steps.m: all-powerful, having complete ability (as a quality of اللَّهُ)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this describes Allah's attribute mentioned in this ayah
This is a time particle that introduces when something happened in the past. Here it introduces when Moses spoke to his people.
The و at the start connects this story to previous stories being told, showing it's part of a sequence of events being narrated
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
The و changes to ا because it's a weak letter
This is the basic form of the name Moses, borrowed from Hebrew. In Arabic, foreign names are adapted to fit Arabic pronunciation patterns.
Added damma (ُ) on م and fatha (َ) on س to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: Moses (the prophet who spoke to قَوْمِهِ)
steps.n: Added alif khanjariya (ٰ) after ى which is a small alif mark showing this is the end of the word. In this ayah, Moses is speaking to his people (قَوْمِهِ).

steps.m: to stand upright, rise up, establish

steps.n: Changed the root meaning from an action (standing) to a group of people who stand together as a community
The kasra ending shows this word follows لِ (to/for)
Added هِ (his) to show these are specifically Moses's people, as we can see from the context وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ (And when Moses said)

steps.m: to his people (those Moses is speaking to)
steps.n: Added لِ (to) at the beginning because Moses is speaking to/addressing his people. The small ۦ after هِ is just a pronunciation mark.

steps.m: to stand together, to rise up, to establish

steps.n: Changed from an action to a group noun - refers to people who stand/rise together as one community
Adds 'O!' at start to call out to someone
The ِ at the end shows possession - makes it 'my people'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he remembered' to 'remember!' by adding U- at start and making it a command to one person
Added وا at the end to make the command apply to a group instead of one person
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into a noun representing one instance of blessing
The ending shows this word is being used as an object in the sentence. It is the object of the command 'remember' (اذْكُرُوا), telling the people what to remember.
Added shadda to emphasize that this is the one and only God
Added kasra at the end to show this belongs to نِعْمَةَ (favor/blessing). Also changed alif to wasl-alif since it connects with previous word.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'
The ى changes to يْ when connecting to pronouns
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to
This is a time word that shows when something happened in the past. Here it shows when Moses spoke to his people.

steps.m: to make something into something else

steps.n: Added fathas (َ) after each letter to show this happened in the past and was done by 'he'
Basic preposition meaning 'in' or 'among'

steps.m: in/among you all (referring to the people Moses is addressing)
steps.n: Added the plural 'you' pronoun كم to show who is being addressed. This word connects to جَعَلَ (he made) in this ayah, showing who received the action.

steps.m: one who tells important news from Allah
steps.n: Changed from 'telling news' to 'a person who brings news from Allah (a prophet)'
This is a special plural form for important people. The word is the object of جَعَلَ (He made) in this ayah.

steps.m: to make something into something else

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past
Added وَ (and) to connect this action with what came before in the ayah

steps.m: and he made you all (into kings - مُّلُوكًا)
steps.n: Added كُم (you all) to show who was made into kings. This connects to مُّلُوكًا (kings) which comes right after in the ayah.
Changes from verb 'to rule' into noun meaning 'one who rules'
Changed the word to show many kings instead of just one
Added ًا to show this word is being used as a direct object in the sentence
Doubled the first letter to add extra emphasis to the word
Added و (meaning 'and') at beginning + changed the root letters to make 'he gave' instead of 'to come'
Added كُم at the end to show that the giving was done to multiple people being addressed
This is a relative pronoun that refers to things rather than people. The shadda emphasizes the initial meem.

steps.m: not (makes the following verb يُؤْتِ refer to the past)
steps.n: This is a negative particle that changes the meaning of the verb after it to show that something did not happen in the past. In this ayah, it works with يُؤْتِ to mean 'did not give'.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at start. The أ became ؤ because it comes after the ُ sound.
The final ي is dropped because this verb comes after لَمْ which requires the jussive mood
Connected the root letters together with fatha on first letter
Added tanween fatha to show this is the object receiving the action of 'giving' (يُؤْتِ). This means no one was given what Allah gave to them.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows where something comes from or what it belongs to
The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after a connecting letter (و), which causes the first letter to be doubled in pronunciation
The fatha (َ) is added at the end to help connect this word smoothly with the next word (الْعٰلَمِينَ)
Added alif after first letter and vowels to make it mean 'world' - the thing that contains everything we can know about
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many worlds. This ending also shows it comes after the word مِنَ (from/of).
Added ال at the beginning to show we're talking about specific worlds, not just any worlds. The alif in عالم becomes longer (ٰ) when ال is added.

steps.m: to stand together, rise up as one

steps.n: Added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning 'people' - those who stand together as one community

steps.m: O my people! (addressing them before ٱدْخُلُوا۟)
steps.n: Added يٰ at start to call out to them, and ِ at end to show possession (my people). This is Moses calling out to his people before telling them to enter the holy land.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed from 'he entered' to 'enter!' by adding u- at start and changing ending - makes it a command to one person
Changed from commanding one person to commanding many people by adding وا at the end
Added basic vowel marks to connect the letters
Adding ال makes it specific - 'the land' instead of just any land. This is the object of the command 'enter' (ٱدْخُلُوا۟)
The fatHa at the end shows this is the object being acted upon by the command 'enter'
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters

steps.m: he purified intensely, made very holy
steps.n: Intensifies the meaning through doubling middle root letter

steps.m: being made holy (describes something receiving the action)
steps.n: Changed from 'he made holy' to 'being made holy' by adding مُ to show something receives the action instead of does it
Added ة to make it describe a feminine word (الأرض/land)
Added ال to match with the specific land being discussed

steps.m: the holy (feminine, describing the land that is being entered)
steps.n: Added َ at end because this describes what is being entered (object of ادخلوا/enter)
Added kasra under ت to show it's a feminine word
Added ال at the beginning to make it more specific, like adding 'the one' in English

steps.m: which (referring to الْأَرْضَ ٱلْمُقَدَّسَةَ - the holy land)
steps.n: Changed spelling but same meaning: Points to 'the holy land' and connects it to what comes next in the sentence
Connected the root letters together to form a word

steps.m: he wrote (past action that اللَّهُ did)
steps.n: Added fatha vowels to show this is a past tense verb. In this ayah, Allah (اللَّهُ) is the one who did the writing/decreeing.
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the basic word for 'deity'
This is the unique name for the One True God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ُ shows this is the subject of كَتَبَ (wrote).
Added fatha to show the vowel sound
Added كُمْ at the end to show this is directed at multiple people. This word is connected to كَتَبَ (wrote) in this ayah, showing what was written for them.
Basic negative particle that negates the verb تَرْتَدُّوا (turn back) in this ayah

steps.m: and not (connects to previous command ٱدْخُلُوا)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this negative command with the previous positive command to enter the land
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Adding ت at start shows the action is done to oneself
Added وا at the end to make it 'you all' (plural) + changed middle vowels to match this form
Basic preposition showing position or direction. Here it connects to the word أَدْبَارِكُمْ (your backs) to show direction.
Added fatha marks to show correct pronunciation
Added alif maksura mark to show the long 'aa' sound at the end
Added maddah mark to show the extended pronunciation at the end

steps.m: to turn back or follow behind

steps.n: Root letters got vowels (fatha) to make it mean the physical back part of something
Changed to plural by adding أ at the start and ا after ب to mean 'multiple backs'
Added kasra to show possession
Added كُمْ to show it belongs to multiple people being addressed
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Added تَنْ at start to show 'you' doing the action to yourself (reflexive), and changed vowels to show present tense

steps.m: and then you turn yourself over
steps.n: Added conjunction ف at the beginning to mean 'and then' or 'so'

steps.m: and then you all turn back (as a warning after ٱدْخُلُوا۟)
steps.n: Changed from single person to multiple people by adding وا at the end (matches with earlier command ٱدْخُلُوا۟)
Adding vowels to show this is a past tense verb meaning 'to lose'
Adding alif after first letter changes this from an action to someone who does that action
Adding ين makes this plural (more than two). This describes the people who will become losers if they turn back, as mentioned in the verb فَتَنقَلِبُوا (then you will turn back/return).
The َ (fatha) at the end shows this word is in the accusative case because it follows the word فَتَنقَلِبُوا (then you will turn back)
Adding vowel marks to make basic past tense verb pattern
Adding وا shows that a group of people (they) did the action. In this ayah, it refers to the Children of Israel speaking to Moses.
This is a proper name borrowed from ancient Egyptian meaning 'born of' or 'child of', commonly associated with Prophet Moses (peace be upon him)
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation

steps.m: O Moses! (being called by قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Added يٰ at the beginning to show someone is being called or addressed directly. The long alif (ٰٓ) at the end shows a pause in speech.

steps.m: indeed (emphasizes the statement فِيهَا قَوْمًا جَبَّارِينَ - 'in it are mighty people')
steps.n: This word makes what comes after it stronger or more certain. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' at the start of a sentence in English. In this ayah, it emphasizes that there are definitely mighty people in the land.
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment
Added ها which refers back to the place they were discussing. From context, this refers to the land/city they were afraid to enter.

steps.m: to stand or rise up together

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the letters. The root meaning of standing together became associated with a group of people who stand as one.

steps.m: a people (being described in this ayah)
steps.n: Added an -an ending to show this is talking about 'a people' who are receiving the action in this sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The doubled ب (with shadda) and added alif show someone who does this action strongly or repeatedly
Added ين to make it plural. This describes the people (قَوْمًا) that Moses and his people found in the land
This is an emphasis word that makes statements stronger, like adding 'definitely' in English
Added ا at the end to show 'we' are the subject being emphasized
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase with the previous statement. In the ayah, this connects to their statement about the powerful people in the land.
This is a special word that makes something impossible in the future. In this ayah, it's used with يَخْرُجُوا۟ (they exit) to say 'they will not exit.'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense 'we' form by adding ن at start and changing vowels
Added ها to show what is being entered (referring back to the city mentioned earlier)
Added shadda on ن because it comes after إِنَّا لَن in the ayah
Added fatha and shadda to form the basic particle. This word connects to يَخْرُجُوا۟ (they exit) to show the limit or extent of an action.
Added the ى at the end which is a standard part of this particle's spelling.
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى which is a pronunciation guide showing that this ى is pronounced as a long 'aa' sound.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they leave/exit (referring to the strong people mentioned)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people doing the action. This refers to the جَبَّارِينَ (strong people) mentioned earlier in the verse.
This is a basic preposition showing something coming from somewhere else

steps.m: from it/her (referring to the land/city they won't enter)
steps.n: Added ها (meaning 'it/her') to مِنْ (from) to say 'from it' - referring back to the land/city
The فَ shows this condition follows from the previous statement. Here it connects to يَخْرُجُوا۟ (they leave), meaning 'then if they leave'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Changes from past tense (he went out) to present tense (he goes out) by adding يَ at the start
The وا suffix changes the subject from 'he' to 'they'. The word follows لَن which means 'will not' in this ayah.
Basic preposition meaning 'from'. The sukun (ْ) is added to show where the word stops.

steps.m: from it (feminine) (referring to the land/city they won't enter)
steps.n: Added ها at the end to show what we're talking about (the land/city). ها means 'it' when talking about something feminine.
Starting with the emphatic particle إِنَّ which adds certainty to what follows
Adding ا (alif) attaches 'we' as the subject to 'indeed'
Adding فَ to show this is a result or consequence of what was mentioned before (if they leave, then we will enter)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to active participle form to describe someone who does the action

steps.m: ones who enter (masculine plural, predicate of إِنَّا)
steps.n: Added masculine plural ending to show multiple people. This word describes 'we' in the sentence (after إِنَّا meaning 'indeed we')
Added vowel marks to make past tense. This word is what two men (رَجُلَانِ) did.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter). This is a common change that happens with these types of words to make them easier to say.
The vowels make it into a noun meaning 'man'

steps.m: two men (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added انِ to show exactly two men. The ِ (kasra) at the end shows this is the subject doing the action.
Basic preposition showing something comes from somewhere or belongs to a group
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly to the next word الَّذِينَ (those who). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun used to connect descriptions to people or things
Added ال (the) to make it mean 'the one who' instead of just 'who'

steps.m: those who (subject of يَخَافُونَ - they fear)
steps.n: Added ين with fatha to show this refers to multiple people. This word connects to يَخَافُونَ (they fear) in the ayah, describing the people who are afraid.
Added fathas to make it past tense. The و changes to ا because it's between two fathas.
Adding يَ at the start and ُ at the end shows it's happening in the present
Adding ون at the end makes it plural - now it's about multiple people fearing
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding أَ at the beginning changes the meaning from 'being blessed' to 'giving blessings to others'. This word is connected to قَالَ (he said) in this ayah.
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject
The ى changes to ي when adding suffixes. This is a common spelling change in Arabic.
Added هِ to show who/what is being referred to
Added ما to show it refers to exactly two people
Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it a command (imperative) form
Added helper alif at start to make pronunciation easier
Added plural ending to make it a command for multiple people
The base preposition عَلَى changes its final ى to ي when connecting to pronouns
Added هِ which means 'him/it'
Added مُ at the end to change from 'upon him' to 'upon them' (for multiple people)
The middle letter و changes to ا, which is common with hollow roots (roots with و or ي in the middle). The word becomes a noun meaning a specific thing.
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it specific and َ at the end to show it's the object being acted upon by the verb ٱدْخُلُوا۟ (enter)

steps.m: when (connects to دَخَلْتُمُوهُ - you enter it)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that shows something will happen at a specific time. Here it connects to the next word دَخَلْتُمُوهُ (you enter it).

steps.m: and then when (connects to دَخَلْتُمُوهُ - you enter it)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before - it's connecting the previous statement about entering the gate to what will happen after they enter.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
The تُ at the end shows that 'I' did the action
Changed تُ (meaning 'I') to تُمُ (meaning 'you all') to show many people did the action
Added هُ meaning 'it' at the end (و is just a helper letter to connect smoothly)
This is the basic emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before
Added كُمْ at the end to show this refers to multiple people being spoken to
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed pattern to show who does the action
Changed alif to alif with bar on top (purely spelling variation)
Added plural ending to show multiple people are victorious
This is a basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'. It's used to show something is on top of or dependent on something else.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. This و means 'and' and joins related ideas together.
Changed the root into the basic noun form for 'a deity'
Changed to the proper name for the One God by adding ال and merging letters. The shadda shows the ل is doubled.
Added kasra at the end to show possession. This word is possessed by the verb 'rely upon' (فَتَوَكَّلُوٓا۟) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger

steps.m: he put his complete trust (in someone)
steps.n: Adding تَ shows doing the action to oneself - making oneself trust

steps.m: so put your complete trust (in someone)
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this is a result or consequence

steps.m: so all of you put your complete trust (in Allah)
steps.n: Adding وا makes it a command for a group
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects 'if you are' (كُنتُم) with what came before it, meaning 'if you are believers, then do these things.'
Command form - just uses the basic root letters with 'u' vowel, dropping the middle و
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this in the past (not 'you')
Added م to make it plural, showing it refers to multiple people. This word is connected to مُّؤْمِنِينَ (believers) in this ayah.
Added مُ at the start to show who does the action, like changing 'trust' to 'truster'
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many believers
The doubled م adds extra emphasis to show these are strong/firm believers
Changed و to ا and added fathas to show past tense. This is a common change in words with و in the middle.
Added وا with damma before it to show many people did the action. In this ayah, it refers to the people speaking to Moses.
This is the proper name Moses (مُوسَىٰ). The alif at the end (ىٰ) is written specially in Arabic names.

steps.m: O Moses! (being called by the people)
steps.n: Added يٰ at the beginning to show someone is calling out to Moses. The ٓ (small alif) at the end shows a slight pause in the calling sound. This is how people address Moses directly when speaking to him.
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' at the start of a sentence.
Adding ا at the end shows this is about 'we/us'. In this ayah, it connects to قٰعِدُونَ (staying/sitting) to say 'indeed we are staying here'.

steps.m: will never (negates نَّدْخُلَهَآ - we will enter)
steps.n: This is a particle that strongly negates future actions. Here it's used with the verb نَّدْخُلَهَآ (we will enter) to say they will definitely not enter.
This is the basic concept of entering
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Added 'na' (ن) at start to make it 'we', and changed vowels to show present tense
Added fatha (َ) at the end to show this is after 'that' or 'so that'
Added هَا (meaning 'it') at the end to show what is being entered
Changed the ending to the formal written form with long alif
Adding vowel marks to make it pronounceable

steps.m: forever (describing how long they won't enter)
steps.n: Adding ًا at the end shows this word describes how long the action (not entering) will last. It connects to the verb يَدْخُلَهَا (enter it) in the phrase لَن نَّدْخُلَهَآ (we will never enter it).
This is a connecting word that shows time duration. Here it connects to دَامُوا (they remain) to mean 'as long as they remain'.
Added emphasis mark (shadda) because this word connects to the next word. This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
The root letters د-و-م became دَامَ (he remained) by adding vowels and changing و to ا - a common change in hollow verbs
Added وا at the end to show that a group of people did the action
This is a basic preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Since it's a particle, it doesn't come from a root.

steps.m: in it (referring to the land they won't enter)
steps.n: Added ها which refers back to the land they're talking about. This connects the preposition to what it's talking about.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he went' to 'go!' by adding اِ at start and removing vowels - this makes it a command to one person

steps.m: so go! (command to Moses in response to what was said)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this is a response or result of what came before. The people are telling Moses to go because they refuse to enter the land.
This is a personal pronoun used when speaking directly to one male person. In this ayah, it's being used to address Prophet Moses specifically.
The doubled middle letter (shadda) transforms this into a title or role, emphasizing the one who does the nurturing completely
The ُ shows this word is the main subject or focus in the sentence
The كَ at the end shows this master/nurturer belongs to or is connected to you (if speaking to one male)
The وَ connects this to the previous words. In this ayah, it's part of the Israelites speaking to Moses about him and his Lord.
Added vowels to make past tense 'he' form
Added ا to show two people fighting each other
Added 'fa' (so/then) at the start to connect it to what happened before

steps.m: so you two fight! (command to two people)
steps.n: Adding alif at the end makes it a command for two people instead of one person
This is an emphatic particle that makes what comes after it more certain. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'surely' in English.

steps.m: indeed we (subject of قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Added ا at the end to show this means 'we'. This word is part of what they said, as shown by قَالُوا (they said) at the start of the verse.
This is the basic word meaning 'here' in Arabic
Added هٰ at the beginning to make the meaning more specific and emphatic, like saying 'right here' while pointing to an exact spot

steps.m: to sit still or stay in one place
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of sitting or staying in one place
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changes from showing an action that happened to showing who is doing it right now

steps.m: ones who are sitting/staying still (referring to the people who refused to enter the holy land with Musa)
steps.n: The ون ending shows this refers to multiple men. In this ayah, it refers to the people telling Musa they will stay behind while he and his Lord go fight.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word introduces what Musa says to his Lord (رَبِّ).
The middle letter و changes to ا because it comes after fatha - this is a common change in hollow verbs

steps.m: to take care of, raise, nurture

steps.n: The doubled ب (shown by ّ) makes the meaning stronger, showing complete and perfect care. This word is being used as a form of address to Allah.
Adding kasra (ِ) at the end makes this a special way of calling out to Allah, like saying 'O my Lord!'
This is a word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like saying 'truly' or 'definitely' in English

steps.m: indeed I (speaking to رَبِّ - my Lord)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to show 'I' am speaking. The ن gets doubled (with shadda) when we add this ending. This word starts Musa's statement to Allah.
This is the basic negative particle that means 'no' or 'not'. It negates what comes after it.
Marks the word with fatha to show it negates the next word (I have control)
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer for emphasis
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

Changed to first person present tense by adding أَ at start, putting sukun on م, kasra under ل, and damma at the end. This word comes after لَا which means 'not', so together they mean 'I do not control/own'

steps.m: except (what comes after it from what came before)
steps.n: This is an exception particle that excludes what comes after it from what came before

steps.m: except (نَفْسِى وَأَخِى from what I control)
steps.n: In this ayah, إِلَّا excludes 'myself and my brother' from what the speaker has control over. It connects to لَآ أَمْلِكُ (I do not control) before it.
Basic noun pattern that makes the root into a thing. This word is the object of أَمْلِكُ (I control/possess) in this ayah.
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by something

steps.m: my self, my soul (object of أَمْلِكُ)
steps.n: Added ى at the end to show this belongs to 'me'. This whole phrase means 'I do not control except my self' in the ayah.
Connected the root letters together. The final weak letter و is dropped in this form.
Added ي at the end to show possession (my)

steps.m: and my brother (connected to نَفْسِى - myself)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this word with the previous word نَفْسِى (myself). Together they form 'myself and my brother'.

steps.m: to separate or make a distinction between things

steps.n: Adding fatha vowels creates the simple past tense verb form
Changed from 'he separated' to 'you separate!' by adding U sound at start and making middle letter silent - this makes it a command
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence of what came before
The root meaning of separation becomes a word meaning 'between' - the space that separates things.
Added نَا to show this means 'between us'. This word is what is being separated in the command 'separate between us'.

steps.m: to separate or distinguish between things

steps.n: Adding vowels changes this from a root into a common preposition

steps.m: and between (connects to ٱلْقَوْمِ - the people)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to mean 'and', connecting this phrase to the previous part about 'myself and my brother'
Changed the root meaning from an action (standing) to a group of people who do that action together. The word قَوْم refers to a group that stands together as one community.
Added the genitive case ending ِ because this word comes after بَيْنَ (between) in بَيْنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ (between the people)
Adds definite article to make the noun definite
Changes from describing the action to describing the person who does it
Regular masculine plural form. This word is in the genitive case because it describes and belongs to القوم (the people) in the phrase القوم الفاسقين (the disobedient people).
Added ال to make it specific and a final َ to show it follows the word القوم (the people)
Added fathas to make the past tense form. This word introduces Allah's statement about the punishment.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
This is an emphasis word that makes you sure about what comes after it. The shadda (ّ) makes the emphasis even stronger.

steps.m: so indeed (connects to قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: The فَ means 'so' or 'then' and connects this statement to what came before (قَالَ - he said)

steps.m: so indeed it (referring to the land/region)
steps.n: The هَا refers to something feminine we're talking about (in this case, the land or region that's being discussed)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger
Adding مُ at the start changes it from an action to describing something affected by the action
Adding ة makes this describe something feminine that is forbidden
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation. عَلَى is a common word that shows something is on top of or affecting something else.
Changed the ending to prepare for adding the pronoun. The ى changes to يْ when we add something after it.

steps.m: upon them (referring to the disobedient people mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added هِمْ at the end to show who is being affected. This refers back to the disobedient people mentioned in the previous verses.

steps.m: to make into four parts, to form a group of four

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to make it a proper past tense verb
Adding أ at start changes it from 'be four' to 'make into four'
Added ين to make it mean 'forty'. This number tells us how many years (سَنَةً) they would wander.
Added the fatha at the end to show this number is being used as an object in the sentence
Added basic vowel marks to connect the root letters

steps.m: a year (one complete cycle of time)
steps.n: The word gets ة at the end to mean 'one year' instead of just the idea of time passing
Adding tanween fatha (ً) shows this word is being counted by the number forty (أَرْبَعِينَ) in the ayah
Root letters put together with 'i' sound to make a noun meaning the state of being lost
Added يَ at start to show present tense action
Added ُ to show this is the subject doing the action

steps.m: they wander/are lost (in الْأَرْضِ - the earth)
steps.n: Added ونَ to show multiple people doing the action. This word connects to الْأَرْضِ showing where they wander.
This is a basic preposition that shows location or being inside something. In this ayah, it shows where they are wandering (يَتِيهُونَ).
The ي at the end changes to ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters together
Adding ٱلْ makes it specific - talking about THE earth
Adding kasra shows this word comes after the word فِى (in) in this ayah
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: and so not/then do not (connected to تَأْسَ)
steps.n: Added لا which means 'not'. This word connects to تَأْسَ (grieve) to say 'then do not grieve'
Connected root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense. The final و changes to ا when it comes at the end.
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and putting a sukun on the middle letter. The final alif changes to ى to show ongoing sadness.
Adding تَ shows the person is feeling the sadness themselves
This is a primitive preposition (حرف جر) that doesn't derive from a root
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation: fatha (َ) on ع and alif at end makes long 'aa' sound
The root meaning of standing/establishing becomes a noun for an established group of people
Adding ال makes it specific - these particular people
The kasra shows this word describes 'the defiantly disobedient' (الفٰسِقِينَ) that follows it

steps.m: one who breaks away from obedience
steps.n: This pattern describes someone who does the action. The word is in the genitive case because it describes what kind of people القوم are - they are the disobedient people.
Adding ال makes the noun definite, specifying these particular disobedient ones

steps.m: the disobedient ones (describing القوم - the people)
steps.n: The ين ending shows this is plural and describes القوم (the people) in the phrase

steps.m: to follow or recite something in order

steps.n: Added helper alif at start to make the command easier to pronounce
Added وَ to connect this command to what came before it. The alif gets a wavy top (ٱ) because it comes after وَ.

steps.m: upon (preposition showing direction towards ابْنَىْ ءَادَمَ)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows something is directed towards or affecting something else. In this ayah, it's directing the action towards 'the two sons of Adam'
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for attaching the next part. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added هِمْ which means 'them' (for a group). In this ayah, it refers to the two sons of Adam who are being talked about.

steps.m: to tell news or important information

steps.n: Adding the vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb
Same spelling but different meaning - changed from 'he told' (verb) to 'news' (noun) that is being recited
Root 'build/produce' becomes the common word for 'son'
Changed spelling of initial alif to help pronunciation
Added dual ending to show exactly two sons. This word is connected to آدَمَ (Adam) in the ayah, showing these are Adam's two sons.

steps.m: to be brown/tanned (referring to skin or earth)

steps.n: The root letters were turned into the name Adam by adding a long 'aa' sound at the beginning. Adam was the first human created by Allah.

steps.m: Adam (as the object of ٱبْنَىْ/sons of)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show Adam is the object of 'sons of' in this ayah
The root letters ح-ق-ق became حَقِّ (truth) with a kasra ending because it comes after the word بِ (with/by)
Added بِ (with/by) at the start and ال (the) to make it specific

steps.m: when (connecting to قَرَّبَا - they both offered)
steps.n: This is a time word that introduces past events. In this ayah, it connects to قَرَّبَا (they both offered) to show when something happened.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Doubling the middle letter changes the meaning from 'coming close' to 'making something/someone close'
Adding alif at the end makes it dual form (for two people)

steps.m: to come near or bring something close

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun meaning the state of being near
The suffix ان makes it mean 'something characterized by nearness' - in this case an offering brought near to Allah

steps.m: an offering (as the object being presented)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this is the object being offered in the story of the two sons of Adam
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to accept'
Changed the vowel on first letter to damma and doubled the middle letter to show something was done to it rather than it doing something
Adding تُ at the start shows something was done rather than someone doing it
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened as a result of something before it
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or starts. In this ayah, it shows who the acceptance came from.
Adding short vowels (fat-ha) to spell out the number 'one'
The word changes to show it belongs to or is connected to something else
Added هِ to show possession by a single male
Added ما to show it refers to two people
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce this negation particle that makes verbs refer to the past
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added ت at beginning and doubled middle letter to show thorough acceptance
Added ي to make it present tense
Changed the first vowel to ُ and middle to ِ to show something is being accepted rather than accepting
Added sukun at end because this is in a negative context with لَمْ
This is the basic form of the word that means 'from'
Added a fatha (َ) at the end to connect smoothly with the next word that starts with ال. This is a common change in Arabic to make words flow better when speaking.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added long alif (آ) at start to make it mean 'another' or 'other'
Added ال to make it specific and ِ because it comes after مِنَ (from)
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed middle weak letter و to ا, which is very common in hollow verbs
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding hamza prefix for first person singular present tense conjugation. This transforms the verb to indicate the subject is 'I'.
Adding emphatic lam prefix for emphasis

steps.m: surely I will kill (with strong emphasis)
