steps.n: Adding كَ shows you are receiving the action. This connects to the context where Allah questions how they seek your judgment while having the Torah.
This is a common preposition in Arabic that comes from the idea of being present with something
The vowels change the word into a preposition meaning 'near' or 'with'
Added هُم to show who the preposition relates to
Added وَ to connect this phrase to what came before, and ُ at the end to show this connects to what follows (they have the Torah)
This is a borrowed word from Hebrew (Torah) referring to the Jewish holy book. The base form includes the ة which is part of the borrowed word structure.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation: fatha on ت, sukun on و
The ت has a shadda because it merged with another ت before it
Added ال (with connecting hamza) to make the word definite, meaning 'the Torah' instead of just 'Torah'
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of حُكْمُ (judgment) in this ayah
Added kasra (ِ) to show this is a preposition meaning 'in'
Added ها to show this refers to the Torah (التَّوْرَىٰةُ) which was mentioned earlier in the verse

steps.m: to judge or make wise decisions

steps.n: Changes the verb into a noun meaning 'judgment' or 'ruling' - this is what results from the act of judging
Adds the ُ ending to show this word is connected to 'Allah' in the phrase 'judgment of Allah'
Added shadda to show that the ل is doubled in pronunciation
Added kasra at the end to show possession. This word is connected to حُكْمُ (judgment) in this ayah, showing that the judgment belongs to Allah.

Added damma and shadda which are required parts of this word. This word shows that their turning away happens after they seek your judgment.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter shows this is done with intensity or multiple times

steps.m: he turned himself away/took himself in charge
steps.n: Adding تَ at start shows the person is doing this action to themselves
Adding وْنَ shows that multiple people are doing the action
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects with the word بَعْدِ (after) to mean 'from after'.
Added a small ن (called tanwin) at the end to help with pronunciation when connecting to the next word بَعْدِ

steps.m: to be far away from something

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the letters. The meaning shifted from physical distance to distance in time (after).
Added kasra at the end to show this word connects to the word ذٰلِكَ (that) to form the phrase 'after that'
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show distance
Added ل with kasra (لِ) to strengthen the meaning of distance

steps.m: that (referring to their turning away mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to make it point to something far away (like pointing at something over there)
This is a negative particle that makes statements negative, like adding 'not' in English
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Changed the final alif to a long alif (آ) to show emphasis in pronunciation
This is the basic form of the demonstrative pronoun used to point to things or people far away
The alif with overbar (ٰ) extends the sound to emphasize distance
The hamza with kasra (ئِ) makes it specifically for plural

steps.m: those (far away, plural, being pointed to)

steps.m: to trust/believe in something or someone

Added ال at the start to show we're talking about a specific group of believers

steps.m: with the believers (connected to يُحَكِّمُونَكَ - they seek your judgment)

Adding أَ at the beginning and sukun on first root letter makes the verb causative - showing someone making something happen
Adding نَا at the end changes the subject to 'we' and shows this is connected to إِنَّآ (indeed we) at the start of the verse

steps.m: Torah (the divine scripture revealed to Moses)
steps.n: This is a borrowed word from Hebrew meaning the Torah

steps.m: the Torah (object of أَنزَلْنَا - We sent down)

steps.m: the Torah (object of أَنزَلْنَا - We sent down)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the verb أَنزَلْنَا (We sent down)

The ها suffix refers back to the Torah (التَّوْرَىٰةَ) mentioned earlier. It's feminine because التَّوْرَىٰةَ is a feminine word.

Added و with fatha at beginning to connect this word with هُدًى (guidance) that came before it
Added double damma to show this is indefinite (a light) and is in the same case as هُدًى that it's connected to

This is a preposition that shows how something is done
Added هَا which refers back to the Torah (التَّوْرَىٰةَ) mentioned earlier in the verse. This shows what is being judged with.

steps.m: to inform or bring important news

steps.n: Root letters joined with short 'a' sounds to make the word for 'news'

Added plural ending to show multiple prophets
Added 'the' (ال) at the start to make it specific prophets
Added a fatha mark at the end to show the word's role in the sentence
This is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence, used for plural masculine. The lam gets shadda due to sun letter assimilation.

Adds conjunction prefix و meaning 'and'

steps.n: Added plural ending وا to show multiple people are addressed. In this ayah, it's used with لا to mean 'do not exchange'

Adds preposition بِ to show how something is done

This is the basic interrogative/relative pronoun meaning 'what' or 'whatever'. In this ayah, it refers to what was entrusted from Allah's Book.

The alif is elongated to help with pronunciation at the end of the word. This word connects to ٱسْتُحْفِظُوا۟ (they were entrusted with) to mean 'with what they were entrusted with'.
These are the core letters that give us the basic idea of downward movement
Adding the vowel marks makes this a past tense verb about someone descending
Adding أَ at the beginning makes this about causing something else to descend, rather than descending yourself
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' or 'something worshipped'
Adding 'al' makes it refer to a specific deity rather than any deity
Adding damma shows this word is the subject of the sentence

Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does this action
Adds the masculine plural ending to show multiple disbelievers
Adds 'al' (ال) at the start to mean 'the', and uses a special long alif (ٰ) in the middle
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action instead of 'he'

steps.m: and we wrote down (about عَلَيْهِمْ what follows)

This is a preposition that shows something is on top of or over something else. The root letters combine to mean 'highness' or 'elevation'.

steps.m: upon (ready to connect to a pronoun)
steps.n: The ى changes to يْ to prepare for adding the pronoun. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: upon them (object of وَكَتَبْنَا - and We prescribed)
steps.n: Adding هِمْ shows who the action is happening to - in this case, the group that received what was prescribed.
This is a basic preposition meaning 'in' or 'within'

steps.m: in it/her (referring to the Torah mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added the feminine pronoun 'ha' to show what is being referred to

steps.m: in it/her (referring to the Torah mentioned earlier)

Adding shadda makes the word stronger, emphasizing what comes after it. Here it emphasizes that the law about 'the soul' is definite and important.
The meaning changed to 'self' because it's the precious, breathing part of a person
Basic noun pattern with fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter, turning the concept of breathing/being precious into the precious breathing essence - the soul/self
Added emphasis by doubling the ن with a shadda

steps.m: the soul, the self, the person (emphasized)
steps.n: Added ال at the beginning to make it specific

steps.m: the soul, the self, the person (as object of أَنَّ)

The meaning changed to 'self' because it's the precious, breathing part of a person
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to create the basic noun form
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific

steps.m: for/with the soul/self (object of قِصَاصٌ - retribution)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to show how/with what, and kasra at end to connect it to the word قِصَاصٌ (retribution). The hamza on the ال changed to a wasl form (ٱ) because it comes after another prefix.
The root carries meanings related to seeing and eyes, as well as the essence or reality of something
Root letters with fatha-sukoon vowels turn it from 'seeing' into the body part 'eye'
Adds the definite article to specify a particular eye
Adds 'and' to connect this with the previous word
Adds fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon in the sentence
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make a proper word
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific eye

Adding 'ال' makes it refer to a specific nose

steps.m: and the nose (object being equated with العين/the eye)

Added vowel marks to connect the letters together

Added the definite article al- to specify this particular nose being discussed

steps.m: with/by the nose (in retaliation for another nose)

Changes from root meaning 'to permit/hear' to the noun 'ear' by adding damma vowels
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific ear rather than any ear (the eye for the eye, the ear for the ear)
Adding و connects this with the previous parts about fair punishment (eye for eye)

steps.m: and the ear (object of equal retaliation)
steps.n: The fatha shows this ear is being mentioned as part of equal punishment - an ear for an ear
Adding damma marks changes this from a verb root into a noun meaning 'ear'
Added ال to show we're talking about a specific ear in the context of equal punishment - an ear for an ear
Added بِ to show this is describing equal punishment - an ear in exchange for an ear
Added a small line (kasra) under the last letter because it comes after the word بِ (bi) meaning 'with/for'
The shadda doubles the letter to strengthen the meaning
Added 'the' (ال) at the start. The ل blends into س making it stronger (shown by the ّ mark)
Added و at beginning to mean 'and', and fatha at end because it's the object of the comparison
The shadda doubles the letter to strengthen the meaning
The word gets 'the' (ال) at the start, which joins with the 's' sound to make it stronger
Adding kasra makes the word genitive case since it follows a preposition
Adding the preposition بِ shows this is part of the law of equal retaliation - a tooth for a tooth
The root letters were given vowels to make a noun meaning 'wound' instead of the action 'to wound'
Changes from one wound to many wounds by adding و and changing the vowels to ُ (u sound)
Adds 'and' to connect this word to previous words
Makes the word definite by adding 'the'

steps.m: and the wounds (as something being equated)
steps.n: Adds a mark to show this is part of a list of things being equated with each other in retribution
The word takes this form to show a system where one action is followed by an equal response. In this ayah, it explains that injuries (الجروح) must be matched with equal treatment.
The tanween (ٌ) shows this is talking about the general concept of equal retaliation, not a specific case

steps.m: whoever (subject of تَصَدَّقَ - gives in charity)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that introduces a conditional statement about anyone who might perform the action. It connects to the verb تَصَدَّقَ (gives in charity) that comes after it.

steps.m: so whoever (subject of تَصَدَّقَ - gives in charity)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence of what was mentioned before. It introduces what should happen after the previous rules about equal punishment were stated.

steps.n: Added tanween fatha to show this is the object of the verb لَجَعَلَكُمْ (He would have made you)
Changes from basic root to active participle form, meaning 'one'
Added ة to make it feminine since it describes أُمَّةً (community/nation) which is feminine
Changed to show this is describing 'one nation/community' (أُمَّةً), and wrote alif in a special way with a line on top
This is the basic particle that shows contrast between ideas. The alif above the ل (ٰ) helps with pronunciation.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this contrasting idea with the previous statement. In this ayah, it connects to the previous idea about Allah's ability to make everyone one community.
Added يَ at the beginning to show someone is doing the testing now or in the future, and added ُ (damma) after ل to complete the verb pattern
Added لِ at start to show this is happening for a purpose or reason

steps.m: so that he tests you all
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show who is being tested - 'you all' (when speaking to multiple people)
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. In this ayah, it's used with ما to mean "in what" referring to the matters/blessings Allah has given.
This is a relative pronoun that refers to things (not people). It connects to فِي (in) to mean "in what" or "in that which"
The alif is written with a special elongated form (آ) instead of regular alif (ا). This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.
Basic meaning of these three letters together
Added vowel marks to make past tense verb. The ي at the end becomes ى because it's at the end of a word.
Added a hamza with alif at the start to change the meaning from 'coming' to 'giving' - like bringing something to give it
Added small alif above the ى to show proper pronunciation
Added كُمْ at the end to show the gift was given to multiple people
The اِسْتَ prefix shows doing something with others or seeking to do it
The kasra (ِ) in the middle changes it to a command form
Added 'fa' at start to mean 'so/then'
Added plural ending to make it a command to many people

steps.m: to be good or better than something else

steps.n: Changed the ending to prepare for making it plural. The alif with a small line over it (ٰ) is used because it's going to become plural.
Adds feminine plural suffix to make 'good' into 'good things'

steps.m: the good deeds (that you should race towards)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific, and the kasra (ِ) shows this word follows فَٱسْتَبِقُوا۟ (so race towards)
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to the word اللَّهِ (Allah) to show that the return is 'to Allah'.
Changed from 'a god' to 'The God' by adding ال and merging letters. The word Allah is unique and refers only to the One True God.
Added kasra at the end to show possession - Allah owns or is connected to your return (مَرْجِعُكُمْ) mentioned in the next part of the verse

steps.m: place or time of return (related to إِلَى ٱللَّهِ - to Allah)
steps.n: Added مَ at the start and changed vowels to show where/when something happens. This word connects to إِلَى ٱللَّهِ (to Allah) in the ayah.

steps.m: place or time of return (as main subject)
steps.n: Added ُ at the end to show this is the main subject being talked about
Added كُمْ at the end to show this belongs to all the people being spoken to. This connects to the earlier part where Allah is talking to everyone about returning to Him.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (gathered) to a noun meaning 'all together' by adding ي in the middle

steps.m: all together (describing how something is done in إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ - 'to Allah is your return')
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this describes how the return to Allah happens - everyone returns together
The doubled letter ب shows this isn't just regular news-telling, but announcing or declaring important information
The يُ prefix shows this is happening now or will happen in the future

steps.m: and then he tells important news (now/future)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happens as a result of or following something else mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: and then He (Allah) will inform you all (about بِمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ - what you used to differ in)
steps.n: The كُم shows this is addressed to multiple people. From context, we know Allah is the subject and He's informing about their differences mentioned in the next part of the ayah
This is a relative pronoun that refers to something general or unknown

steps.m: with/by what (refers to what أَنزَلَ اللَّهُ - Allah has revealed)
steps.n: Added the preposition بِ at the beginning to show this is the tool or means by which something is done. In this ayah, it refers to judging by or with what Allah has revealed.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from past tense 'he was' to a command 'be!' by shortening the word and changing vowels
Added تُمْ to show this refers to a group being spoken to (you all)
Basic preposition meaning 'in' or 'within'

steps.m: in it, within it (referring to ٱلْكِتٰبِ - the Book)
steps.n: Added هِ (hi) at the end to show this means 'in it', referring back to the Book (ٱلْكِتٰبِ) mentioned earlier in the verse
Added letters show people differing from each other - it's a mutual action between people

steps.m: you (all) differ from each other (present tense)
steps.n: The ت at the beginning shows this is happening now and you all are doing it

steps.m: you (all) are differing from each other
steps.n: The ُ shows this is a basic statement (not a command or possibility)

steps.m: you (all) are differing/disagreeing with each other
steps.n: The ون ending shows this is about a group of people (2 or more)
This is a compound particle made up of و (and) + أن (that)

steps.m: to judge or make wise decisions
steps.n: This root is about making wise judgments or decisions
Changed the root into a command by adding marks (damma and sukun) - like saying 'you judge!'
Added alif at beginning to help pronunciation of the command form
Added fatha and sukun marks to make it a preposition showing location between things

steps.m: between them (referring to the people being judged)
steps.n: Added هُم to show this applies to multiple people being discussed. This word connects to احْكُم (judge) to show who should be judged between.
Added vowel mark to show pronunciation
Extended the alif to show it's a long vowel
Added بِ at the beginning to show 'with'. This word connects to أَنزَلَ (He sent down) to mean 'with what He sent down'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he caused to send down (subject of اللَّهُ)
steps.n: Added أَ at the start to show Allah made something happen rather than doing it directly
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added the U mark at the end to show it's in the nominative case
Added 'the' at the start to make it specific/definite
This is the basic negative particle that means 'not' or 'do not'. In this ayah, it's used with تَتَّبِعْ to say 'do not follow'.
Added و at the beginning to connect this negative command with what came before it. This connects to the verb تَتَّبِعْ to mean 'and do not follow'.
Adding vowels turns the root letters into a complete past tense verb
Added ت between first and second root letters and doubled middle letter to show intensive following
Changed from past tense 'he followed' to present tense 'you follow' by changing vowels and adding ُ at end

steps.m: you follow intensively/closely (as a command not to do it)
steps.n: Changed from 'you follow' to 'do not follow' by removing the last vowel - this happens after لا to make it a command not to do something
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Adding أ at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Changes singular 'desire' to plural form for multiple desires/whims
Adds هُمْ to show the desires belong to them (a group)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he was cautious' to a command meaning 'be cautious!' by adding alif at start and changing the ending
Added و to connect this command with previous phrase
Added هُمْ to show who to be cautious of
This is a connecting word that joins two parts of the sentence together. Here it connects to يَفْتِنُوكَ (they tempt you) to show what someone is worried about happening.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
The و indicates plural masculine subject (they). Requires damma (ُ) before it to connect properly.

steps.m: they test/tempt you (object of أَن in the warning)
steps.n: The ك suffix shows you are receiving the action. This comes after dropping the ن from ون. The whole phrase means 'beware that they tempt you away from...'
This is a preposition showing separation or distance. In this ayah, it connects to the verb يَفْتِنُوكَ (they tempt you away) to show what they are trying to tempt you away from.
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the basic noun
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it (their sins). This is part of the phrase 'with some of their sins'.
Added fatha to show proper pronunciation. This is a relative pronoun meaning 'what' or 'that which' that connects to the word أَنزَلَ (revealed) to mean 'what (Allah) revealed'.
The alif is elongated with a madda mark (آ) to show a longer vowel sound. This word is the object of أَنزَلَ (revealed), showing what Allah revealed.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أَ makes the verb causative - instead of something coming down by itself, someone (Allah in this case) is causing it to come down.
Added ال to specify THE one God, and merged the letters according to Arabic rules where ال + إ becomes الل

steps.m: Allah (subject of أَنزَلَ - He sent down)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل to emphasize the uniqueness, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added ُ to show this is the subject doing the action of sending down (أَنزَلَ) in this verse
Basic preposition meaning direction or destination
The ى changes to ي before attachments

steps.m: to you (object of أَنزَلَ - sent down)
steps.n: Added ك at the end to show this means 'to you'. This word shows who received what was 'sent down' (أَنزَلَ) in this verse.
This is a conditional particle that introduces a hypothetical situation. In this ayah, it introduces what should happen if people turn away.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. It connects the condition 'if they turn away' to what should happen next.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb. The ي changes to ى at the end because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: he turned away (by his own choice)
steps.n: Added تَ at beginning and doubled ل to show this is something done deliberately by choice

steps.m: they turned away (by their own choice)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people did this action
Command form made by adding alif at start and putting sukoon at end - tells one person to know/understand something
Added فَ at beginning to show this command follows from what was said before. The alif changes to hamzat wasl (ٱ) because it comes after فَ.
Basic emphatic particle that introduces a statement with certainty
Added ما to strengthen the emphasis. This compound introduces what Allah wants to do in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added alif at beginning to show causing or making something happen
Changed from past tense (he wanted) to present tense (he wants) by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowels
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Changed from the general word for 'a god' to the special name 'Allah' by adding ال and joining letters together

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of يُرِيدُ - He wants)
steps.n: Added ُ to show Allah is the one doing the action (wanting) in this verse

steps.m: that (connects to يَفْتِنُوكَ - they tempt you)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that joins two parts of the sentence. Here it connects 'be cautious of them' with what they might do - 'that they tempt you.' It works like saying 'Watch out that they don't trick you' in English.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing middle vowels
Added هُم at the end to show who is being affected, with َ connecting it to the word
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make a noun meaning 'a part' or 'some'. This word refers to 'some/part of what Allah has revealed'
Added بِ at the beginning to mean 'with/by' and added ِ at the end because it comes after a preposition
Changes from the root meaning 'sin' to plural form 'sins' by adding vowels (ُ) and و - like going from 'wrong' to 'wrongs'
Adds هِمْ to show the sins belong to them. This word is connected to the preposition بِ (with/by) in this ayah.
Basic emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Added connecting particle at beginning to link this statement with previous one
Added vowels to make it mean 'it became many' in the past
Changed the vowels and added ي to make an adjective meaning 'many'
Added ًا at the end to make it describe how something is done, like 'greatly' or 'abundantly'
Basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or belongs to something
Added shadda for emphasis and stronger pronunciation
Added fatha at the end to connect it to the next word (ٱلنَّاسِ) in a flowing way
Root means being social/friendly, transformed into the word for the social beings - people
Added 'al' (the) at the beginning which caused the n sound to be doubled

steps.m: of the people (possessed by/connected to مِّنَ)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to مِّنَ (from/of) in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the root letters together

steps.m: one who breaks away from obedience
steps.n: Changes verb into a noun describing someone who does this action

steps.m: those who break away from obedience
steps.n: Makes the word plural to show multiple people
Adds emphasis to show this is definitely true

steps.m: to judge or make wise decisions

steps.n: Changed from an action (judging) to the result of that action (a judgment)
Added فَ to show this follows from something before it

steps.m: Is it then the judgment? (object of يَبْغُونَ - they seek)
steps.n: Added أَ at start to make it a question and َ at end to show this is what's being sought. This word is what's being sought in the phrase 'they seek' (يَبْغُونَ)

steps.m: to be unaware or act without knowledge

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to describe someone who is characterized by ignorance
Added يّ to show something related to or characterized by ignorance
Added ة to make it a feminine noun meaning 'state/period of ignorance'
Added the definite article to specify this particular state of ignorance

steps.m: of the state of ignorance (possessed by حُكْمَ)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by حُكْمَ (judgment/rule) in the phrase حُكْمَ ٱلْجٰهِلِيَّةِ (judgment of ignorance)

steps.m: to seek or desire something wrongfully

steps.n: Root letters were connected with fathas to make it mean 'he wrongfully sought' in the past
Added يَ at start to show present tense, changed middle vowel to sukun, and final vowel to damma. The ي at the end disappeared because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: they seek wrongfully (referring to those who reject Allah's law in favor of man-made laws)
steps.n: Added ون to show many people doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to those who prefer human judgments over Allah's law.
This is a question word asking about people. In this ayah, it's asking 'who is better...'
Added و to connect this question with the previous phrase, linking 'Do they seek the judgment of ignorance?' with 'and who is better than Allah in judgment?'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added أَ at start and changed vowels to make a comparative form. In this ayah, it's comparing with Allah's judgment, showing that no judgment could be better than His.
This is a preposition that shows something coming from or originating from something else. Here it connects to Allah's name (ٱللَّهِ) to show something coming from Allah.
Changed the ending from نْ to نَ because it's followed by the word ٱللَّهِ which starts with ال. This makes it easier to pronounce.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns

This is the unique name for The One True God in Islam. The ال makes it definite and specific, while the shadda emphasizes its uniqueness. The kasra at the end shows it's being acted upon by مِنَ (from) in this ayah.

steps.m: to decide or rule on something

steps.n: Changed the word from an action (to judge) into a thing (judgment/decision) by adding ُ on first letter and ْ on middle letter

steps.m: a judgment (object being compared after أحسن/better)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this is the thing being compared when asking 'who has better judgment than Allah?'
Changed from action to noun - when people 'stand together' they form a community. This word is connected to يُوقِنُونَ (they are certain) in this ayah.
Added tanween (two kasras) to show this is any people/community, not a specific one
Added لِ at beginning to show this is 'for' a people
Added shadda on ل for emphasis and proper pronunciation

steps.m: to be completely certain without any doubt

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at start and و in middle to show ongoing certainty
Added ون at the end to show multiple people are doing the action
The root letters combine to make a question word that asks 'which one?' or 'who?'
Vocative particle added to call attention
Adds the nominative case ending (damma) to show this is the subject
Adds ها to show we're talking to multiple people
We start with the basic demonstrative word ذا and change it to make it connect with what comes after it
Added ن to make it refer to multiple people
Added ال at the beginning with a shadda on the following letter to point to specific people. This word connects to ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) to tell us which people are being talked about.
Changed the root letters to make Form IV verb (ءَامَنَ) which means 'to believe' rather than just 'feel secure'. The hamza with alif at start shows this change in meaning.

steps.m: they believed (referring to those addressed in يٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ - O you who)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did the action. This word refers back to 'those who' (ٱلَّذِينَ) mentioned at the start of the verse.
This is a negative particle that doesn't come from a root. It's used here to tell people not to do something - specifically not to take (تَتَّخِذُوا۟) certain groups as allies.
Basic meaning of taking or grasping something
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make basic past tense verb
The ت combines with following letter to show deliberate action
ت at start shows either 'she' or 'you' is doing the action
The ُ at the end shows this is a normal present tense verb

steps.m: you (all) take (as a command/prohibition with لا)
steps.n: Added وا to show plural 'you'. This is in a prohibition (لا تَتَّخِذُوا = do not take)
Added ي at beginning and vowels to form the collective noun for Jewish people
Added ال to make it specific

steps.m: the Jews (as object of لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because this word is the object of the verb لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ (do not take)
Basic meaning of helping or supporting
Added shadda and alif to show someone who does this action intensively
Changed from word meaning 'helper' to special plural form meaning 'Christians', with long alif (ٰ) in middle
Adding ال makes it specific/definite - 'the Christians' instead of just 'Christians'
Adding و connects this word to what came before it, meaning 'and'

steps.m: to be close to and protect
steps.n: Basic meaning of being close or in charge
Changes to past tense verb showing state of being close/protecting
Changed to broken plural form to show many protectors

steps.m: protectors, allies (as objects of لَا تَتَّخِذُوا - do not take)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because this word is being acted upon by the verb 'do not take'

steps.m: to separate or divide something into parts

steps.n: Root letters ب-ع-ض were joined together with vowels to make the noun 'some/part' - it tells us which portion of أَوْلِيَآءُ (allies) is being talked about

steps.m: some of them (referring to اليهود والنصارى - the Jews and Christians)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show we're talking about 'them' (referring back to the Jews and Christians mentioned earlier). The ُ before هُمْ shows this word is doing something in the sentence.

steps.m: to be close to and take care of
he was close to/took care of
steps.n: Adding vowels makes it a past tense verb showing someone took care of or was close to something
Changed the word to show many protectors instead of just one
Added ُ to show this word is the subject of بَعْضُهُمْ (some of them) in the ayah
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun. The fatha-sukun pattern is common for simple nouns.
The word gets a tanwin (double dots under) to show it connects to 'allies' - like saying 'some of the allies'
Combination of conjunction وَ (and) with مَن (who/whoever)

steps.m: he takes for himself as protector
steps.n: ت shows the action is done to/for oneself

steps.m: he takes them as protectors (referring to اليَهُودَ وَالنَّصٰرَى - the Jews and Christians)
steps.n: Added هُم meaning 'them' to show who is being taken as protectors. This refers back to the Jews and Christians mentioned earlier in the verse.
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added emphasis mark (shadda) on م because it comes after ا in the previous word (يَا)
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to
Starting with the base word that shows emphasis
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement
Added هُ at the end to show who is being talked about (referring back to whoever takes them as allies)
Added sukun to show end of preposition

steps.m: from them (referring to اليهود والنصارى - the Jews and Christians)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this applies to 'them' (referring back to the Jews and Christians mentioned earlier in the verse)

steps.m: indeed/verily (emphasizes that اللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى is true)
steps.n: Basic emphasis particle that makes what follows more certain. In this ayah, it emphasizes that 'Allah does not guide' is definitely true.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.
The word 'Allah' gets a shadda mark on the ل and an ending mark (fatha) because it comes after the word 'inna' (indeed)
This is a negative particle that makes the verb تَتَّخِذُوا۟ (take) into 'do not take'. It's one of the basic negative words in Arabic that can't be broken down into smaller parts.
Added fatha (َ) on the ل to show how it's pronounced. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb

steps.m: he guides (the subject of this verb is اللَّهَ - Allah)
steps.n: Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels - like changing 'guided' to 'guides'
The ى at the end shows this is an ongoing action (present tense)

steps.m: to stand upright, rise up, establish

steps.n: Changes verb root into a noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Added ال for 'the' and fatha at end to show this is the object receiving Allah's guidance in the ayah

steps.m: to do wrong or harm to others
someone who does wrong to others
steps.n: Changes the word from an action (doing wrong) to a person who does that action (wrongdoer). This word is being acted upon by the verb يهدي (guides) in this ayah.
Adds 'the' to make it specific. When ال is added before ظ, the ل combines with it making a stronger ظ sound (shown by ّ)
Adds ين at the end to show we're talking about many wrongdoers, not just one. The َ at the end shows this word is being acted upon by the verb يهدي (guides).
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of the word.
Added ت at beginning to show 'you' and changed vowels for present tense. The middle hamza (أ) drops out in present tense.

steps.m: and then you see (connected to يُسٰرِعُونَ - they hasten)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this seeing happens as a result of their hastening mentioned in the ayah
Starting with the basic demonstrative word and changing it to show connection
Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people or things
Adding ال makes it specific and definite, like adding 'the' in English

steps.m: those who (describing people with disease in their hearts)
steps.n: Adding the shadda emphasizes the letter, and the final fatha helps connect to the next word. The hamza at the start is a special form of alif.
Basic preposition showing location or containment. Here it connects 'hearts' (قُلُوب) with 'disease' (مَّرَضٌ) to mean 'disease in their hearts'.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha and sukun to make the singular noun 'heart'
This is a broken plural - instead of adding letters at the end, it changes the pattern of the word to show many hearts
The word 'hearts' gets a kasra (i) sound at the end because it belongs to something after it - like saying 'hearts of'
Adding هِم shows who the hearts belong to - in this case, it refers to those who have disease in their hearts (mentioned in the ayah)
Changes from verb 'to be sick' to the noun 'sickness'
Adding tanween (double damma) makes it 'a' sickness rather than 'the' sickness
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding ا changes it from just 'hurrying' to 'racing against others'
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they rush/race (subject of ترى - you see)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. The alif is written as ٰ in this form.
This is a basic preposition meaning 'in' - it has no root since it's a particle
Added هِمْ to show this refers to multiple people (them). This connects back to those mentioned earlier who are rushing/hastening.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The middle و changes to ا because it's between two fathas
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ونَ at the end to show multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: to fear or be afraid of something

steps.n: Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added ن at beginning to show 'we' and changed ending to show present tense. The ى replaces the ي because it's at the end of the word.
Added small alif (ٰ) above the ى to help with pronunciation
This is a primitive particle (حرف) that exists in this basic form without morphological derivation. It functions to connect clauses and introduce subordinate clauses.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation. This particle connects نَخْشَىٰٓ (we fear) with تُصِيبَنَا (it will afflict us), showing what they fear might happen.
The ُ shows this verb is in the basic present tense form
نَا means 'us' - it shows who is being affected by the action
Added نَا at the end to show who is being affected by the action
Added vowel marks to make the root letters 'to turn' into 'it turned' in the past
Changed from verb 'it turned' to noun meaning 'one who turns'

steps.m: a turn of events, change in fortune (something that turns)
steps.n: Added ة to make it a specific instance or event
Added tanween to show it's indefinite. In this ayah, it refers to a turn of fortune they fear might strike them.
This makes it into a common Quranic word meaning 'perhaps'

steps.m: so perhaps/maybe (connecting to previous statement about fearing misfortune)
steps.n: Added فَ to connect this hope with the previous statement about people fearing misfortune
Added shadda to emphasize the uniqueness and absoluteness of Allah

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of يَأْتِىَ - comes/brings)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show Allah is the doer of the action 'comes/brings' in this ayah. The alif at the start changed to a wasl form since it connects with the previous word.

This is a particle that connects two ideas, showing what someone fears might happen. Here it connects 'we fear' (نَخْشَىٰٓ) with what they fear (تُصِيبَنَا - will strike us). It works like saying 'We fear THAT something will happen' in English.
Connected the root letters together to form the base word
Added ي at beginning to show present tense. This comes after أَن (that) in the ayah.
Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation. The kasra (ِ) in the middle shows this is about a state or condition.
The verb gets a different ending (ى with fatha) because أَن comes before it
Changed the root into a noun that means the result of conquering
Added ال to make it specific - this refers to a particular victory Allah will bring
Added بِ to show this victory is the means/instrument, and added kasra at the end because it follows the word يَأْتِىَ (comes with) in the ayah

This is a connecting word that shows two possible things they are worried about: either being struck by a disaster OR Allah bringing victory. It connects تُصِيبَنَا (it strikes us) with يَأْتِىَ (He brings).
Changed from action to the thing itself - from commanding to a command/matter
Added tanween kasra to show this is referring to an indefinite matter or affair that Allah may bring. This connects to يَأْتِىَ (may bring/come) in the ayah.
Basic form of the preposition that shows where something starts or comes from

Added shadda and sukun. The shadda appears because this word comes before certain letters that cause the ن to be doubled in pronunciation. This word connects to عِندِهِۦ (from His presence) in this ayah.
Changed from an action (being near) to a word showing location (at/with/near)
Added kasra to show this word connects to the word after it

steps.m: from Him (referring to Allah in أَمْرٍ مِّنْ عِندِهِۦ - a matter from Him)
steps.n: Added هِۦ to show this refers to Allah. This is part of the phrase 'a matter from Him' where 'Him' refers back to Allah.
Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he became/entered morning'
Adds يُ at start to make it mean 'he becomes' (present tense) instead of 'he became' (past tense)
Added plural masculine ending to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: and then they become (following from يَقُولُونَ - they say)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happens as a result of the previous action (they say)
This is a preposition that shows something being on top of or over something else. In this ayah, it connects to what comes after it to show what people will regret.
Added the small alif (ٰ) at the end to show how to pronounce the word correctly when stopping at it
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to things or actions. In this ayah, it refers to what they concealed in themselves. The آ is a spelling variation of ا.

steps.m: to hide or keep as a secret

steps.n: The middle letter is doubled (has shadda) to show doing something with extra strength or effort
Adding أ at the start shows making someone else do the action
Adding وا at the end changes it from 'he' to 'they' and makes it mean 'they hid' instead of 'he made hide'
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. In this ayah, it shows that something (مَّرَضٌ 'disease') is located inside their hearts.
The elongation mark (ٓ) is added to help with pronunciation when stopping at this word. It doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to be precious, valuable, or desired

steps.n: Root letters got vowels to make the word mean 'soul' or 'self' - belongs to 'them' (هِم)
Changed to broken plural form by adding أ at start and changing vowels - this is a common plural pattern for words meaning 'self/soul'
Added هِمْ at the end to show it belongs to them

steps.m: someone who feels sorry (predicate of يُصْبِحُوا/they become)
steps.n: This form shows who is doing the feeling sorry. It's connected to the word يُصْبِحُوا which means 'they become' in this verse.

steps.m: people who feel sorry (predicate of يُصْبِحُوا/they become)
steps.n: The ين ending shows this is talking about multiple men and connects to the word يُصْبِحُوا (they become)
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowels. The و stays because it's part of the root.
Added وَ at the start to connect this word to what came before it. This word is followed by ٱلَّذِينَ (those who) in the ayah.
This is a special demonstrative word used to point to a group of people or things that were mentioned before
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changed from basic 'trust' to stronger 'believe' by adding hamza at start - makes the action more intense and direct
Added the plural marker to show multiple people did the action. This word is connected to يَقُولُ (says) in the ayah, showing who is speaking.
This is a basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to multiple people or things that are near. It's like saying 'these' in English.

steps.m: Are these the ones? (subject of يَقُولُ)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning to turn the statement into a question, like adding 'Are...?' in English. This word is part of what is being said (يَقُولُ - he says) in the verse.

Added ال with a shadda to make it more specific
Added ن at the end to show we're talking about multiple people. These are the same people who will swear by Allah in the next part of the verse.
Added fatha vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Adding أ at start makes it mean 'to make/do an oath' rather than just 'swear'
Added وا at the end to show that a group of people did this action
This is the unique proper name of God in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning of divinity but is treated as a proper noun.

steps.m: by/with Allah (object of أَقْسَمُوا۟ - they swore)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show this is what they swore by, and ِ at the end to connect it with the rest of the sentence. The ٱ is just a different way of writing ا.

steps.m: to strive or exert maximum effort

steps.n: Changed verb into noun meaning the maximum amount of something
Added fatha at end to show this word describes 'their oaths' in the phrase 'they swore their strongest oaths'

steps.m: to swear or take an oath

Added kasra to prepare for pronoun attachment

Added كُمْ at the end to show this means 'with you all' (speaking to a group)

steps.m: and so they became (subject of خٰسِرِينَ)

steps.n: Adding the kasra changes it from a verb to a noun meaning

Added ون to show many people are doing the action
Connected the letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun showing the action itself

The ٍ at the end makes it mean 'a blamer' (not specific). The آ is just a fancy way to write اَ - both are pronounced the same way.
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby

This is a question word that introduces a conditional clause. In this ayah, it's asking about who might turn away from their religion. It connects to the verb يَرْتَدَّ (turns away/apostatizes) that comes right after it.

steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root

steps.n: Added ال to point to specific people who overcome - in this case, it's referring to Allah's group (حِزْبَ ٱللَّهِ) mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: to point to or draw attention to something

steps.n: The root letters أيي were joined together and the middle ي got doubled (shadda) to make the question word 'which/who'
Added يا (ya) at the start to make it a call/address to someone, like saying 'Hey you!'
Added ها to make it specifically address a group, like saying 'O you all!'
Starting with the basic word ذا (this) and changing it to ذي which is used for connecting descriptions to things or people
Adding ال makes it specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'

steps.m: those who (referring to the disbelievers mentioned in the ayah)
steps.n: Adding ين makes it refer to many people instead of just one person

steps.m: those who (referring to the disbelievers mentioned in the ayah)
steps.n: Adding the proper pronunciation marks and changing ال to ٱ for proper Quranic spelling
Adding alif makes it mean 'to believe' instead of just 'to feel safe'

steps.m: they believed (referring to the believers being addressed in يٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ)
steps.n: Added وا with damma to show multiple people did this action. The small mark after وا (۟) is just to help with pronunciation.
This is a negative particle that tells us not to do something. Here it's telling us not to do the action in تَتَّخِذُوا۟ (take/adopt).
Basic meaning of taking or grasping something
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Added اِتّ at the start to show he took something specifically for his own benefit/use
Changed to present tense with تَ prefix for 'you'
Added plural ending to show multiple people are addressed
Starting with the basic word ذا (this) and changing it to ذي which can mean 'who'
Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'
Adding ين at the end makes it plural, changing from 'the one who' to 'those who'
Adding the proper vowel marks and changing the alif to wasl form (ٱ) for correct pronunciation
Basic meaning of taking or grasping something
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added ت and merged it with first letter to mean 'took for himself/adopted' instead of just 'took'
Changed from singular 'he' to plural 'they' by adding وا
Added ا at start to help pronounce word beginning with two consonants
Connected the letters together to make a basic word
The kasra (ِ) under the د makes this specifically mean a complete system of beliefs and practices. This word is what's being taken/adopted in the phrase 'do not take'.
Added fatha (َ) because it's the object (thing being taken) in the sentence
Added كُمْ to show this religion belongs to all of you (the believers being addressed)

steps.m: to mock or make fun of something
steps.n: Basic meaning of making fun of something
Adding the ُ marks changes it from a verb (to mock) into a noun (mockery)
Added و and tanween alif to show this is the object being acted upon in the sentence. This word is what some people are taking your religion as.

steps.m: to play or treat something as not serious
steps.n: Basic meaning of playing or having fun

steps.m: he played/treated something as not serious
steps.n: Adding vowels makes it a past tense verb meaning 'he played'
Changed from a verb (he played) to a noun (amusement) by adding tanween. Shows what religion is being taken as.

steps.m: and as amusement (object of تَتَّخِذُوا along with هُزُوًا)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this with هُزُوًا (mockery) - both describing how some people wrongly treat religion
This is a preposition showing something comes from somewhere or someone. The kasra and fatha are essential parts of the preposition.
The shadda emphasizes the م sound, which happens because this word follows a word ending in a similar sound.
The fatha at the end connects this word to the next word الَّذِينَ (those who), showing that we're talking about 'from those who'.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun used to refer to one person or thing
Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people or things
Adding ال makes it point to a specific group. The ل gets a shadda (doubled) after ال

steps.m: those who (specific group being described) (describes أُوتُوا۟)
steps.n: The ا in ال becomes ٱ (waṣla) because it connects with the previous word. Adding َ (fatha) at the end shows this describes the group who were 'given' (أُوتُوا۟) in this verse
Changed to passive form to show receiving instead of bringing
The و appears between first and second letters due to passive voice with weak middle letter

steps.m: they were given (referring to الَّذِينَ - those who)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show multiple people received the action. This refers back to الَّذِينَ (those who) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'something written' by adding كِ at start and ا in middle

steps.m: the book (object of أُوتُوا - were given)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it specific ('the book'), and َ at the end shows it's being acted upon - in this case, it's what was given to them
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or starts. Here it connects to قَبْلِكُمْ (before you) to show 'from before you'
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun meaning 'before'
Added kasra to show this word connects to what comes after it
Added كُمْ to show this refers to multiple people being addressed
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he disbelieved' to 'people who disbelieve a lot' by making it plural and adding emphasis
The word is being acted upon (someone is doing something to the disbelievers)

steps.m: the specific disbelievers (referring to those mentioned in the previous ayah who mock religion)
steps.n: The ال makes it refer specifically to the previously mentioned disbelievers who mock religion
Adds conjunction meaning 'and'
These root letters show the basic idea of being close to someone to protect them
Added vowels and doubled the last letter to make it mean someone who protects
Changed from single protector/friend to many by making it plural (like changing 'friend' to 'friends')
Added fatha at end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb تَتَّخِذُوا (do not take)
Changed to command form for one person
Added وا to make it a command for many people
Added وَ at beginning to connect it to previous phrase
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of وَٱتَّقُوا۟ - fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: Added the fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being acted upon. Also added the shadda to emphasize the L sound and changed the initial alif to a joining alif. This word is what we should fear/be mindful of according to the command وَٱتَّقُوا۟ in this ayah.
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects with كُنتُم (you were) to mean 'if you were', showing that what comes after depends on being true believers.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he was' to 'you were' by adding ت at the end. The middle letter و drops out when we add ت - this is a common pattern with this type of word.
Added م to make it plural 'you all'. The ُ (damma) is added before م as required for this pronoun form.

steps.m: to be safe, to trust, to believe
steps.n: Connected the root letters together
Added مُ at start and changed middle vowels to make it mean 'someone who believes'
Added ين at the end to show this refers to many male believers
Added shadda on م and fatha at end to show this word is affected by something else in the sentence
This is a time word that shows when something happens. In this ayah, it introduces the time when the prayer call happens.

steps.m: and when (followed by نَادَيْتُمْ - you call)
steps.n: The و at the start connects this with what came before it, showing this is part of a sequence of ideas
This is the basic meaning of calling or announcing something
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to show extended action
Added ي with sukoon to show this is in jussive mood

steps.m: you all called out (إِلَى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ - to prayer)
steps.n: Added تُمْ at the end to show that 'you all' did the action. This word connects to 'إِلَى ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ' which tells us they called out to prayer.
This is a preposition that shows direction or movement towards something. In this ayah, it connects to the word ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ (prayer), showing that the call is directed towards the prayer.
This root is about the act of prayer and worship
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed the weak letter و to ة and added alif to create a noun for a single instance of prayer
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ص with shadda because it comes after ال
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word إِلَى (to). The alif is written as a small alif above the letter.
This is about taking or grabbing something
Changed to Form VIII pattern which shows doing something for oneself
Added و to show multiple people doing the action
Added ا to complete the plural marker

steps.m: they took it (referring to ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ) for themselves
steps.n: Added هَا to show what they took (referring back to the prayer mentioned earlier in the verse)

steps.m: to mock or make fun of something
steps.n: This is about making fun of something
Changes from verb 'to mock' into noun meaning 'mockery' by adding damma vowels
Added tanween alif to show this word is the object (what is being taken/used) in relation to the verb ٱتَّخَذُوهَا (they took it)
This is about playing or having fun
Changed from basic root meaning 'play' into a noun meaning 'play/mockery' by adding the vowels a-i (fatha-kasra)
Added tanween alif to show this is the second object of 'they took it' (ٱتَّخَذُوهَا)
Added و to connect it with هُزُوًا (mockery), showing these are both objects of 'they took it'
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this' for something close by
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show this points to something farther away
Adding ل with kasra (ِ) changes it from pointing to something close to pointing to something far

steps.m: that (referring to how they mock prayer)
steps.n: The ك at the end makes this word connect back to what was just mentioned - in this case, how they mock the call to prayer
The doubled ن (with shadda) makes the meaning stronger and more certain
The word أَنَّ (that/because) got هُمْ (they) attached to the end to mean 'that they' - like adding 'they' to make it about a group

steps.m: because they (explaining why they took it in mockery)
steps.n: The بِ at the start shows this is explaining why something happened
This root is about standing or rising
Changes from verb root to noun meaning a group of people who stand together
Added tanween (double damma) to show this is the subject who 'do not understand' in the verse
This is the basic negative particle that means 'no' or 'not'
Added emphasis by adding shadda. This makes the negation stronger and connects it to the verb يَعْقِلُونَ (they understand) that comes after it, showing they definitely do not understand.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense form
Changes from past tense (he understood) to present tense (he understands) by adding يَ at the start

steps.m: they understand (referring to those who mock prayer)
steps.n: The ون ending shows this is about multiple people. In this ayah, it refers to those who mock the call to prayer, showing they lack understanding.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense form
Changed to a command form by: 1) dropping the middle weak letter و, 2) adding damma on first letter, 3) adding sukun on last letter. This is telling one person to speak.

steps.m: to settle, dwell, belong to family

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make it a noun meaning the people who belong together
Added يٰٓ at the beginning to show we're calling out to these people, like saying 'Hey people!' The َ at the end shows this word is being directly addressed.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (wrote) to a noun (book/writing) by adding alif
Added ٱلْ to make it definite ('the book') and ِ to show it's possessed by أَهْلَ (people) in the phrase أَهْلَ ٱلْكِتٰبِ (People of the Book)
This is a question word that starts a yes/no question. Here it's asking if the People of the Book do something.

steps.m: to resent or find fault with something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to create the past tense form

steps.m: you resent (addressing the People of the Book)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding تَ prefix and changing vowels. This addresses the People of the Book who are being questioned in this verse.

steps.m: you all resent (addressing multiple People of the Book)
steps.n: Added ون to show this is addressing multiple people. In this verse, it's asking the People of the Book what they resent about the believers.
This is a preposition meaning 'from'
Added 'us' by combining with نا. The ن at the end of مِنْ combines with the ن of نا to form a shadda
The alif is written in its long form (آ) according to Quranic writing style. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is a basic exception word that shows something or someone is not included in what was mentioned before. It's like saying 'but not' or 'except for' in English.

steps.m: except (connected to أَنْ ءَامَنَّا - that we believed)

This is a connecting word that introduces what comes after it. Here it connects to the word ءَامَنَّا (we believed) to show what people are criticizing - that we believed in Allah.
Added alif after first letter and fatha marks to form the past tense verb meaning 'believed'
The doubled letter at the end makes the belief stronger/more emphatic
Added نا at the end to show 'we' are doing the believing. This word shows what is being resented in the phrase 'do you resent us'
This is the base word - the proper name of God in Arabic. The shadda shows emphasis on the ل.
The small diagonal line (ِ) under the last letter shows this means 'of Allah' or 'to Allah', like saying Allah's or to Allah
The بِ at the beginning shows how something is done or what it's done with. The ٱ is just a different way of writing ال.
The basic word 'ma' means 'what' or 'that which'. The alif is elongated (آ instead of ا) for emphasis in pronunciation.

steps.m: and what/that which (connects to أُنزِلَ - was sent down)

Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to passive form to show the action was done to something rather than by something
Basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'towards'
Added sukun before adding pronoun - this helps connect the words smoothly
Added نَا which means 'us' - this shows who the action is going to

steps.m: what (connects to أُنزِلَ - was sent down)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that links to what follows. Here it connects to the verb أُنزِلَ (was sent down) to mean 'what was sent down'

steps.m: and what (connects to أُنزِلَ - was sent down)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it

steps.n: The alif is lengthened at the end for emphasis in recitation, but doesn't change the meaning

Changed vowels to show this happened to something rather than something doing it
This is a preposition showing where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects to تَنقِمُونَ (you resent/criticize) to show what they are resenting from.
This root relates to being in front or before something
Adding vowels to make it a usable noun meaning 'before'
Adding damma at the end shows this word is in the nominative case
This is a special word that introduces emphasis or a new clause. It makes what comes after it more certain or important.

steps.m: and that/and indeed (connects to ءَامَنَّا - we believed)
steps.n: The و connects this clause to what came before it, showing another reason in the list after ءَامَنَّا (we believed)
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: most of you (referring to the People of the Book being addressed)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show this refers to the group being spoken to (the People of the Book mentioned earlier in the verse)

steps.m: to break rules or leave the right path
steps.n: This root relates to breaking divine law or leaving the right path

Adding ون makes it plural to show we're talking about many people. The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) but pronounced the same.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it a command form. The ُ on ق and ْ on ل show it's a command.

steps.n: This is a question word that changes a statement into a yes/no question. Here it introduces the Prophet's question to his audience.
Added sukun (ْ) at the end to show where to stop when reading

The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes the meaning more intense
Added أُ at the beginning to show 'I' am doing the action

steps.m: I inform you all (in response to قُلْ هَلْ)
steps.n: Added كُم at the end to show who is being informed. This word follows 'Say: Shall' (قُلْ هَلْ) in the verse.

steps.m: with a very evil thing (something worse than مَثُوبَةً)

This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or is part of something else. It's like saying 'from' in English.
The shadda (ّ) is added to show this word connects strongly to the word before it. In this ayah, it's comparing something to show it's worse than what was mentioned before.
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this' for something close by
The alif is written with a special mark (dagger alif) above the ذ
Adding ل with kasra (لِ) changes it from pointing to something close ('this') to pointing to something far away ('that')
Adding كَ shows you are talking to someone while pointing to that thing

steps.m: to return back or come back

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and make it a past tense verb
Added مَ at start to show the result of returning (like a reward being returned for good deeds), changed middle vowel to ُ, and added ة at end to make it a specific instance

steps.m: a reward/recompense (object of أُنَبِّئُكُم - I inform you)

This word is used to show location or possession, like saying something is 'at' or 'with' someone
Adding vowel marks creates a preposition meaning 'with/at/by'. In this ayah, it shows something is 'with/at' Allah.
Added emphasis by doubling the ل
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word

steps.m: to curse or drive away from mercy

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. Allah (اللَّهُ) is doing this action.

steps.m: he surely cursed him (done by اللَّهُ)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who received the curse. This connects to the person being talked about earlier in the verse.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added the U mark at the end to show it's in the nominative case
Added 'the' at the start to make it specific
Added vowels to show this is about feeling anger. The َ ِ َ pattern often shows emotions and feelings.

steps.m: and he became angry (connected to لَّعَنَهُ - cursed him)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous action (cursing) in the verse
This is the basic preposition that shows something is on top of or over something else
The ى changes to يْ when we're going to add something to the end
Added هِ at the end to show who is being acted upon (him)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb. This shows a completed action.

steps.m: and he made/set/placed (connected to previous statements about Allah's actions)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this action with the previous statements about what Allah did to those He was angry with
Basic preposition indicating source or origin
Added هُم to show this applies to a group (them)
Changed the final vowel to ُ because it's followed by the word الْقِرَدَةَ (the apes) in the ayah
Added vowel marks to form the singular noun for monkey

steps.m: the monkeys (object being made from them)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it definite and َ to show it's the object being made from them in this verse
Changes basic concept into proper noun form for 'pig'
Changed the word pattern by adding alif after first letter and changing middle vowels to make it plural - this is a common way to make plurals in Arabic
Added the definite article ال to make it specific
Added و at beginning to connect with previous phrase
Added fatha at end to show this is the object of the verb جَعَلَ (he made)

steps.m: he worshipped/served (connected to مَن in this ayah)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb. This word refers back to the person (مَن) mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: and he worshipped/served (connected to مَن in this ayah)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this action with the previous actions (being cursed and earning anger) in the verse
Added ا after first letter and vowels to show someone who does the action regularly
Added و and ت to create a noun meaning something that strongly embodies transgression, used specifically for false objects of worship
Added 'the' (ال) which joins with ط to make it stronger (with shadda)

steps.m: the false deity (object of وَعَبَدَ - and worshipped)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being worshipped in the phrase وَعَبَدَ ٱلطَّاغُوتَ (and worshipped false deities)
This is the base form of the demonstrative pronoun used to point to things far away
The alif is elongated with a special mark (ٰٓ) to add emphasis

steps.m: those (pointing to the previously mentioned people)
steps.n: Adding ئِكَ at the end makes it specifically point to something mentioned before - in this case, the people Allah cursed
Added vowel marks (fatha) to the root letters to form a noun meaning 'harm'
Adding shadda (doubling) on ر makes the meaning stronger and more intense

steps.m: a very evil thing/person (subject of مَثُوبَةً)
steps.n: Adding the double damma (tanween) shows this word is describing something specific - it means 'a very evil one' instead of just 'evil' in general

steps.m: a place (as object of شَرٌّ - worse)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is receiving the action of being 'worse' in position/rank

steps.m: to stray from the right path

steps.n: Changed from basic root to past tense verb by adding vowels and doubling middle letter
Added أَ at beginning and changed vowels to make it comparative
Added وَ to connect this word to what came before it

steps.m: away from (shows moving away from سَوَآءِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ - the right path)
steps.n: This is a preposition showing movement or turning away from something. In this ayah, it's used to describe those who have strayed away from the right path.

steps.m: to be level, equal, or straight

steps.n: Adding fathas makes it a past tense verb meaning 'he made equal'
Changed from a verb (doing something) to a noun meaning 'equal' by adding hamza at the end
The short 'a' sound is stretched longer to add emphasis, like saying 'saaame' instead of 'same'

steps.m: equal, alike, same (describing السَّبِيلِ - the path)
steps.n: Added kasra to show this word describes 'the path' (السَّبِيلِ) in the ayah

steps.m: to make a way or path

steps.n: Adding ي and vowels changes it from a verb into a noun meaning 'path'
Added definite article ال which causes the س to get a shadda
Added kasra at the end because this word follows عَن (away from) in the phrase عَن سَوَآءِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (away from the equal/straight path)

steps.m: when (introduces the condition of جَآءُوكُمْ - they came to you)

steps.m: and when (connects this condition to previous statements)
steps.n: The و connects this new condition with previous statements. It shows this is another example being added to what was mentioned before
Changed middle letter ي to ا and added vowels to make past tense 'he came'
Added alif with madda (آ) to show long vowel sound
Added و and ُ to show multiple people doing the action
Added كُمْ to show who they came to (you all)
Adding vowel marks to make basic past tense verb
The middle letter و changed to ا, and وا was added at the end to make it 'they said' instead of 'he said'
Added pronunciation marks that don't change the meaning
Adding alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Added shadda to emphasize the action

steps.m: we believed (what we said to قَالُوا)
steps.n: Added نا at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. This is what was said in the statement introduced by قَالُوا (they said).
This is a particle that emphasizes the verb دَّخَلُوا۟ (they entered) that follows it, showing the action was definitely completed
و connects this phrase to the previous statement قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا (they said 'we believe'), showing contrast between what they said and what they actually did
Basic meaning of entering or going into something
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make past tense
Added وا to show multiple people did the action
Added shadda because this word comes after another word ending in the same letter sound. The small circle is just a pronunciation mark.

steps.m: to reject, to cover up, to be ungrateful

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it a noun meaning the concept itself
Added ال to make it specific
Added بِ meaning 'with' and kasra at the end because it follows the preposition بِ
Independent pronoun meaning 'they' for a group of males. This pronoun is the subject of the verb خَرَجُوا (went out) in this ayah.

steps.m: and they (subject of خَرَجُوا - went out)
steps.n: و adds 'and' to connect this clause with what came before, showing these actions happened in sequence

This is a particle that adds emphasis and shows the action دَّخَلُوا۟ (they entered) definitely happened in the past. It's like saying 'they have definitely entered' or 'they already entered' in English.
