Added the elongated ى and ٓ marks to show proper pronunciation

steps.m: Israel (the prophet Jacob's other name, meaning 'one who struggled with God')
steps.n: This is a proper name that comes from Hebrew. It was given to Prophet Jacob (يعقوب) after he wrestled with an angel. It combines two parts: يسرا (struggled with) + ايل (God).
Added alif at beginning to show causative meaning. The sukun (ْ) is added on س because of the alif prefix.
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' as the subject doing the sending
Added و at beginning to connect this action with the previous statement in the verse
This is the basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'towards'
The ى changes to يْ before adding a pronoun
Added هِمْ at the end to show 'them'. This word connects to رُسُلًا (messengers) in the ayah, showing who the messengers were sent to.

steps.m: to send or be a messenger

steps.n: Changed the vowels to make a plural form showing multiple messengers
Added ًا to show this word is receiving the action of sending (we sent messengers)
Added damma and shadda to make a word meaning 'all' or 'every'
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by something else

steps.m: whenever (connects to جَآءَهُمْ - came to them)
steps.n: Adding ما changes the meaning to 'whenever' - this connects to the verb جَآءَهُمْ (came to them) to show 'whenever came to them'
Basic past tense verb pattern with fatha marks
Added هُمْ at the end to show who was affected by the action (them). This word connects to رُسُلًا (messengers) mentioned earlier in the verse.
The و makes it mean someone who does this action as their main role
Added tanween to show this is any messenger, not a specific one
This is the basic relative pronoun meaning 'what'
Added the preposition بِ (with) to show how something is done. In this ayah, it connects to the verb تَهْوَىٰٓ (desires), showing what their souls don't desire.
This is a negative particle that negates the verb تَهْوَىٰٓ (desire) that comes after it, changing its meaning to 'do not desire'
The root letters were connected by adding fathas (small a sounds). The ي at the end became ى to show past tense 'he desired'
Added ت at start to show present tense for 'she'. Changed first vowel to sukun to make it easier to pronounce.
Added alif maksura (ى with two dots) to show this is the correct spelling for this verb type
Added a small alif (ٓ) above to show this is a complete word ending
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and form a basic noun
Changes from single soul to plural souls by adding أَ at start and changing vowels
The suffix هم shows possession - 'their'. This word is the subject of the verb لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ (does not desire) in this ayah.

steps.m: to split or divide into distinct parts

steps.n: The ي makes it mean 'a distinct group' rather than just the act of separating

steps.m: a group (as the one being killed)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this group is receiving the action of killing
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
The doubled middle letter shows that the denial was intense or repeated
Added the plural ending to show many people did this action

steps.m: a distinct group (that has been split off)
steps.n: Creates a noun meaning 'a group that has been separated from others'. The ي shows this is a strong or permanent characteristic.

steps.m: a group (object of كَذَّبُوا - they denied)
steps.n: The ًا shows this group is being acted upon - they are the ones being denied in the phrase 'they denied a group'

steps.m: and a group (object of كَذَّبُوا - they denied)
steps.n: Connects this group to something mentioned before - in this case, connecting to another group that was also denied
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
The ي at the start changes it from past (he killed) to present (he kills)

steps.m: they kill/slay (referring to some of the Children of Israel who killed prophets)
steps.n: The ونَ ending shows multiple people are doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to some of the Children of Israel who rejected and killed prophets that were sent to them.
Added vowel marks to make the basic past tense form. This pattern is often used for verbs about thinking and feeling.
Added وا at the end to show that a group of people (they) did the action
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement, plus the elongation mark (ـٓ) and small و (۟) which help with proper recitation
Combined the two separate words أَنْ (that) and لَا (not) together
When نْ meets ل, the نْ changes into another ل with a shadda (ّ). This is a common pronunciation rule in Arabic to make words flow better.
Added fathas to connect the root letters together, making the basic past tense form
Added تَ at start to make it 'she/it' in present tense, and changed middle vowels to show ongoing action
Changed the ending because it comes after the word أَلَّا (that not) which affects how this word ends
Changed vowels and added ة to make it a noun meaning one specific instance of testing

steps.m: a trial/test (subject of تَكُونَ - to be)
steps.n: Added tanween (ٌ) to show this is the subject (doer) of 'to be'. It means any general trial/test, not a specific one
Added vowels to make the basic past tense form. The ي at the end is a weak letter that will change in later forms.
Changed to plural form by replacing the weak letter ي with و and adding ا. This shows many people did the action.

steps.m: so they became blind/lost understanding (after وَحَسِبُوٓا۟)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened as a result of what came before in the verse (their thinking there would be no trial)
Added shadda (doubling mark) on middle letter to show intensity of the deafness
Changed from one person to many people by adding وا at the end

steps.m: and they became very deaf (connected to عَمُوا۟ - they became blind)
steps.n: Added و with fatha (وَ) at beginning to connect this word with the previous word عَمُوا۟ (they became blind), showing these actions happened together

steps.m: then (connecting عَمُوا۟ to what came before)
steps.n: This word connects two events in sequence, showing that one happened after the other. In this ayah, it connects their becoming blind (عَمُوا۟) to what happened before.

steps.m: to return or turn back (especially from wrong to right)

steps.n: Added vowels (a) to make the basic 'he' past tense form, meaning he returned/repented
Changed to hollow verb form by adding alif, which is common for verbs with middle weak letter و
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
The word for 'god' becomes the name 'Allah' by adding ال (the), doubling the ل with a shadda mark, and adding ُ at the end to show it's connected to the word 'All-Seeing' in this ayah
This is a basic preposition showing something is on top of or upon something else
The ى changes to يْ to prepare for connecting to the next part
Added هِمْ which means 'them' to show who is being acted upon
Basic particle showing sequence of events

steps.m: then, after that (connects تَابَ with previous actions)
steps.n: Added damma and shadda with fatha to complete the word. This particle connects the previous actions (becoming blind and deaf) with Allah's later acceptance of their repentance (تَابَ).

steps.m: to lose sight or become blind

steps.n: Added vowels to make the basic past tense form showing a change in condition
Changed to plural form to show multiple people became blind

steps.m: he became completely deaf/stopped listening entirely
steps.n: Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more intense
Added plural ending وا to show multiple people
Added و with fatha at beginning to connect this action with the previous one
Root letters got vowels and ي added to make an adjective meaning 'a lot' or 'many'

steps.m: many (describing those who became blind and deaf)
steps.n: Added tanween damma (ٌ) to show this is describing a group mentioned in the ayah
This is a preposition that shows something coming from somewhere or being part of something
The letter م has a shadda because it merged with the ن of مِنْ, which is a common change when certain letters come after ن

steps.m: from/of/among them (many of the people mentioned who became blind and deaf)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show this refers to the group mentioned in the ayah who became blind and deaf
This is a unique word that is the proper name of God in Arabic. It likely comes from combining ال (the) with إله (deity), but is now treated as a single inseparable word.
Added shadda on ل to show it is doubled in pronunciation
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence. Here, Allah is described as being بَصِيرٌ (All-Seeing).

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of بَصِيرٌ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this statement to what came before. Also added helping alif (ٱ) which is common before ل.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Forms an intensive adjective showing Allah's perfect and complete ability to see and know all things
This is a relative pronoun that refers to something general or unknown
Added the preposition بِ which shows how something is done. Here it connects to the verb يَعْمَلُونَ (they do) to show Allah sees what they are doing.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form past tense verb
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changes from singular to plural by adding ونَ. This word follows بِمَا (with what) in the ayah.
Adds final fatha to complete the word
Added sukun at the end to show this is a complete particle. قَدْ is used to emphasize that something definitely happened.
Added لَ at the beginning to make the emphasis even stronger. This combination is often used at the start of important statements. Here it introduces the verb كَفَرَ (they disbelieved).

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word describes the people who 'said' (قَالُوٓا۟) something about Allah.
Base form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Added ن at the end to make it refer to multiple people
Added ال at the beginning and shadda on ذ to make it specific. This word is the subject of كَفَرَ (disbelieved) in this ayah.
Changed the alif to wasl form (ٱ) because it comes after another word
Connected the letters and changed و to ا because it's between two fathas. This makes it easier to say.
Added وا at the end to show many people did the action, with ُ before it to connect smoothly
Added small marks (ٓ and ۟) that help with proper Quran recitation but don't change the meaning

Adding the shadda (ّ) changes this from a conditional word to an emphasis word. It makes what comes after it stronger, like adding an exclamation mark in English. Here it emphasizes the word ٱللَّهَ (Allah).
Changed إِلَه to اللَه by adding ال and merging letters. This special word refers only to The One True God.
Added shadda on ل to show emphasis, fatha at end to show this is the object being talked about, and changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word

steps.m: He (refers to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a personal pronoun used to refer to Allah in this context. It connects to the previous words where some people claimed 'Indeed, Allah...' (إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ), and this pronoun هُوَ refers back to Allah.

steps.m: to anoint, to wipe clean, to stroke gently

steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters and vowels to create a word describing someone affected by anointing
Adding ال makes it a specific title rather than a general description

steps.m: the Messiah (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: The ُ ending shows this word is doing the action of saying in this verse
The root letters combine to form a noun meaning 'son' - someone who comes from (is built from) their parents
Added a helper alif at the start to make it easier to pronounce the word
The ُ shows this word is the subject. Here it's part of the phrase 'son of Mary' (ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ)
This is a proper noun (name) that comes from Hebrew/Aramaic 'Maryam', not from an Arabic root. In this ayah, it's part of the phrase 'son of Mary' (ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ) describing Jesus.

steps.m: he said (introduces what المَسِيحُ says next)
steps.n: Added fatha marks to show this happened in the past. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two vowels - this is a common change in words with و in the middle.

steps.m: and he said (connects to the previous statement)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this to what came before
Changes the word from the action of anointing to describe someone who has been anointed
Makes it a specific title by adding ال (the) at the start and ُ at the end. This word refers to Jesus in this ayah.
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed from a verb meaning 'to build' to a special plural noun meaning 'children/descendants' - these words are not directly related

steps.m: O children! (addressing the Children of Israel)
steps.n: Added يٰ at the beginning to make it a call or address. The ىٓ at the end is a spelling variation of ي

steps.m: Israel (meaning 'one who struggles with God', referring to Prophet Jacob)
steps.n: This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew. It combines two parts: 'isra' (struggle/persist) and 'il' (God). It was given to Prophet Jacob (يعقوب) after he wrestled with an angel.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb

Changed from 'a god' to 'The God' - this is a unique word that became the proper name for Allah

steps.n: Adding ى at the end shows this is talking about 'my Lord'. In this ayah, Jesus (عليه السلام) is telling the Children of Israel to worship Allah, who is both his Lord and their Lord.

steps.m: to nurture, sustain, develop to completion

steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this a noun for someone who does this action completely - a complete nurturer and sustainer

steps.m: your (plural) Lord (addressing the Children of Israel)
steps.n: كُمْ means 'your' when talking to multiple people. Here it's addressing the Children of Israel (بَنِى إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ) mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: and your (pl) Lord (connected to رَبِّى/my Lord)
steps.n: Adds 'and' to connect this with the previous phrase where Jesus mentions 'my Lord' (رَبِّى), showing that his Lord is the same as their Lord
This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English
Added هُ at the end to show who or what is being talked about (in this case referring to Allah)

This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition about anyone who does something. Here it introduces whoever commits shirk (associates partners with Allah). It connects to the verb يُشْرِكْ (associates partners) that comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make it into a past tense verb
Added يُ at beginning to show present tense, changed vowels to show conditional form, and added sukun at end because it's in a conditional phrase (whoever associates...)
This is the unique name of God in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning of divinity but is treated as a proper name.

steps.m: by/with Allah (object of يُشْرِكْ - associates partners)

Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what was said before. This word connects to حَرَّمَ (He has forbidden) that comes right after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb

Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
This is the unique name for the One True God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the') and the shadda emphasizes the L sound to show uniqueness and importance.
This is a common preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'
The ى changes to يْ when adding a suffix
Added هِ at the end which means 'him'

steps.m: to cover or hide from view

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger

steps.m: garden (because it's hidden by trees and walls)
steps.n: Added ة to make it refer to a specific place that is hidden/covered

steps.m: garden (as object of حَرَّمَ - has forbidden)

Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Forms noun meaning 'fire' from root meaning 'to light/illuminate'
Adds the nominative case marker ُ to indicate this is the subject

Adding ال causes the first letter after it (ظ) to get a shadda, doubling its sound
When ل is added before ال, the ا in ال is not pronounced but the ل gets a kasra (ِ). The meaning becomes 'for/to the wrongdoers'
This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what it belongs to. In this ayah, it connects with the word أَنصَارٍ (helpers) to show that wrongdoers will not have any helpers.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed the word pattern to show who does the action
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple helpers
Added two dots under the last letter to show it's 'of helpers' or 'any helpers' (because it follows مِنْ)
This is a basic emphasis word that shows something definitely happened or is definitely true
Adding لَ at the beginning makes the certainty even stronger
Adding a shadda on the ل makes the emphasis even stronger, like saying 'absolutely' or 'definitely' in English

steps.m: to reject/deny truth, to be ungrateful for blessings
he rejected/denied (the statement that Allah is third of three)

Starting with the basic relative pronoun. Added kasra and ي to make it singular.

Added ال at the beginning to make it point to specific people. This word connects to كَفَرَ (disbelieved) to tell us which people disbelieved.

Added small alif (ٓ) and small circle (۟) above as pronunciation helpers

Changed to the unique name for the One True God by adding ال and merging letters
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of what they said. Also changed the alif to a wasl form since it comes after another word.
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters
Changes verb 'to make three' into 'third one' by adding alif
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject. This word is describing Allah as being 'third of three' in what some people wrongly claimed.

steps.m: three (being described as part of a group in this ayah)
steps.n: The word gets a double kasra (ٍ) at the end to show it's part of a longer phrase - like saying 'three of' instead of just 'three'
This is a basic negative particle that makes statements negative
Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement. This word negates the existence of other gods in this ayah.
This is a basic particle that shows something comes from somewhere or belongs to something. In this ayah, it connects with the word إِلٰهٍ (god) to show there is no god from/of the three they claim.

steps.m: to worship or be devoted to

steps.n: Root أله became إِلَه by adding vowels to make it mean 'one who is worshipped' - like changing 'worship' into 'god'

steps.m: any god (after مِنْ meaning 'there is no')

steps.m: to worship or be devoted to

steps.m: a deity (one of multiple deities being discussed)
steps.n: The double-damma (ٌ) shows this is talking about any deity in general, not a specific one. In this ayah, it's being used to discuss the concept of multiple deities.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to be'
Changed from a verb (he was one) to a describing word (one) by adding alif
Added tanween dammah to show this is any 'one', not a specific one
The alif (ا) changed to a small alif (ٰ) above the و but keeps the same meaning of 'one'

Basic negative particle that makes present tense verbs refer to the past and makes them negative
The ل is doubled because it combines with another ل from a nearby word - it's not for emphasis. It's still a past negation word that says 'did not' happen.

Added reflexive pattern to show doing action to oneself
Changed to present tense by adding يَ prefix and changing final vowel

steps.m: they stop themselves (from what they are saying)

Added يَ at beginning to show present/future tense
Added لَ to emphasize that this will definitely happen
Added نَّ at end to add extra emphasis that this will definitely happen

Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. In this ayah, it refers to rejecting the truth about Allah.
Added وا at the end to show a group of people did this action. The small mark after وا (۟) helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning.
This is a preposition that shows something coming from or originating from something else

steps.m: from them (who said this about اللَّهَ)
steps.n: Added 'them' to show who is being referred to. In this ayah, it refers back to those who said Allah is one of three.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Forms a noun indicating intense punishment/torment

Added tanween dammah to show this is describing punishment in a general way
This letter turns a statement into a question, like adding a question mark in English
Adds فَ to show this question follows from what was said before

steps.n: Adds لا (not) to make this a rhetorical question suggesting they should do something. This connects to the next word يَتُوبُونَ (they repent), asking 'will they not then repent?'
Added vowels to connect the letters. The middle و changes to ا because it's a weak letter.
The يَ at the start and ُ at the end show this is happening now or in the future. The middle letter returns to و.

steps.m: they turn back/repent (referring to the disbelievers in this ayah)
steps.n: The ونَ ending shows this is about a group of people (masculine plural). This word describes what the disbelievers are being asked to do in this ayah.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or connection. In this ayah, it connects the action of returning (يَتُوبُونَ) with Allah (اللَّهِ), showing who they should return to.
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One True God. The hamza from إِلَه is dropped in this special word.
Added shadda on ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added kasra at end to show this connects to the word إِلَى (to) before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added سْتَ to show seeking or requesting the action
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Added هُ to show whose forgiveness they seek
Added وَ to connect this to the previous phrase
This is a unique word that refers specifically to the one true God in Islam. While it comes from roots related to divinity and worship, it is considered a proper name.
Added shadda on ل to show it is doubled in pronunciation
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject (doer) of the sentence. Allah is being described as غَفُورٌ (Most Forgiving).

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of غَفُورٌ)

steps.m: to be merciful, to show compassion

steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters to show strong quality, with vowels to connect letters
Added double damma mark at end to make the word indefinite
The ّ (shadda) makes the ر doubled/stronger, showing extra mercy. When describing Allah, it means He has the most complete and perfect mercy.
This is a basic negative particle that means 'not' in Arabic. In this ayah, it's used to say that the Messiah is not just a messenger.
Adding a shadda (doubling mark) on the م makes the negation stronger, like saying 'definitely not' or 'really not' in English. This emphasizes that the Messiah is definitely not just a messenger.
Added ي between second and third root letters and vowel marks to show this describes someone who received anointing

steps.m: the one who is anointed (title of عِيسَى/Jesus)
steps.n: Adding ال makes this a specific title referring to Jesus

steps.m: the one who is anointed (subject of إِلَّا رَسُولٌ/is only a messenger)
steps.n: The ُ shows this word is the subject being described by إِلَّا رَسُولٌ (is only a messenger) in this ayah

steps.m: to build or establish a foundation

steps.n: Root meaning 'to build' became a noun for 'son' - like a building block that extends the family

Added ُ to show this is the subject, and changed the initial alif to facilitate pronunciation. This word is describing عِيسَى (Jesus) in the phrase 'المَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ' (The Messiah, son of Mary)
This is a proper name that comes from Hebrew/Aramaic 'Maryam', meaning 'wished-for child' or 'beloved'. It refers to the mother of Jesus (عِيسَى) in this context.
The fatha at the end shows this is the object of ابْنُ (son of) in the phrase المَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ (the Messiah, son of Mary)

This is a fixed word that shows an exception - it connects what comes after it (رَسُولٌ meaning 'messenger') to what came before it (ٱلْمَسِيحُ meaning 'the Messiah'), showing that the Messiah was nothing except/but a messenger
The و makes it mean someone who does this action a lot
The tanween (double damma) shows this is talking about any messenger, not a specific one
This is a particle that emphasizes the past tense verb خَلَتْ (passed away) that comes after it, making it clear that this definitely happened

steps.m: to pass away or be gone

steps.n: The root letters got vowels (َ) and the last letter و changed to ا to make 'he passed away'

steps.m: she passed away (referring to messengers before him)
steps.n: Added feminine marker ت with sukun to show this is about a feminine subject, referring to messengers (الرسل) that came before

steps.m: from/of (connects قَبْلِ to what came before)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition showing where something starts from or showing possession. Here it connects the word قَبْلِ (before) to what came earlier in the sentence.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic word meaning 'before'
Added kasra because this word comes after مِن (from/of)
Added هِ to show this means 'before him', coming after the word مِن (from/of)
The root letters r-s-l become plural 'rusul' (messengers) by adding the vowel marks damma

steps.m: the messengers (subject of خَلَتْ - passed away)
steps.n: Added ٱل to specify these particular messengers, doubled the ر with ّ (shadda) for emphasis, and added ُ at the end to show these messengers are doing the action of passing away in خَلَتْ

steps.m: to be in front, to lead as a mother

steps.n: The ُ (damma) sound makes this into the noun 'mother'
Added هُۥ (his) to show possession. This word is the subject (doer) paired with صِدِّيقَةٌ (truthful) in the ayah.
Added وَ (and) to connect this word with the previous phrase about Jesus being a messenger
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added shadda (ّ) to double the middle letter and added ي to show someone who does this action very much
Added ة to make the word feminine, showing it's talking about a woman

steps.m: a woman who always tells the truth (referring to Mary, mother of Jesus)
steps.n: Added two dots (ٌ) at the end to show this is describing Mary specifically as a truthful woman
This is the basic concept of existing or being in a state
Adding the vowel marks changes it from a concept to a past action
Added ا at the end to show it's talking about two people (Jesus and his mother). This word connects to يَأْكُلَانِ (they both eat) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Changed from past tense (he ate) to present tense (he eats) by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they both eat (referring to Jesus and his mother in this ayah)
steps.n: Added انِ to show it's specifically two people (Jesus and his mother) doing the action together
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from verb to noun meaning the thing that is eaten. This word is the direct object (what is being eaten) of the verb يَأْكُلَانِ (they both eat).
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ط with shadda because it comes after ال

steps.m: the food (object of يَأْكُلَانِ - they both eat)
steps.n: The word gets a fatha mark (-َ) at the end because it's receiving the action of eating
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make past tense verb
Changed the vowels to make it a command - when you tell someone to look at something
Added helper alif at beginning to make pronunciation easier
This is a question word used to ask about the way something happens or the state of something
Adding the vowel marks helps show this is a question word asking 'how'

steps.m: how (asking about manner in انظُرْ كَيْفَ - see how)
steps.n: Adding the final fatha mark. In this ayah, it's used with انظُرْ (see/look) to say 'look how' or 'see how'.
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic
Changed from 'he' to 'we' by adding نُ at the start of the word

steps.m: we make very clear (as a statement of fact)
steps.n: Final damma shows this is a statement of fact rather than a possibility or command
This is a basic particle that shows direction or purpose
Added هُمُ which shows this applies to a group of people (them)

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be evident
a sign or miracle (something that shows truth)
steps.n: The root letters combine in a special way to make a noun meaning 'sign' - like how a sign shows us something important
Makes the word plural to show multiple signs or miracles
Makes the plural noun definite by adding 'the'
The kasra at the end shows this word is being acted upon by the verb نُبَيِّنُ (we make clear/explain) in this ayah

This is a connecting word that shows one thing happens after another, with some time passing in between. In this ayah, it connects two commands to 'look' (انظُرْ), showing to first look at one thing, then look at another.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed from 'he looked' to 'look!' by making it a command - added 'u' at start and changed the vowels
Changed initial 'u' sound to regular alif, keeping same command meaning
This is a question word that asks about manner or method, similar to 'how' in English. It's a basic particle that doesn't come from a root.

steps.m: how (deluded away from the truth are they)
steps.n: These are actually two different words - أَنَّ means 'that/indeed' while أَنَّىٰ means 'how'. They just look similar. أَنَّىٰ is used here to ask in amazement how someone could turn away from the truth.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form past tense verb
Changed to passive present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added plural masculine ending to show multiple people are being turned away
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it a command form. The damma (ُ) and sukun (ْ) show it's a command.
The word is shortened because و is a weak letter. The command meaning stays the same.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changes from 'he worshipped' to 'you/she worship' by adding تَ at the start
Added ونَ to make it plural - now means multiple people are doing the action

steps.m: do you (all) worship? (question addressed to those who worship other than اللَّهِ)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning to make it a question. This word appears in a verse questioning those who worship other than Allah.

steps.m: from (connects دُونِ to what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or connects parts of a phrase. Here it connects دُونِ (besides/instead of) to the verb أَتَعْبُدُونَ (do you worship)
Adding damma (ُ) to make it a complete word
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to اللَّهِ (Allah) that comes after it, showing that something is 'besides Allah'
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One True God. The hamza is dropped and the lam gets a shadda.
Added kasra at the end to show possession/connection. This word is connected to يَمْلِكُ (possesses/owns) in this ayah. The alif at the start becomes a wasl form (ٱ).
This is a connecting word that refers to what is being worshipped. In this ayah, it connects to يَمْلِكُ (possesses/owns) to say 'that which does not possess'
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يَمْلِكُ (possesses/owns) into 'does not possess/own'
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changed to present tense. This word is negated by لا (not) in this ayah, showing what false gods cannot do.
This is a basic particle that shows direction or purpose
Added كُمْ to show this is directed at multiple people. This word is connected to يَمْلِكُ (possesses/owns) in this ayah, showing what is possessed.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic noun
The shadda combines the repeated ر letters to show intensity
Added tanween alif to show this word is receiving the action of يَمْلِكُ (possess/own). The full meaning in context is 'what does not possess/own for you any harm.'
This is a negative particle that makes something not happen or denies something
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it into a noun. This changes it from an action (to benefit) into a thing (a benefit).
Added ًا at the end to show this is the thing being possessed in the phrase 'does not possess any benefit.' Like saying 'I want a cookie' - the cookie is what's being acted on.
This is the unique proper name of God in Arabic. It's related to the root meaning of divinity but is a special word that only refers to the One God.
Added shadda to show the doubled 'l' sound, which is a special feature of this holy name

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence 'He is the All-Hearing'

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to mean 'and'. Also added the connecting hamza (ٱ) that comes after و.

This is the basic word for 'he' when talking about one male person or thing. Here it refers to Allah who is described by the following words السَّمِيعُ (The All-Hearing) and الْعَلِيمُ (The All-Knowing)
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to experience hearing'
Changed from past tense verb 'heard' to a special form with ي that means 'one who always hears' - used as a name of Allah
Added ال to make it specific, which causes the س to get a shadda

steps.m: The All-Hearing (one of Allah's names, subject of هُوَ)
steps.n: Added ُ to show this is the subject of هُوَ (he) in this ayah
Added ي between second and third root letters with vowels to show someone who possesses this quality completely
Adding ال makes it definite (like 'the' in English) and when used as one of Allah's names it indicates complete and perfect knowledge. The ُ shows this is describing Allah in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: say! (command to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to speak to أَهْلَ ٱلْكِتٰبِ)
steps.n: Changed to a command form by: 1) Adding damma on first letter, 2) Dropping the middle weak letter و, 3) Adding sukun on last letter. This is Allah commanding Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to speak to the People of the Book.

steps.m: to settle, to inhabit, to be familiar with

steps.n: Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make the basic noun meaning 'people/family'. This word is part of أهل الكتاب (People of the Book).
Adding يا at the start turns the word into 'O people!' - like calling out to get someone's attention
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from verb 'wrote' to noun meaning 'something written'
ٱلْ makes the word definite (like 'the' in English), and the kasra at the end shows it follows a preposition
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it into a prohibition. Here it tells people not to do the action in تَغْلُوا۟ (go to extremes).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added تَ at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense. The ا changes back to و.

steps.m: you (all) go beyond bounds (in دِينِكُمْ your religion)
steps.n: Added ا with small و above (۟) to show that many people are being addressed. This word relates to دِينِكُمْ (your religion) in the ayah, showing what they are going beyond bounds in.
This is a helper word (preposition) that shows something is inside or within something else. Since it's a helper word, it doesn't come from a root.
Added ى at the end to complete the word. In this ayah, it shows that the action happens within 'your religion' (دِينِكُمْ).

steps.m: to follow/practice a way of life

steps.n: The kasra vowel transforms the root into a noun indicating a way/system

steps.m: your (all of you) religion (object of فِى)
steps.n: Adds 'your' (plural) to show ownership. This word comes after فِى (in/concerning) in the ayah, showing what they shouldn't go beyond limits in.
Root letters were joined together with a fatha and sukoon to make a word meaning 'other than' or 'different from'
Added fatha at the end because this word is being acted upon by the verb لَا تَغْلُوا۟ (do not exceed). It tells us what they shouldn't exceed beyond.
Connected the letters and doubled the middle letter to make a noun meaning 'truth'
Added ال to point to a specific truth rather than truth in general
Added kasra at the end because this word comes after غَيْرَ (other than) in the ayah
This is the basic negation word used to say 'no' or 'not' to something
Added و at the beginning to connect this negation to the previous statement. This connects to the verb تَتَّبِعُوٓا۟ (follow) to say 'and do not follow'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Added تَ at start and doubled middle letter to show intensive following
Added plural masculine ending to show multiple people are doing the action
The sukun shows the word ends silently

steps.m: to desire something, to have a strong inclination

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to make it a complete past tense verb
Changes from a verb (to desire) into a plural noun (desires) by adding أ at the start and اء at the end
Adds a fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb 'follow' in the ayah

steps.m: to stand firmly, rise up, establish

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun meaning 'people' or 'nation' - the idea comes from people standing together as one group
Added the tanwin kasra (ٍ) to show this word is indefinite (a/any people) and is possessed by the word أَهْوَآءَ (desires), making it mean 'desires of a people'
This is a helper word that adds emphasis and shows something definitely happened in the past. In this ayah, it emphasizes that these people definitely went astray before.

steps.m: to wander off the right path
he wandered off the right path
steps.n: Connected the letters with fatha vowels to show this happened in the past
Changed middle vowel to damma to show being lost was his state
Added وا at the end to show a group of people (they) did this action

steps.m: from (connects to قَبْلُ to mean 'from before')
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows where something starts from. In this ayah, it combines with قَبْلُ (before) to show these people went astray in the past.

steps.m: to be in front of or before something

steps.n: The root letters were given vowel marks (fatha and sukun) to make a word meaning 'before' - like saying 'in front of' in time or space
Added damma at the end to show this word is being used as a complete concept by itself, not connected to another word.
Added vowels and combined letters with shadda on ل to show doubled letter
Changes to causative form - making others go astray. The word كَثِيرًا is the object showing who was led astray - meaning 'many people'.

steps.m: and he made (someone) lose the way
steps.n: Adds conjunction 'and'

steps.m: and they made (many) lose the way
steps.n: Adds third person masculine plural subject marker to show many people did this action
Added ي between the last two root letters and vowels to make it into a describing word
Added ًا at the end to show this word is receiving the action of 'leading astray' - like saying 'they led many astray'
Adding vowels makes it a past tense verb meaning 'to go astray'
Changed to ضَلُّوا by adding وا at the end to show many people went astray instead of just one person

steps.m: and they lost the right path
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this with the previous part
This is a preposition that shows movement away from something. In this ayah, it connects to the word سَوَآءِ (right path) to show moving away from the right path.

steps.m: to be level, balanced, or straight

steps.n: Adding fathas makes it a past tense verb meaning 'he made straight'
Changed from a verb (making something straight) to a noun meaning 'middle/balanced path' by adding hamza at the end

steps.m: middle path, right way (of ٱلسَّبِيلِ - the path)
steps.n: Added kasra to show this describes 'the path' (ٱلسَّبِيلِ) that comes after it

steps.m: to make a path or way

steps.n: Adding ي and vowels creates the pattern for 'path'
Added ٱل to make it specific and doubled the س with shadda
Added kasra at the end because it comes after عَن (from) in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: was cursed (subject: those who disbelieved - الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا)
steps.n: Changed the vowels to show that the curse happened to someone rather than someone doing the cursing. This word is about those who disbelieved being cursed.
Added ين at the end to make it refer to multiple people or things
Added ٱلّ at the beginning to make it specific and point to particular people. This word connects to كَفَرُوا۟ (they disbelieved) in the ayah, showing who did the disbelieving.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb. This word describes who was cursed (لُعِنَ) in this ayah.
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. These are the ones who were cursed (لُعِنَ).
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what it's made of. It's like saying 'from' or 'of' in English. Since it's a particle, it doesn't come from a root.
Added kasra (ِ) under م to show how to pronounce it correctly. This word connects to بَنِىٓ إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ (Children of Israel) in this ayah.
Added a small م mark (called qalqala) after ن to show it should be pronounced clearly. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.

steps.m: to build or establish something (like a family or structure)

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fathas) to the root letters to make it mean 'he built' in the past tense
These are two different words - 'banaa' means built, while 'bani' means children/descendants. They just look similar but aren't related.
Added the long vowel mark to show this word connects to what comes after it (إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ/Israel), making the phrase 'Children of Israel'
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew. It combines two parts: إِسْرٰٓء (meaning 'to struggle/strive') and إِيلَ (meaning 'God' in Hebrew).
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or over something else

Added the small alif (ٰ) which is called alif khanjariyah. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning. In this ayah, it shows what the cursing happened upon - the tongue/speech of Dawud.
Root letters ل-س-ن form the noun لِسَان meaning tongue/language. The word is connected to داوُد (David) to mean 'tongue of David'.
The ِ (kasra) at the end shows this word belongs to داوُۥدَ (David) who comes after it. Like saying 'tongue of David' in English.
This is a proper noun, the name of Prophet David (peace be upon him)

steps.m: David (object of عَلَىٰ لِسَانِ - upon the tongue of)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show that David is the object being referred to in the phrase 'upon the tongue of David'
This is a proper noun, the name of Prophet Jesus
The و at the beginning connects this name to دَاوُۥدَ (David) mentioned just before it in the ayah, showing both prophets together

steps.m: to build or produce something from components
steps.n: This root relates to building and producing, which connects to how a son is 'produced' from parents
Added alif at start and sukun on middle letter to create the noun 'son' from the concept of being produced/built
Changed initial alif to hamzat wasl and added kasra at end to show possession - this connects 'son' to 'Mary' in the phrase 'son of Mary'
This is a proper noun, the name of Mary, mother of Jesus. In this ayah, it's part of the phrase 'عِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ' (Jesus son of Mary)
The fatha at the end shows that Mary is the object of the word 'son of' in the phrase 'Jesus son of Mary'
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is replaced with a special mark (ٰ) to show distance
Added لِ to strengthen the meaning of pointing to something

steps.m: that (pointing to what was mentioned before)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show we're referring to something mentioned before (in this case, referring to what was cursed)
This is a preposition that shows cause or reason in this context

steps.m: because of what (connects to يَعْتَدُونَ - they transgressed)
steps.n: Added ما which means 'what' to specify what the reason is. This combination introduces the reason for the curse mentioned earlier, connecting to their transgression (يَعْتَدُونَ) mentioned later.
Added fathas and changed ي to ى because it's at the end of the word. This shows one person did this in the past.
Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did this action. The ى changes to و because of this ending.

steps.m: to be or exist in a certain state

steps.n: Changed the root letters into past tense by adding the vowel marks 'a' (fatha)
Added وا at the end to show multiple people (they) did this action
Added و with fatha at the beginning to mean 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ت after first letter to make it mean 'going too far' or 'crossing boundaries' in a bad way
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Adding the fatha (َ) makes it a past tense verb
Adding the second fatha (َ) completes the past tense verb pattern
Added وا at the end to show many people (they) did this action. This word is the subject of لَا يَتَنَاهَوْنَ (they did not forbid) in this ayah.
This is a negative particle that makes the action after it not happen. Here it shows they did NOT forbid wrong actions.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start and ا in middle to show stopping oneself (like saying 'he prevented himself')
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start. This verb follows كَانُوا (they were) in the ayah.
Added وْنَ to show multiple people doing the action. The ى is dropped before adding this ending.

steps.m: from/away from (preposition connecting يَتَنَاهَوْنَ with مُّنكَرٍ)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition showing separation or moving away from something. Here it connects 'they would not forbid' (يَتَنَاهَوْنَ) with 'wrong' (مُّنكَرٍ) to mean 'they would not forbid from wrong.'

steps.m: to not accept or approve of something
something wrong or bad (that people shouldn't do)
steps.n: Adding مُ at the start and َ in the middle turns this into a word for something that is wrong or disapproved of
The ٍ at the end shows this is talking about any wrong deed, and the ّ shows emphasis on how wrong it was. This word connects to فَعَلُوهُ (they did it) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Makes it 'they' (masculine) in past tense
The ه at the end means 'it' - the thing they did. This word is a complete verbal phrase and is not related to other words through case endings.
Adding vowels changes this into a common word used to criticize evil actions
Adding لَ at start makes the word stronger, like saying 'truly' or 'indeed'
This is a basic particle that asks about or refers to things. Here it connects to يَفْعَلُونَ (they used to do) to show what actions they did.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb
Added وا to show multiple people (they) did the action. This word is the subject of لَا يَتَنَاهَوْنَ (they would not forbid) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense base form
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at the start
Adds 'u' sound at the end to show this is the doer of the action
Adding ون changes it from singular (he) to plural (they). This word is connected to كَانُوا (they were) in the ayah.

steps.m: to see/perceive with eyes or mind

steps.n: Basic past tense pattern added vowels (fatha) to make 'he saw'
Changed the last letter to ى because it's a special verb ending
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and changed to present tense form
Added alif khanjariya (ٰ) to show the long vowel sound more clearly
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (something happening) to a describing word showing a lot of something
The word got a tanween (double line) at the end to show it's being acted upon - it's what is being seen
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added emphasis by doubling the م sound

steps.m: from them (referring to those who turn in friendship to the disbelievers)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being referred to. This connects to the people mentioned who turn in friendship to those who disbelieve.

steps.m: to be close to, support, or take charge of

steps.n: Added intensity to the basic meaning by doubling the middle letter

steps.m: he takes as an ally (for himself)
steps.n: Added reflexive meaning (doing action for oneself)

steps.m: he takes/is taking as an ally (for himself)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start

steps.m: they take/are taking as allies (for themselves)
steps.n: Changed from 'he' to 'they' - added ون at the end to make it plural
This is the basic form used to point to someone or something specific
Added ين to make it refer to multiple people instead of just one
Added ال at the beginning to make it more specific, like saying 'the ones who' instead of just 'ones who'
Added final vowel marks and shadda for proper pronunciation. This word connects to كَفَرُوا (they disbelieved) in the ayah, showing who did the disbelieving.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb

steps.m: they disbelieved (subject of يَتَوَلَّوْنَ - they take as allies)
steps.n: Added وا with damma before it to show multiple people did this action. The small circle (۟) is just a pronunciation mark.
This is a special verb form used specifically for condemning or criticizing something. The hamza gets a kasra (ِ), and the final letter gets a fatha (َ).
Adding لَ at the start makes the meaning stronger, like saying 'truly' or 'indeed'
This is a connecting word that links to what comes after it. Here it connects to قَدَّمَتْ (presented) to mean 'that which was presented'
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb

Adding ت with sukun at the end shows the subject is feminine (she)
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning
Added ه (him) to show who it's for
Added م with sukun to make the pronoun plural (them instead of him)
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun

Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation
Changed the final ى to يْ to prepare for connecting with the pronoun
Added هِمْ at the end to show who is being acted upon (them)
Basic preposition meaning 'in'
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Changed the final ي to ى which is just a writing style variation - it doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Added alif after second root letter to make it a noun meaning the result of punishment
Added the definite article ال to specify this particular punishment
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word في (in)
This is a standalone pronoun that refers to a group of people. In this ayah, it's the subject of خٰلِدُونَ (abiding forever), referring to those who turned to the disbelievers.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and changed middle vowel to kasra to show who does the action
Added ون to make it refer to multiple people

steps.m: those who last forever (plural, in regular case)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show regular grammatical case

steps.m: and even if (connecting to previous statement)

Added fatha marks to show past tense. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter.
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Added an alif at the start changed it from 'trust' to 'have religious faith/belief'
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. The hamza changed form due to pronunciation rules.
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This word is connected to كَانُوا (they were) in the ayah.
This is the unique proper name of God in Islam. It comes from the root meaning of divinity but is treated as a proper noun.
Added بِ at the beginning to show how someone relates to Allah, and added ِ at the end because it follows another word. The ٱ is just a different way to write the same ال.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic noun
Changed middle vowel to kasra and added hamza to show someone who does this action

Added ال at beginning to make it specific and doubled the ن because it comes after ال
Added و at beginning to connect it to previous word, which changes ال to ٱل
Added kasra at end to show this is being acted upon by the verb يُؤْمِنُونَ (they believe in)
This is the base interrogative particle 'what'. The alif is written long (آ) for emphasis in writing but doesn't change the meaning.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to the previous statement about believing (يُؤْمِنُونَ)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to passive form to show the action was done to something rather than by something
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination
Added sukun and changed alif to ya to prepare for connecting with pronoun
Added هِ (him) at the end to show who is being referred to
In this ayah, مَا is being used as a relative pronoun referring to what was revealed, not as a negation particle. It's connecting to أُنزِلَ (was revealed) which comes after it.
Changed to show doing something for oneself. The ٱ replaces أ at the start, and we add ت with a shadda after it.
و indicates masculine plural subject (they)
Added هُمْ to show who they took. This connects to the word أَوْلِيَآءَ (allies) that comes after it in the ayah.

steps.m: to be close to and protect

steps.n: Changed the root letters into a noun by adding vowels and doubling the last letter to mean 'one who protects'
Single 'protector' changed to plural 'protectors'. Like changing 'friend' to 'friends' in English. This is what they took others as.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'but'. The alif after ل is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this case.
Adding ّ (shadda) makes the word stronger and more emphatic, like saying 'but indeed' or 'but certainly'
Adding وَ at the beginning connects this word to what came before it. Here it connects to the previous statement about if they believed in Allah and the Prophet.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Added ي to make it into an adjective meaning 'much' or 'many'
Added tanween alif to show this word is describing what is being acted upon
This is the basic preposition meaning 'from'. It shows where something starts or comes from, like saying 'from the store' in English.
Added sukun (ْ) on the ن to show where the word pauses when not connected to anything.
Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being referred to. هُمْ means 'them' and refers back to the disbelievers mentioned earlier in the verse.
Added shadda (ّ) on the م because in the Quran, when this word connects to the word before it in speech, the ن sound blends into the م sound, making it stronger.

steps.m: to break rules, to disobey limits
one who breaks rules (predicate of لٰكِنَّ)
steps.n: Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does this action. This word describes what many of them (كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ) are like in this verse.
Adding ون makes it plural to show we're talking about many people. The ُ shows this describes what they are.
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters
Changed to present tense with ت for 'you' and changed middle vowel to ِ
Added لَ for emphasis and future meaning
Added نَّ for strong emphasis, replacing the final ُ with َ
Connected the root letters and added shadda to show doubled letter
Added أَ at the beginning to make it comparative, showing something is more intense than something else. In this ayah, it's describing how intense the enmity is.

steps.m: to be friendly or social with others

steps.n: Root meaning 'friendly/social' became a noun meaning 'people' who live together in society
The kasra (ِ) at the end shows this word comes after a word meaning 'of'

steps.m: to go beyond limits, act with hostility

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after second letter and ة at the end to make it into an abstract noun meaning the concept of hostility

steps.m: hostility (as the object of أَشَدَّ - most intense)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha at the end to show this word is receiving the action of being 'most intense'. The alif is written as a dagger alif (ٰ) in this word.
Starting with the basic demonstrative word that points to something
Adding ال and ي changes it from pointing at something to describing who or what we're talking about
Adding ين makes it refer to many people instead of just one
Adding لِ shows this is being done for or to someone
The ل gets doubled (shadda) when it comes after another ل, like combining لِ + ال into لِلّ. The vowel marks help show it means 'for the ones who' and connects to 'believed' after it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding alif at beginning changes it from 'to trust' to 'to have faith/believe religiously' - makes it specifically about religious belief
Added وا to show many people (they) did the action. This word is what the earlier word لَتَجِدَنَّ (you will surely find) is talking about.

steps.m: to return, to guide, to become Jewish
steps.n: This is the basic root meaning
Changed the root letters into a name for a group of people by adding the vowel mark 'oo' sound
Added ي at start to make it a proper noun referring to the Jewish people
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it specific

steps.m: the Jews (as object of لَتَجِدَنَّ - you will surely find)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because this word is being acted upon by the verb لَتَجِدَنَّ (you will surely find)

Added ين at the end to make it plural, showing we're talking about multiple people or things
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it in the verse. It connects to the word أَشَدَّ (most intense) to show another group being described.

steps.m: to join or associate something with something else
steps.n: This is the basic root meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Added hamza at beginning to show causing someone else to do the action. Changed vowels to match Form IV pattern.
Added plural ending to show multiple people did this action. In this ayah, it refers to those who associate partners with Allah in worship.
This is the basic root meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding تَ at start and changing middle vowel to kasra and end to damma
Added لَ for emphasis and future meaning, and changed final vowel to fatha

steps.m: surely you will find (with strong emphasis)

steps.m: and surely you will find (with strong emphasis)

This pattern shows that being close is a natural quality of the subject

steps.n: Adds هُم at the end to show who the word is referring to. In this ayah, it's referring to the Christians mentioned right after.

steps.m: to love deeply, to show affection

steps.n: Added vowels and combined letters with shadda to show strong feeling
Changes from action of loving to the feeling/concept of love
Makes the word indefinite (like saying 'a love' instead of 'the love')
Starting with the base word meaning 'those who', adding vowel marks to show pronunciation
Added لِ at the beginning to show purpose or direction
The ل merged with the ل after it, shown by the shadda mark
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: to make someone/something secure, to believe
steps.n: Adding alif at the start changes the meaning from 'feeling secure' to 'believing in something'. Like going from 'safe' to 'trusting in something'.

Added fatha vowels to connect the letters and make it past tense
Changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb
Adding وا at the end changes the subject from 'he' to 'they'

Added ى at the end to create plural form specifically referring to Christians (named this way because they were 'helpers/supporters' of Jesus)

This is the basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby

Adding لِ makes the word stronger and more emphatic

steps.m: that (pointing to what was mentioned before)

Added ي to show this is someone who does this as their job/role

Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb

Changed to present tense with يَ prefix and ُ ending

The ِ ending shows this follows the word إِلَى (to) in the ayah

Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ at the start

Added possessive pronoun 'their'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb

Root letters form a noun meaning 'tear' by adding the vowels

Added وا with a damma before it to show many people did this action. The small circle after وا (۟) helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning.

Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word مِنَ (from) in the ayah
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense, and vowels to show how it's pronounced

steps.n: The ونَ ending shows a group of people (either all male or mixed group) are doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to the Christians who recognize the truth when they hear the Quran.

steps.m: our Lord (object of يَقُولُونَ - they say)

نا at the end means 'we' - shows who did the believing
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb

نا here means 'us' as the object (the ones being written/recorded)

steps.m: so write/record us! (in response to يَقُولُونَ - they say)
steps.n: فَ shows this is a response or result following what was said before. This is part of what they say after recognizing the truth.

steps.m: to witness or testify about what one sees

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed from past tense verb (he witnessed) to active participle (witness/testifier) by adding alif after first letter
Added ين to show multiple witnesses
Added ال (the) at beginning and doubled the ش with shadda. This word comes after مَعَ (with) in the ayah.

steps.m: what (asking about why we shouldn't believe)
steps.n: This is the question word 'what' that introduces the question in this ayah about why they shouldn't believe

steps.m: and what (connects to previous statement about belief)
steps.n: Added 'wa' at the beginning to connect this question to the previous statement. This shows the question is a continuation of the discussion about belief.

steps.m: for us (connected to نُؤْمِنُ - we believe)

This is a negative particle that makes something not happen. In this ayah, it negates the verb نُؤْمِنُ (we believe), changing its meaning to 'we do not believe'
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Changed the first letter to make it specifically about religious belief
Changed to present tense 'we' form. This word connects to 'Allah' later in the ayah showing what we believe in.
This is the base word - Allah's proper name. It's unique and doesn't come from a three-letter root like most Arabic words.
Added shadda on ل to show it's pronounced strongly

steps.m: in/by/with Allah (object of نُؤْمِنُ - we believe)
steps.n: Added بِ at start to mean 'in/by/with' and ِ at end to connect it to the verb نُؤْمِنُ (we believe). In this ayah, it's showing what we believe in.
This is a basic particle in Arabic that can either ask questions (like 'what?') or make something negative (like 'not'). In this ayah, it's being used to ask 'what?' about why they wouldn't believe.
Added و at the beginning to connect this question with what came before. Here it's connecting to لَنَا (for us) to ask 'and what is for us?'
The middle letter ي changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
Adding vowel marks to make it a past tense verb
Adding نا at the end changes it from 'he came' to 'he came to us'
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or starts
Added َ (fatha) at the end to help connect it smoothly to the next word الْحَقِّ (the truth). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
The second letter is doubled (has shadda) to make it stronger and more emphatic
Root letters ح-ق-ق became the word 'truth' by combining the double ق with a shadda mark
Added the definite article ٱلْ to make it specific
Added kasra to show this word is connected to what comes before it (مِنَ)
The kasra shows this word is connected to what comes before it
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added نَ prefix to show 'we' as the doers, added ُ ending to show present tense, and put sukun on middle letter for present tense pattern. This word connects to أَن (that) which comes after it.
