steps.n: The prefix وَ (and) connects this hope with the belief mentioned earlier in نُؤْمِنُ (we believe). The whole phrase means 'we believe and we hope'.

steps.m: that (connects to يُدْخِلَنَا - He admits us)
steps.n: This is a particle that connects the verb يُدْخِلَنَا (He admits us) to the previous word وَنَطْمَعُ (and we hope), showing what is being hoped for. It's like saying 'we hope THAT He will admit us'
Basic past tense form showing someone completed the action of entering
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at the beginning makes the verb causative - showing someone is making the action happen to someone else
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: he makes us enter (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show who is being made to enter (us)

steps.m: to care for and help grow
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this an intensive form, emphasizing the complete nature of the nurturing
Connected the letters together and added the shadda to show strong caring
Added ُ at the end to show this word is doing the action of admitting/entering (يُدْخِلَ) in the verse

steps.m: our Lord (as the one doing something)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show it belongs to us ('our'). This word is doing the action of admitting/entering (يُدْخِلَ) in the verse

steps.m: with, together with (connects to ٱلْقَوْمِ - the people)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows things happening together. In this ayah, it connects to 'the people' (ٱلْقَوْمِ) to show who they want to be with.
The root meaning of standing/rising is transformed into a collective noun for people who stand together
Root 'stand' becomes 'people' - like how people stand together as a group. The vowels make it mean a collection of people.
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific group of people rather than just any group

steps.m: of the people (connected to مَعَ meaning 'with')
steps.n: The ِ ending shows this word follows مَعَ (with) in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changed from verb (to be good) to noun (one who is good)
Adding definite article to make the word definite

Sound masculine plural suffix ين indicates multiple righteous people. The word is in genitive case because it comes after القوم in a possession relationship, where القوم is the possessor and الصالحين is what is possessed.
Final fatha shows this word is the object of مَعَ (with) in the phrase مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلصَّالِحِينَ (with the righteous people)
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Added hamza at beginning to show causative meaning
Added فَ to show this happened as a result of what came before
Added هُمُ to show who received the reward
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
This is the unique name for the One True God in Islam. The ال makes it specific, and the doubled ل shows the specialness of this name.
مَا is a special word that can mean either 'what' (as a question) or 'that which' (referring to something). Here it means 'that which' because it's referring to what was said.
Adding بِ at the beginning shows why something happened. Here it connects what they said (قَالُوا) to Allah's reward (فَأَثٰبَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ).
Changed the middle letter و to ا and added fathas to show this happened in the past
Added وا at the end to show many people did this. This word is connected to فَأَثٰبَهُمُ (so Allah rewarded them) in the ayah - it shows what they said before being rewarded.
Added fatha vowels and doubled the middle letter to show strong/complete covering

steps.m: a garden (because it covers with its shade)
steps.n: Forms the noun 'garden' from the idea of covering, since gardens provide covering shade
Makes it plural (more than one garden). This word is what Allah is rewarding them with in this ayah.
The tanween kasra (ٍ) shows this is the object being given as a reward
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Added تَ to make it present tense
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from
Added the kasra (ِ) mark under م to show how to pronounce it correctly. In this ayah, it's used with تَجْرِى (flows) to show the rivers flow from under the gardens.
Becomes an adverb showing location
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word - fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter
Added kasra at the end to show this connects to the next word
Added ها at the end to show what is beneath - refers back to the gardens (جَنَّاتٍ) mentioned earlier
Root letters were connected with vowels (fatha) to make the word for 'river'
Changed to plural form showing multiple rivers
Added the definite article ال to specify these particular rivers
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of the verb تَجْرِى (flows) in this ayah. Also changed alif to alif madd for proper pronunciation.
Added alif after first letter and kasra on second letter to show who does the action. This creates the basic form meaning 'one who stays'.
Added ين to show this describes multiple people/things. In this ayah, it describes the state of those in the gardens (جَنَّاتٍ) - they will stay there permanently.

steps.m: those who stay permanently (in accusative case)
steps.n: Added the fatha (َ) at the end to show this word is in the accusative case, matching with جَنَّاتٍ (gardens) that came before it
This is a basic preposition showing location or being inside something
Added ها to show what is being referred to - the gardens mentioned earlier in the ayah
This is the basic demonstrative word that points to something
Adding ٰلِ changes it from pointing to something near (this) to something far or abstract (that)
Adding كَ makes it more specific and emphatic

steps.m: and that (referring to the reward mentioned before)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this word to what came before it in the ayah, linking the reward with what was previously mentioned

steps.m: to give back what is due

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fatha) to the root letters to make it mean 'he gave back' in the past
Changed the verb into a noun that names the action itself
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject. The alif is written long (آ) for emphasis.

steps.m: to do good, to be excellent

steps.n: Added أ at the beginning to make it mean 'he made something/someone good' instead of just 'being good'
Changed to describe someone who does this action regularly
Adds kasra to prepare for making it plural
Makes the word plural to refer to multiple people who do good

steps.m: the ones who do good (to others)
steps.n: Makes the word definite by adding 'the'

steps.m: the ones who do good (to others)
steps.n: Adds the accusative case ending
Added ن to show we're talking about multiple people
Added ال to point to specific people we're talking about
Added و at the beginning to connect this with what came before. This word is connected to كَفَرُوا۟ (they disbelieved) in the ayah.

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form. This word describes what 'those who' (وَٱلَّذِينَ) did.
Added وا to show multiple people did this action. The small mark (۟) above وا is just for proper recitation.

steps.m: to deny, to reject as false
he denied intensely (rejected as completely false)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows he did this action strongly and deliberately

steps.m: they denied intensely (rejected as completely false)
steps.n: The وا ending shows a group of people (could be all men or mixed group) did this action in the past

steps.m: and they denied intensely (connects to كَفَرُوا۟ - they disbelieved)
steps.n: Added 'and' at the beginning to connect this action with the previous word كَفَرُوا۟ (they disbelieved), showing these actions happened together

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be evident

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun meaning 'sign'. The long آ at the start comes from combining أ with ي.
Made the word plural to show multiple signs

steps.m: our signs (object of كَذَّبُوا - they denied)
steps.n: Added 'our' to show these are Allah's signs. This is what is being denied in the phrase 'they denied our signs'

steps.m: with/by our signs (object of كَذَّبُوا - they denied)
steps.n: Added بِ at the start to show how they denied - specifically by/with these signs
This is the base form of the demonstrative pronoun used to point to things
Adding ئِ shows that what we're pointing to is far away
Adding كَ shows we're addressing someone about these far things/people
Changes from verb 'to be close' to noun meaning 'one who is close'
Changed the word to show many companions instead of just one
Changed spelling slightly and added u-mark at end to show these companions are being talked about (connected to 'those' أُو۟لٰٓئِكَ)
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters to show intense quality, with vowel pattern changes to form noun
Adding ال makes it refer to the specific hellfire mentioned in the Quran, not just any intense fire
The kasra ending shows this word belongs to or is possessed by أَصْحٰبُ (companions), forming the phrase 'companions of the hellfire'

steps.m: to point to or indicate something
which (used to point to something specific)
steps.n: The root letters combine with a shadda (doubled letter) to make a word that asks 'which one?'
Added يٰٓ at the beginning to call attention, like saying 'hey' to someone
Added ها to make it a complete attention-calling phrase, like saying 'O you there!'
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun used to connect descriptions to people or things
Adding ال makes it specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'
Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people. This word describes the believers who are being addressed in this ayah.
Added proper pronunciation marks: the ٱ shows where to start, the ّ doubles the ل sound, and the other marks show the correct vowel sounds
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to make the meaning causative - changing from just feeling secure to actively putting trust/faith in something

steps.m: they believed/had faith (referring to believers being addressed)
steps.n: Added plural ending to show multiple people doing the action. This word describes the believers being addressed in يٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ (O you who...)

This is a negation word that makes the verb تُحَرِّمُوا۟ (make unlawful) negative, telling people not to make things unlawful. The fatha (َ) helps show it's being used as a complete word rather than attached to something else.

steps.m: to forbid or make something sacred/protected

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter shows doing something thoroughly or making something happen
تُ at the start shows this is happening now and you are doing it

steps.m: you all make (something) forbidden (addressing multiple people)
steps.n: Adding وا shows multiple people are being addressed
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it an adjective
The shadda intensifies the meaning to emphasize complete purity
Adding feminine plural ending to make it refer to multiple pure things
Added kasra at the end because this word is the object of the verb تُحَرِّمُوا (do not forbid)
This is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it connects to the word أَحَلَّ (made lawful) to mean 'what was made lawful'
Added fatha (َ) on م to show how to pronounce it correctly
Added the elongation mark (آ) to show that the sound is stretched when reading
Added vowel marks to make it 'he permitted' in the past tense
Form IV transformation adds causative meaning ('to make something permissible')

steps.m: he made (something) permissible (referring to what اللَّهُ made permissible)
steps.n: When two identical letters come together, they merge with a shadda. The first letter's sukun is replaced by a fatha for easier pronunciation.
Changed the root into a noun form that means 'one who is worshipped'

This is the unique name for The One True God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the ُ shows this is the subject of لَا يُحِبُّ (does not love) in this ayah.
This is a basic particle meaning 'for' or 'to' that connects actions or things to who they're meant for
Added fatha to help with pronunciation

steps.m: for you all (referring to what Allah has made lawful)
steps.n: Added كُمْ which means 'you all'. This shows that what Allah made lawful is specifically for this group being addressed.
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation of the negative particle

steps.m: and not (connects to تُحَرِّمُوا۟ - do not forbid)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this negative phrase with what came before. This connects to the verb تُحَرِّمُوا۟ (do not forbid) to add another prohibition.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Adding اِت changes it to form VIII, showing someone doing the action to themselves
Changed from 'he did' to 'you all do not do' - a negative command for a group

steps.m: you all transgress (as a prohibition with لا)
steps.n: Added final writing marks to show it's connected to لا as a prohibition
This is the basic form of the particle that connects parts of a sentence, like saying 'that' in English
Adding the shadda makes this word stronger, changing it from just connecting words to emphasizing what comes next. Here it emphasizes 'Allah' (اللَّهَ) in the phrase.
Changed إِلَه (any deity) to اللَه (the one true God) by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object being referred to)
steps.n: Added shadda to show emphasis on ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added fatha at end to show Allah is the object being referred to in this sentence
This is a negative particle that makes verbs or statements negative
When two identical letters come together, they combine with a shadda

steps.m: he loves (لا in this ayah makes it 'he does not love')
steps.n: Added يُ at the start to show present tense and changed vowels to show ongoing action. In this ayah, the word comes after لا which makes it negative - 'does not love'

steps.m: to go beyond proper bounds, to exceed limits

steps.n: Added ا and ت to show someone doing this action to/for themselves. The و changes to ى at the end because it's a weak letter.
Changed the beginning to مُ and added ِ in the middle to show this describes someone who does this action

steps.m: the one who goes beyond bounds (object of يُحِبُّ)
steps.n: Added 'the' (ال) to make it specific - refers to those who go too far that Allah does not love
Changes from 'the one who transgresses' to 'those who transgress' by adding ين at the end
Shows this word is being acted upon in the sentence (adds -a sound at end)
Basic command form telling one person to eat
Adding وا changes it from commanding one person to commanding many people
Adding وَ at the beginning connects this command to what came before it
Combined the two basic words مِن (from) and مَا (what) together

steps.m: from what (of رَزَقَكُمُ - what He provided you)
steps.n: The ن changes to م and gets a shadda (doubled) because it's easier to say. This is like how 'in memory' becomes 'immemory' in English when spoken quickly.

steps.m: to provide with what is needed (especially food, money, or resources)

steps.n: Added fatha marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This is the main verb in this part of the ayah.

steps.m: he provided you all (referring to Allah providing to the believers)
steps.n: Added كُمُ (kum) at the end to mean 'you all' - showing Allah gave provision to you (the believers). Like changing 'he gave' to 'he gave you'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' by adding vowel marks

This is a unique word that refers specifically to the one true God in Islam. The ال at the beginning makes it definite (like 'the'), and the shadda emphasizes the L sound. The ُ at the end shows this is the subject of the verb رَزَقَ (provided) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb form

steps.m: lawful, permissible (describes food in this ayah)
steps.n: Changes the verb into an adjective describing something that is lawful. Here it describes the food Allah has provided.

steps.m: as lawful (describes how to eat the food)
steps.n: Adds an alif and tanween (double line) at the end to make it work as a describing word for how to eat the food
Added shadda to strengthen the meaning, making it more intense

steps.m: lawful and pure (describes رَزَقَكُمُ - what Allah has provided you)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is describing what Allah has provided, working with حَلٰلًا to give a complete description

steps.m: to protect oneself or be cautious

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Added 'it' and 'ta' to show doing the protecting/being cautious for oneself
Added و at beginning to connect with previous command in ayah (وَكُلُوا۟ - and eat)
Changed to plural command form to address multiple people
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object of اتَّقُوا - fear/be mindful of)
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize the ل and fatha at the end to show Allah is the object being acted upon. The word اتَّقُوا (fear/be mindful of) is acting on Allah in this ayah.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects different parts of a sentence
Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'
The ال changes to ٱلّ with a shadda for easier pronunciation, but doesn't change the meaning
The long vowel mark (ٓ) helps with proper recitation but doesn't change the meaning. This word connects to مُؤْمِنُونَ (believers) in the ayah.
This is the basic pronoun form for addressing someone directly (like saying 'you' in English)
Adding م at the end makes it refer to multiple people. In this ayah, it's specifically referring to the believers who are being addressed about Allah.
This is a basic preposition that shows how something is done or connected
Added هِ to show who/what is being referred to. The small ۦ after هِ is just to show proper pronunciation.
Adding vowels makes this a past tense verb about feeling safe
Changed from a past-tense verb (he trusted) to a noun meaning someone who believes/trusts
Added ون to show multiple believers. This word describes 'you all' (أَنتُم) in this ayah.
Basic negative word that makes the following verb يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ (He holds you accountable) into 'He does not hold you accountable'
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to show mutual or intensive action
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: he holds you all accountable (subject of لَا)
steps.n: Added كُمُ to show the action is done to 'you all'. This word is negated by لَا (not) in the ayah.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make a noun meaning the result of purposeless speech
Adding ال makes it refer to vain talk in general

steps.m: by/with the vain talk (object of يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ)
steps.n: Added بِ meaning 'by/with' and ِ to show this word is connected to يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ (He holds you accountable). The word explains what you're not being held accountable for.
This is a preposition that shows location or position. Here it connects to أَيْمٰنِكُمْ (your oaths) to show 'in your oaths'
Added a long vowel mark (ٓ) to show proper pronunciation when connecting to the next word

steps.m: to make strong promises or swear by something

steps.n: Added vowels to make a noun meaning 'oath' (historically because oaths were sworn with the right hand)
Changed to plural form to show multiple oaths
Added كُمْ to show these are the oaths belonging to multiple people being addressed

steps.m: but/however (contrasts with previous statement لَا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ)
steps.n: This is a basic particle that shows contrast between ideas. Here it contrasts with God not holding you accountable for unintentional oaths versus intentional ones.

steps.m: and however (connects to لَا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ while contrasting with it)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this contrasting statement to the previous idea about unintentional oaths, showing both connection and contrast
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Added alif after first root letter to show interaction between two parties
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and putting ا after خ

steps.m: he holds you all responsible (subject is اللَّهُ)
steps.n: Added كُم to show who is being held responsible. This word is connected to اللَّهُ (Allah) who is doing the action.
The preposition ب shows how something is done or what it's connected to

steps.m: with what (connects to عَقَّدتُّمُ - you made binding)
steps.n: Added ما which makes this refer to 'what' or 'whatever' was made binding in oaths. This connects to عَقَّدتُّمُ (you made binding) in the ayah.
Doubling the middle letter shows doing something with extra strength or emphasis
Added تُمْ at the end to show 'you all' did this action in the past

steps.m: you all made firmly binding (the oaths)
steps.n: The مُ was already there - this is just a spelling variation of the same word with the same meaning

steps.m: to make a promise or swear

steps.n: Added vowels to the root letters to make the word mean 'a promise/oath'
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple oaths
Added ال to make it specific/definite

steps.m: the oaths (object of عَقَّدتُّمُ - you made/confirmed)
steps.n: Added fatḥa at the end to show this is the object of the verb عَقَّدتُّمُ (you made/confirmed)

steps.m: to cover up or make amends for something

steps.n: Added shadda to show doing something with more effort or intensity
Added alif to change from a verb (doing something) to a noun (the thing that does it) - like changing 'erase' to 'eraser'

steps.m: a specific act of making amends (expiation)
steps.n: Added ة to show one specific instance of making amends
Added فَ to show this is the result or consequence of what came before
Added هُ to show possession - this is the expiation for something mentioned before
The basic meaning focuses on providing food rather than eating
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added أ at the beginning to show causing someone else to do the action
Changed the first vowel and added ا after م to make it a noun meaning 'the act of feeding'
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence, specifically what the expiation consists of
Added vowel marks (fatha) to make the root into the number 'ten'
Added ة to make the number feminine, which is used when counting masculine nouns

steps.m: ten (used with feminine/non-human plural nouns, modified by مَسٰكِينَ)
steps.n: Added kasra because this number is being used to count مَسٰكِينَ (needy people) in this ayah

steps.m: one who is needy (because they have no settled place/means)
steps.n: Added مِ at start and ي between second and third letters to create a noun meaning someone affected by the root meaning
Added long 'aa' sound and changed vowels to make it mean 'many needy people' instead of just one

steps.m: poor/needy people (as object of إِطْعَامُ - feeding)
steps.n: Added fatha at end to show this word is receiving the action of feeding (إِطْعَامُ)

This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what it's made of. In this ayah, it connects to the word أَوْسَطِ (average/middle) to explain what kind of food should be given - from the average of what you feed your family.

steps.m: to be in the middle or balanced

steps.n: Root letters connected with fatha vowels (َ) to make the noun meaning 'middle'
Added أَ at the beginning and changed first vowel to sukun to show this is the most/best of something

steps.m: of the most moderate (describing food in this ayah)
steps.n: Added -i sound at the end to show this word is describing/connected to another word (the food)

steps.m: that which (referring to the oaths mentioned in عَقَّدتُّمُ)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that connects what comes before it to what comes after it. Here it refers to the oaths that were made binding.
Added hamza at beginning to show causing someone else to do the action
The ُ at the end shows this is happening now (present tense)
Adding ون makes it plural - now it means multiple people are feeding
The ون ending shows this is talking about multiple people doing the action
Added ي at the end to show 'my family' - the ي means 'my'
The كُمْ ending shows this belongs to multiple people being spoken to
This is a conjunction that gives a choice between equal options. Here it connects different ways to fulfill the oath expiation: feeding the poor OR clothing them.
Changes from verb 'to clothe' into noun meaning 'a piece of clothing'
Shows this word is the subject of the sentence

steps.m: their clothing (part of the expiation options in this verse)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show the clothing belongs to them. This is one of the options mentioned for expiation (كَفَّارَة) in this verse.

steps.m: or (connecting feeding or clothing as options for expiation)
steps.n: This is a coordinating conjunction that connects two equal options for expiation (feeding or clothing). In this ayah, it shows that either feeding 10 poor people OR clothing them would fulfill the requirement.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added shadda to show stronger or more complete action
Changed the verb into a noun that means 'the act of freeing'. This word is part of the list of actions that can be done as expiation (كَفَّارَة) for breaking an oath.
Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make the basic past tense verb 'he watched'
Adding ة changes the verb into a noun referring to something/someone being watched - historically referring to slaves who needed constant supervision, then becoming the general word for slave
Adding tanwin kasra shows this is an indefinite noun (a slave) that is being acted upon by the word تَحْرِيرُ (freeing) in this ayah
Basic question word that asks about people (like 'who' in English), but here means 'whoever' because it's being used in a conditional sentence

steps.m: so whoever (connects to previous rule about feeding 10 poor people)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence of what came before - it introduces what should happen if someone can't fulfill the previous requirement
Basic negative particle that changes the meaning of verbs that come after it to their opposite. In this ayah, it negates the verb يَجِدْ (find) to mean 'does not find'.
Added shadda for emphasis and sukun at the end to connect with the next word. The shadda makes the 'l' sound stronger.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel to ِ. The initial و drops because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: he finds/obtains (in conditional phrase after فَمَن لَّمْ)
steps.n: Changed ending to sukun because it comes after لَمْ in a conditional phrase
Root letters ص-و-م became the noun صَوْم (fasting) by adding the vowels َ and ْ
Changed the middle و to ي and added ا to show it's an abstract concept
Added فَ (meaning 'then/so') at the start, and ُ at the end to show this is the subject
Adds alif to make it a countable number
Adds the feminine marker ة to make it refer to feminine things
Added kasra to show this word is connected to أَيَّامٍ (days) that comes after it. Also, the alif is written as alif with overbar (ٰ) in the Quran
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun
Added hamza with fatha (أَ) at the beginning to start forming the plural pattern
Added shadda to double the middle letter as part of the plural pattern
Added alif to create the common plural pattern for short words
Added the tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this word belongs to the number three (ثَلٰثَةِ) in the phrase 'three days'
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this' for something close by
The alif is replaced with a long alif (ٰ) to show distance, changing the meaning from 'this' to 'that'
The ل with kasra (لِ) is added as a connector before adding pronouns

steps.m: that (referring to what was mentioned before)
steps.n: The كَ is added at the end to make it more specific, pointing to something mentioned before

steps.m: to cover, make amends for, or compensate

steps.n: The doubled ف and added ا turn this into someone who does the action a lot, but here it means 'extremely ungrateful/disbelieving' not 'compensating'
Adding ة turns it from 'one who compensates' into 'an act of compensation'
The ُ shows this word is the subject explaining what 'that' (ذٰلِكَ) is in the sentence
Forms the singular noun meaning 'right hand' which came to mean 'oath' because Arabs would take oaths by raising their right hands
Changes the singular word to plural using the pattern أَفْعَال
Adds kasra at the end to show the word is in genitive case (after a preposition)
Adds كُمْ to show the oaths belong to multiple people being addressed

This is a conditional particle that introduces a time condition. It connects the oath (swearing) to its consequence. In this ayah, it links to حَلَفْتُمْ (you swore) to show when the rules about oaths apply.

steps.m: to make a promise or oath

steps.n: Connected the letters together with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by adding ت at the end
Changed تَ to تُمْ to make the verb plural (you all)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
Changed from 'he protected' to 'protect!' by adding اِ at start and making it a command to one person
Added و at beginning to mean 'and', which changes اِ to ٱ
Added plural command ending وا with damma before it to address multiple people
Forms a noun meaning 'oath' from the root meaning of swearing
Changes the word to plural form to show multiple oaths

steps.m: your oaths (referring to the oaths mentioned in the ayah about expiation)
steps.n: Adds possession to show these are the oaths belonging to those being addressed, specifically referring to the oaths discussed in the context of expiation
The basic demonstrative word ذا (this) becomes ذٰلِ (that) to show something farther away or previously mentioned
Adding كَ at the beginning changes 'that' to 'like that' or 'similar to that'

steps.m: like that (pointing to the previous rules about oaths)
steps.n: Adding كَ at the end makes the word point more specifically to something mentioned before - in this case, the rules about oaths that were just explained
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters

steps.m: to make clear, explain in detail
steps.n: Adding shadda to middle root letter creates Form II intensive meaning - not just being clear but actively making something clear
Added present tense prefix يُ, changed middle vowel to kasra, and added final damma to show it's doing the action (subject)
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Added 'the' to make it specific/definite
Added damma to show this is the subject
Starting with the basic preposition ل which shows direction or purpose

steps.m: for you all (explaining who should be fed in إِطْعَامُ)
steps.n: Added the pronoun كُمْ to show this applies to multiple people being addressed. This connects to إِطْعَامُ (feeding) by showing who should do the feeding.
The weak letter ي changes to ا after fatha, and the second ي becomes part of the word structure
The taa marboota (ة) marks this as a feminine singular noun
The ات suffix creates the regular feminine plural form. This word is the direct object (what is being explained/clarified) of the verb يُبَيِّنُ (He explains/clarifies).
Adding the possessive pronoun suffix ه meaning 'his'. This word is the direct object (accusative case) of the verb يُبَيِّنُ (yubayyinu) which means 'He makes clear/explains'.
This is a basic particle that shows hope or purpose. It's like saying 'hopefully' or 'in order to' in English.

steps.m: perhaps you all/so that you all (connected to تَشْكُرُونَ)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to. This word connects to تَشْكُرُونَ (you give thanks) in the next ayah, showing the purpose: 'so that you all may give thanks.'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past
Changed to present tense by adding تَ at start and changing middle and end vowels
Added ون to show many people are doing this action together

steps.m: to point to or indicate something

steps.n: Root letters أ-ي-ي combined with shadda to make the question word 'which/who'
Added the calling particle يا with extended alif to show we're addressing someone
Added damma at the end to show this is in the nominative case
Adding ها makes it address everyone in a group rather than just one person
This is the basic form that points to someone or something
Added ال with shadda to make it specific and connect it to what comes after

steps.m: those who (plural, refers to believers who are addressed)
steps.n: Changed from 'the one who' to 'those who' by adding ين at the end. This makes it plural to talk about many believers instead of just one.

steps.m: to cause to feel secure, to make safe
steps.n: Adding long alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'

steps.m: they believed (referring to those who believe in الَّذِينَ)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show many people did the action. This word describes the believers mentioned in الَّذِينَ (those who) at the start of the verse.
This is the basic emphatic word that shows something is definitely true
Adding ما changes the meaning from just emphasis to showing that something is limited or restricted. It's like saying 'nothing else, just this'

steps.m: to cover or mix (originally referring to covering/mixing things, later specifically about fermenting drinks)

steps.n: The root letters that mean 'to cover/mix' become the word for wine when you add the vowel marks

steps.m: the wine (mentioned alongside الْمَيْسِرُ/gambling as things to avoid)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it refer to wine specifically as one of the forbidden things in this verse. The ُ at the end shows this word is the subject being described.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb

steps.m: gambling (lit: that which is made accessible)
steps.n: Added مَ at start and changed vowels to show something that results from making things easy - in this case, gambling, because it makes getting money seem easy
Added و (and) to connect this word to previous words in the list
Added ال to make the word definite (specific gambling)
Added ُ to show this word is the subject of the sentence

steps.m: something set up (especially a stone altar)
steps.n: Forms a noun indicating a concrete object that is set up/erected. This word is one of several subjects in a sentence stating that these things are filth/evil.
The hamza prefix here helps form a plural noun meaning multiple stone altars/idols that were set up
The damma (u-sound) at the end shows this word is the subject - the stone altars are being described as something bad
Added definite article to specify these particular stone altars
Added conjunction prefix to connect with previous items in list
Root letters were connected with fathas (a-vowels) to make a noun meaning 'divining arrow'
Forms broken plural pattern أَفْعَال to show multiple arrows
Added و (and) and ال (the) to connect to previous items in list

steps.m: and the divining arrows (subject of رِجْسٌ)
steps.n: Added ُ to show this is a subject noun. The word is one of several subjects of رِجْسٌ (filth/evil) in this verse.
Changes the action into a noun describing something that is unclean. In this ayah, it's describing wine, gambling, stone altars, and divining arrows as being filthy or impure things.
Adds ٌ (dammatan) at the end to show this noun is the subject of the sentence
Added sukun to show the word ends with a stopped 'n' sound. This word connects رِجْسٌ (filth) with عَمَلِ (works/deeds) to show that the filth comes from Satan's works.
Added shadda to show emphasis and assimilation with preceding sounds. This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic concept of working or doing something
Connected the letters with fatha marks to change from the idea of working to the actual work itself
Added kasra at the end to show this work belongs to or comes from Satan (الشَّيْطٰنِ) in this ayah

steps.m: to be rebelliously distant or deviate far away

steps.n: Added letters to create the pattern for someone/something that embodies the meaning of being extremely rebellious or distant
Added 'the' to specify this particular satan/devil

steps.m: of the satan/devil (possessed by عَمَلِ - deeds)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this belongs to or comes from something (in this case, showing these are the deeds of Satan)

steps.m: to keep away from, to avoid

steps.n: Added ا and ت to show doing the action deliberately for oneself

steps.m: you all keep away! (masculine plural command)
steps.n: Adding و makes it a command for a group of men/boys

steps.m: so you all deliberately keep away from it (referring to الْخَمْرُ)

This is the basic particle that shows hope or possibility for something to happen

Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present/future tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يُ with تُ at the start

steps.m: you all succeed/will succeed (addressing multiple people)

This is a special emphasis word that makes statements stronger. It's like adding 'truly' or 'certainly' at the start of a sentence.
Adding مَا changes the meaning from simple emphasis to restriction. In this ayah, it restricts what الشَّيْطٰنُ (Satan) wants (يُرِيدُ) to only specific things.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas
Added يُ at start to show present tense, changed middle vowels, and added ُ at end to show it's doing the action. This word is what الشَّيْطٰنُ (Satan) is doing in this ayah.
Changed from basic root meaning 'being rebellious' to describe a person who is rebellious by adding vowels
Added alif to intensify the meaning, showing someone who is extremely rebellious

steps.m: that he causes to fall/happen (object of يُرِيدُ)

steps.m: between you all (object of يُوقِعَ - to cause to fall)
steps.n: The word 'between' gets 'you all' (كُم) attached to it to show who is being affected. Like saying 'between you all' instead of just 'between'

steps.m: the enmity (that الشيطان wants to cause)
steps.n: Added ال (the) to make it 'the specific hostility', and the ending َ shows it's being acted upon in the sentence

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic noun meaning the feeling of hate
Added اء at the end to show this is a very strong or deep feeling of hatred
Adds 'the' (ال) at the start to make it specific hatred
Adds conjunction meaning 'and'

steps.m: and the intense hatred (object of يُوقِعَ - to cause)
steps.n: Added َ at the end to show this hatred is what Satan wants to cause between you, connecting to the verb يُوقِعَ (to cause) in the ayah

steps.m: to cover or veil something (especially the mind)

The ِ ending shows this word comes after في (concerning) in the phrase في ٱلْخَمْرِ (concerning the wine)

Changes from past tense (he knew) to present tense (he knows) by adding يَ at the start and changing the ending

steps.m: so that Allah may know (subject of لِيَعْلَمَ is اللَّهُ)
steps.n: The letter ل (lam) was added at the start to mean 'so that' or 'in order to', making it about Allah's purpose
Added ال to specify this is THE one true deity, and merged the letters according to Arabic rules. The hamza is dropped and the lam gets a shadda.

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of يَبْلُوَنَّ - He will test)
steps.n: Added the ُ to show Allah is doing the action (testing) in this verse. Also added proper spelling with ٱ at start.
This is a conditional word meaning 'whoever' that introduces a condition about people. In this ayah, it connects to the verb يَخَافُهُ (fears Him) to mean 'whoever fears Him'.
Added basic past tense vowels. The middle و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
Changes from past to present by adding يَ at the start - means 'he fears' now instead of 'he feared'

steps.m: he fears Him (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Adding هُ ('him') at the end shows who is being feared - in this case Allah. The ۥ mark makes the 'u' sound longer.
Changes from an action to a noun - from 'being hidden' to 'hidden thing'
Added 'the' to make it specific

steps.m: with/by the unseen (related to يَخَافُهُۥ - fears Him)
steps.n: Added بِ at start to show 'with/by' and ِ at end because it follows بِ. This describes how someone fears Allah even in matters that are unseen.
This is the basic interrogative/conditional particle meaning 'who' or 'whoever'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before
Added kasra at the end to help connect with the next word (اعْتَدَىٰ)
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make past tense. The final و changes to ا because it's a weak letter.
Added اِعْتَ pattern which shows someone deliberately going beyond proper limits or boundaries
Changed the ending to ى because this verb ends with a weak letter
Small alif helps show correct pronunciation of final vowel
Adding vowels to make it a common noun pattern
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
The alif is replaced with a special mark (alif khanjariya) that extends the pointing distance
Adding لِ changes it to point to something farther away
The كَ is added at the end to complete the demonstrative pronoun
Added fatha to show basic meaning of direction or purpose
Added هُ at the end to show who it's for
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before
Changed from an action (punish) to a thing (punishment) by adding vowels and alif
Added double damma to show this is indefinite (a/an) and is the subject in the phrase فَلَهُۥ عَذَابٌ (for him is a punishment)
Added short vowels (fat-ha and kasra) to make it mean 'painful' instead of 'to hurt'
Added ي to make it an intensive form showing something that causes extreme or intense pain
Added double dammah to make it indefinite - means 'a painful punishment' rather than 'the painful punishment'

steps.m: to point to or direct attention to something

steps.n: The root letters أ-ي-ي combine together with a shadda (double letter mark) on ي to make the question word 'which/what'
Adds damma to show this word is the subject
Adds ها to make it a calling word
Adds يا with a long alif and hamza to call out to the group being addressed
Added ن to make it refer to multiple people or things
Added ال at the beginning with a shadda on the following letter to point to specific people. This word connects to ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) to tell us which people are being talked about.
Adding alif makes it about having faith in something/someone
Added وا at the end to show many people did the action
This is a negative particle that comes before verbs to tell people not to do something. Here it's telling people not to do the action in تَقْتُلُوا۟ (kill/hunt).
Added basic past tense form with fatha marks
Adds ت at start to mean 'you' and changes vowels to show present tense action
Added وا to show multiple people are being addressed. This comes after لا which makes it a prohibition - 'do not kill'
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it a noun referring to what is hunted
Added the definite article ال which becomes ٱل and causes the first letter after it to double (shown by ّ)

steps.m: the prey (object of تَقْتُلُوا - do not kill)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb تَقْتُلُوا (do not kill)
This is a pronoun without a root. It's used to refer to a group of people being addressed. The مْ at the end shows it's plural.
Adding وَ at the start connects this word to what came before it, like saying 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: ones in a sacred state (describes أَنتُمْ - you all)
steps.n: Changed vowels to damma and added tanween to create a plural noun showing people in the state of ritual consecration (ihram). This word describes 'you all' (أَنتُمْ) in the verse.
This is a compound of two particles: و (and) + من (who/whoever)

steps.m: to end life, to kill, to slay

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: he killed it/him (referring to hunting game while in ihram)
steps.n: The suffix هُۥ shows what was killed. In this ayah, it refers back to الصَّيْدَ (the hunted game) that was mentioned earlier. The ayah is explaining what happens if someone kills game while in the state of ihram (pilgrimage).
This is a preposition that shows something coming from somewhere or someone

steps.m: from you all (referring to the believers who are in a state of ihram)
steps.n: Added the pronoun كُم which means 'you all' to show who the 'from' refers to. In this ayah, it's referring to the believers who are in a state of ihram (ritual consecration)
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
مُ at start turns verb into a noun meaning 'person who does the action'

steps.m: intentionally/deliberately (describing how قَتَلَهُۥ 'he killed it' was done)
steps.n: Added emphasis mark (shadda) on م and an -an ending to show it's describing how something was done
Root letters ج-ز-ي became جَزَا (jazaa) - the ي changed to ا at the end to make it a noun meaning 'repayment'
Added hamza with alif to make it a noun meaning the result of repaying
Added فَ at beginning to show this is a result or consequence
Added tanween dammah to show this is the subject of the sentence
Connected the letters together to form the basic word
Added vowel marks to show this is a noun describing the penalty mentioned in the ayah
Word remains the same as previous step since no new changes are needed

steps.m: what (referring to the type of livestock that equals what was killed)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of the sentence. Here it links the compensation (جَزَآءٌ) with what was killed (قَتَلَ). It helps specify which animal from livestock should equal what was hunted.
Added fatha vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word is connected to فَجَزَآءٌ (then a penalty) in the ayah, showing what action leads to the penalty.
Basic preposition showing source, origin, or type. In this ayah, it connects 'similar' to 'livestock' (مِنَ ٱلنَّعَمِ meaning 'from the livestock')
Added fatha at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word that starts with ال. This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
Root meaning 'blessing/comfort' becomes the word for animals that are a blessing by giving food/milk
The -i ending shows ownership or possession of the livestock
Adding ال makes it specific/definite livestock being referred to
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form

Changed to present tense by: 1) adding يَ at start to show 'he' is doing it now, 2) putting sukun on first root letter, 3) changing middle vowel to damma, and 4) adding damma at end to show this is the main verb
Added kasra to the letter ب to show it's a preposition meaning with/by

steps.m: with it/by it (referring to the judgment يَحْكُمُ)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هِ (hi) meaning 'it' to show what is being referred to. This connects back to the judgment (يَحْكُمُ) mentioned in the ayah.

steps.m: with it/by it (referring to the judgment يَحْكُمُ)
steps.n: Added a small alif (ۦ) after هِ as a pronunciation guide to show the vowel clearly
This is a special word that shows possession or ownership. It's like saying 'one who has' in English.
Changed the word to show exactly two people by adding ا at the end and changing the vowel. In this ayah, it's describing two people of justice who will judge.

steps.m: to be equal, balanced, or just

steps.n: Changed the action into a noun that represents the concept itself
Added tanwin kasra to show this is describing the two people (ذَوَا) who will judge fairly
Starting with the basic preposition that shows something coming from somewhere else
Added كُم at the end to show this applies to a group of people being spoken to
Added shadda on م and sukun at end to help with pronunciation. The shadda appears because this word comes after a word ending in ن, causing the ن of مِن to merge with the م

steps.m: an offering (that guides one to righteousness)
steps.n: Changes from the action of guiding to a noun meaning an offering. This connects to the root meaning because such offerings guide people to righteousness. In this ayah, it refers specifically to an animal offered as compensation.
The ًۢا ending shows this is what is being judged in the phrase 'two just people shall judge it'. It's like saying 'they judge an offering' rather than just 'an offering'.

steps.m: to reach or arrive at something
steps.n: This is the basic concept of reaching or arriving at a destination
Added vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past
Changed the word to describe the offering (هَدْيًۢا) that reaches the Kaaba
Changed the alif to an alif with bar on top (آ) for writing style, but pronunciation stays the same
Makes a noun for the special cube-shaped building in Mecca that Muslims pray towards
Adding ٱلْ at the beginning shows we're talking about the specific Kaaba in Mecca, not just any cube-shaped building
The ِ at the end shows this is the destination of هَدْيًۢا (the offering/sacrifice). It's like saying 'the offering goes TO the Kaaba'
This is a connecting word that gives a choice between two options in this ayah: feeding the poor OR fasting an equivalent amount
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters together
The doubled letter and long alif show someone who frequently disbelieves/denies truth, not someone who makes amends

steps.m: a specific act of making amends
steps.n: Adding ة makes this refer to one specific instance of making amends
Adding tanween makes this 'an' act of making amends rather than 'the' act
This pattern changes the verb 'to feed' into the noun 'food' - the thing that is used for feeding

steps.m: food (that feeds the مَسٰكِينَ - poor/needy people)
steps.n: The ُ ending shows this word is the subject/topic being discussed. In this ayah, it refers to food that is meant to feed poor people as part of a religious compensation.

steps.m: to settle, to be at rest

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who is poor/needy (someone who needs to settle somewhere)
steps.n: Added مِ at the start to make it mean 'someone who' and changed vowels to show a person in need of basic necessities like shelter
Added ا after م to make it refer to multiple poor people instead of just one

steps.m: or (connecting كَفَّارَةٌ 'compensation' as an alternative to جَزَآءٌ 'penalty')
steps.n: This is a connecting word (particle) that shows there's a choice between two options. In this ayah, it connects two possible ways to make up for hunting while in ihram: either paying a penalty with livestock or giving food to poor people.

steps.m: to be balanced, fair, or just

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to be just' into the noun 'justice'
The ُ ending shows this word is the subject of its clause.
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show extended distance
Adding ل with kasra (ِ) changes it to point to something far away

steps.m: that (pointing to something far) (refers to عَدْلُ)
steps.n: The ك at the end makes it masculine and completes the demonstrative pronoun. In this ayah, it's pointing to عَدْلُ (equivalent)

steps.m: to fast (abstain from food, drink, and other things)

steps.n: Root letters ص-و-م became the basic noun صَوْم by adding the vowels َ (fatha) and ْ (sukoon)
Changed the middle letter و to ي and added ا to show it's an abstract concept
Added ًا at the end to show this is what's being done in the sentence
Past tense form of 'to taste' - shows someone tasted something in the past
The fatha ending indicates this is in the subjunctive mood, often following particles like لِ (so that)
The fatha ending shows this follows words like 'so that' or 'in order to'
Adding لِ shows this is explaining why something happens - 'in order to' or 'so that'

steps.m: so that he tastes/experiences (the consequence of his deed)
steps.n: The shadda on ل adds emphasis to show this is a definite consequence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added alif after second root letter to create a noun showing the result of being burdensome
Added fatha at the end to show this is what will be 'tasted' (experienced) as mentioned in the verse
This pattern turns the verb into a noun meaning the action itself or result of the action. The word is in the genitive case because it comes after وَبَال in a possession relationship (idafa).
The ِ at the end shows this word is owned by وَبَالَ (consequence) that comes before it
The هِ suffix shows this belongs to him. This word is in the genitive case because it comes after وَبَالَ in a possession relationship, where وَبَالَ owns or possesses أَمْرِ.

steps.m: to forgive, pardon, or erase (something bad)

steps.n: Added fathas to show this is a past-tense verb about something that already happened
Changed the final و to ا because this is a weak verb
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added ال with a special spelling (ٱل) and merged it with the word, doubling the ل with a shadda. The ه at the end gets a damma to show this is the main subject doing something in the sentence.
This is a basic preposition that shows what something is about or related to
Added مَا which asks 'what' about something
The ن and م combine into مّ (with shadda) to make it easier to say. This word asks about past actions mentioned in سَلَفَ (what has passed)

steps.m: to go ahead or pass by
it passed (referring to what happened before)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show this happened in the past. In this ayah, it refers to past actions that Allah has pardoned.
This is a word that refers to any person who does something. In this ayah, it refers to whoever does the action that Allah will take revenge upon.
The و connects this part to what came before, showing another situation being described
Added short vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'he returned' in the past

steps.m: he returned/repeated (to violating the hunting prohibition)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas in a past tense verb

steps.m: to punish severely, to take revenge

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Added letters to show doing the action for oneself
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start, changing middle vowel to ِ (kasra), and adding ُ (damma) at end

steps.m: so he takes revenge (consequence of عَادَ - returns to violation)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this is a consequence of returning to violation mentioned in previous word عَادَ
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added ٱل (the) and changed the word to become the proper name of the One True God. The ُ at the end shows this is the subject doing the action of عَفَا (pardoned/forgave) in this verse.
Basic preposition showing source, origin, or belonging. In this ayah, it's used with the verb يَنتَقِمُ (take revenge) to show who the revenge is taken from.

steps.m: from him (object of يَنتَقِمُ - takes revenge)
steps.n: Added هُ (him) to show who the action affects. This هُ refers back to whoever repeats the offense (وَمَنْ عَادَ). The whole phrase مِنْهُ shows who Allah takes revenge from.
Connected the root letters to form the basic word for deity or god
Added a double L sound (shadda) to change from 'any god' to 'Allah' - the One True God's special name
Added damma at the end to show Allah is doing the action in this sentence
Added و at the beginning to connect this part to what came before it, like saying 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from verb (he was strong) to adjective (mighty/strong)

steps.m: mighty, powerful (describing Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: The tanween shows this is describing Allah's attribute of being mighty and powerful
This is a special word that doesn't come from a typical root. It's one of the basic words in Arabic used to show possession.
Added the vowel mark ُ (damma) to show this means 'possessor of' or 'one who has'. It's part of a phrase meaning 'two people who have justice'.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Added اِنْتَ at beginning to show someone taking action for themselves, like taking revenge rather than just punishing
Changed from a verb (taking revenge) to a noun (revenge itself)
Changed to 'of retribution' by adding the -in ending, and simplified the spelling with a plain alif at the start
Basic past tense verb form - added short 'a' vowels (fatHa) to make 'he made permissible'
Adding shadda (doubling mark) makes the meaning stronger - like saying 'completely' or 'totally' permissible
Changed vowels to show this happened to something rather than someone doing it

steps.m: it was made permissible (subject of لَكُمْ)
steps.n: Changed to passive form 'it was made permissible' by adding أُ at the start. This word connects with لَكُمْ (for you) to show what was made allowed for you.
Added fatha to show this is connected to what comes after it. This word shows who something is being made allowed for in أُحِلَّ (it was made allowed).
Added كُمْ to show this is happening to multiple people being spoken to. The ْ (sukun) shows the word ends here.
The word became a noun meaning either the act of hunting or the animals that are caught. This word is the subject of أُحِلَّ (is made permissible) in this ayah.

steps.m: sea, be vast like an ocean

steps.n: Added vowels to the root letters to make the word for 'sea'
Adding the definite article ال makes the noun definite
The kasra (ِ) at the end shows this word belongs to or is connected to صَيْدُ (catch/hunting), making the phrase mean 'catch of the sea'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he fed' to 'food' by adding alif after ط to make a noun meaning the thing that is eaten. Connected to صيد to mean 'food from hunting'
Adds conjunction prefix و meaning 'and' to connect this word to previous phrase

steps.m: and its food (referring to the sea's food)
steps.n: Adds possessive pronoun suffix meaning 'its/his'. This refers to the food/catch from the sea mentioned in صَيْدُ ٱلْبَحْرِ (hunting of the sea).
Adding the basic vowel marks creates the past tense verb form
Changed from a verb (doing the action) to a noun (the thing that results) by adding alif
The word gets an alif and tanween at the end to show it's receiving the action (it's what is being made allowed). The alif has a special mark above it.
Added fatha to show the vowel sound
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning

steps.m: surely for you all (object of أُحِلَّ)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show this applies to multiple people being addressed. This word shows who benefits from the permitting (أُحِلَّ) in this ayah.
Added a shadda (double mark) on ي to show this person does lots of traveling
Added ة at the end to change it from 'frequent traveler' to mean a group/collection of travelers (caravan)
Added ال at the beginning to point to a specific caravan
Added لِ at the beginning to show it's for the caravan, and ِ at the end because it follows لِ

steps.m: and for the caravan (connected to صَيْدُ ٱلْبَحْرِ - catch of the sea)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous phrase about the catch of the sea

steps.m: to forbid or make something off-limits

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the vowels to show that something received the action instead of doing it
Added a shadda (double mark) to show this is a strong or emphatic forbidding

steps.m: and it was strictly forbidden (connected to أُحِلَّ)
steps.n: Added و with fatha to connect this with the previous statement about what was made permissible (أُحِلَّ)
This is a common preposition in Arabic meaning 'upon' or 'on'. Since it's a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.
The ى changes to ي with sukun when adding a suffix. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect meaning.
The suffix كُمْ shows this is addressing multiple people (you all)
Changes verb 'to hunt' into a noun meaning 'hunting'

steps.m: hunting/catching (subject of أُحِلَّ - was made permissible)
steps.n: Added the ُ mark to show this is the subject of what was made permissible in the verse
Adding the basic vowel mark to help pronunciation
Adding shadda doubles the 'r' sound to make it stronger
Added 'ال' (the) to make it definite. Different from بَرّ meaning 'righteous' - this بَرّ means 'land/earth' and pairs with 'sea' in the verse
This is the basic form of the particle that can have different meanings based on context
Adding the fatha vowel mark. In this ayah, مَا combines with دُمْتُمْ (you remain) to mean 'as long as you remain'
Connected the root letters and added damma and sukun. The middle weak letter و is dropped in this form.
Added تُمْ at the end to show that multiple people (you all) did this action in the past
Added u-vowels (damma) to make the plural form meaning 'people in ihram' (sacred state for Hajj/Umrah)
Added tanween alif to show this word is the object (describing the state you are in)
The ت merged with و making a shadda
Changed to a command for multiple people by adding وا at the end and changing the middle vowels
Changed إِلَه to اللَه, which is a unique name specifically referring to The One True God in Islam

steps.m: Allah (as the object being feared in وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ - and fear Allah)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل to emphasize it, changed initial alif to waṣla form (ٱ), and added fatha at end to show Allah is the object being feared in this phrase
This is the base form meaning 'one who' or 'that which'. It's used to describe or point to something specific.
Adding ال makes it specific, like saying 'the one' instead of just 'one'
The ال changes to ٱلّ with a shadda for easier pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: the one who/that which (connects to إِلَيْهِ)
steps.n: The ي is written as ى with a small alif (ٓ) on top. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning. This word connects to إِلَيْهِ (to Him) in the next part of the verse.
This is a preposition that shows direction or destination
The ى changes to ي with sukun before adding a pronoun suffix. This is just a spelling change.
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'to him'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he gathered' to 'being gathered' - like going from doing to having it done to you
The ون ending shows this is addressing multiple people. The context shows this gathering will be to Allah.
Added fathas to show this happened in the past. This word refers to Allah (اللَّهُ) making/setting something.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters
Added shadda to emphasize the ل and added ُ to show this is the subject doing the action in جَعَلَ (made/placed)
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a basic noun
The ة makes it a feminine noun referring to the specific cube-shaped building
Adding ال makes it refer specifically to the sacred building in Mecca that Muslims pray towards

steps.m: the Kaaba (object of Allah's making in this verse)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows that this is what Allah made (object of جَعَلَ/made)
Changed the verb into a noun by adding vowel marks. This creates the basic noun meaning a place where dwelling happens.
Added ال to make it specific and َ at the end to show it's receiving the action of Allah making/appointing it

steps.m: to prevent access or make something off-limits

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after second root letter to make it into a noun meaning something that is permanently forbidden or sacred
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific
Added fatha at the end to show this word describes 'the month' (ٱلشَّهْرَ) in the phrase 'the sacred month'
Changes from basic root to noun showing state/condition
Adds accusative case ending to show this word is the object

steps.m: as a means of support/maintenance (object of جَعَلَ)
steps.n: Adds a small alif and tanween marks to show this describes what Allah made the Ka'bah to be (like saying 'as a support')

steps.m: to be companionable, friendly with others

steps.n: Changed from a verb about being social into a noun meaning 'people' by adding an alif after the first letter
Adding the definite article ال makes the noun definite
The kasra ending shows this word follows another word or preposition
Added لِ at the beginning to mean 'for' or 'to'. When this combines with ال, the ل gets a kasra (ِ)
Added a shadda to double the ن letter, which is common in this word
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to create the word for 'month' (named because the moon becomes visible each month)
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ش with shadda because it comes after ال
Added وَ at beginning to connect it with previous words, and َ at end to show it's the object being acted upon

steps.m: to prevent access or make something off-limits
sacred (something special that people can't freely access)
steps.n: Added an alif after the second letter to show this is a strong quality. When we add alif here, it makes the word mean something has this quality very strongly.
Adding ال (the) because this word is describing البيت (the House), so they need to match

steps.m: the sacred (describing the House that Allah made)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is connected to البيت which is being acted on by جَعَلَ (Allah made)

steps.m: to guide or show the way

steps.n: Changes from the idea of guiding to a concrete noun meaning a special kind of offering. In Islamic context, this often refers to animals offered as sacrifice during Hajj.
Added the accusative case ending because this word is the object of جَعَلَ (Allah made/established)
Added ال to make it specific
Added و to connect this word with the previous things that Allah established as sacred

steps.m: to decorate or adorn with necklaces

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (to adorn) into plural noun (necklaces) by adding letters in the middle - similar to how 'necklace' becomes 'necklaces'
Added the definite article to specify these particular garlands
Added the accusative case ending to show this is the object of جَعَلَ (made/established)

steps.m: and the garlands/necklaces (as object of جَعَلَ)
steps.n: Added 'and' to connect this with the previous items Allah established
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby
The alif is replaced with a special mark (alif khanjariya) that shows distance
Adding ل with kasra (لِ) reinforces that we're pointing to something far away

steps.m: that (pointing to what was just mentioned)
steps.n: The ك at the end makes it specifically refer back to something just mentioned - in this case, referring back to what Allah made sacred (the Ka'bah, the Sacred Month, etc.)
Basic past tense 'he' form - added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make it 'he knew'
Added ت at the beginning to show who is doing the action, and changed the vowels to show it's happening now
Added وا at the end to show many people are doing the action

steps.m: so that you all may know
steps.n: Added لِ at the beginning to show purpose - why something is being done
This is the basic form of the particle that connects parts of a sentence, like saying 'that' in English

steps.m: that/indeed (emphasizing what follows: اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ - Allah knows)
steps.n: Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more certain. It connects to the next part which talks about Allah's knowledge, emphasizing this fact.
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name Allah, representing the One True God

steps.m: Allah (as the object of تَعْلَمُوا - that you know)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل and fatha at end to show Allah is the object being known in this sentence. The hamza at the start changed to a special connecting hamza (ٱ).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb

steps.m: he knows/understands deeply (what is in the heavens and earth)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end. The sukun on ع helps pronunciation. This verb tells us what Allah knows, connecting to 'ما في السماوات وما في الأرض' (what is in the heavens and earth)

steps.m: what/whatever/that which (refers to things in السَّمٰوٰتِ and الْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun used to refer to non-human things. In this ayah, it's used to refer to everything that exists in the heavens and earth.
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it connects 'what' (ما) with 'the heavens' (السَّمٰوٰتِ), showing what is IN the heavens.
Added vowels to make it into a basic past-tense verb
Added hamza to make it a noun for something high up
Added feminine plural ending. The hamza changes to و before ات
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the س

steps.m: of the heavens (in مَا فِى ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ - what is in the heavens)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because it comes after فِى (in). The alif is written as ٰ in this style.
Basic relative pronoun meaning 'what' or 'whatever'. In this ayah, it refers to everything that exists in the heavens.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it in the ayah
Basic preposition showing location or containment. In this ayah, it connects 'what' (ما) with 'the heavens' (السَّمٰوٰتِ) to mean 'what is in the heavens'
Connected the root letters together and added basic vowel marks to make it pronounceable
Added ال (written as ٱلْ) at the beginning to make it definite, like saying 'the' in English

steps.m: in/on the earth (after فِى in this ayah)
steps.n: Added kasra (ِ) at the end because this word comes after فِى (in/on) in the ayah. In Arabic, words after prepositions like فِى get this ending.
This is a particle that adds emphasis and certainty to what follows it. It's like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English.
Added و at the beginning to connect this with what came before it in the ayah. This connects it to the previous statement about Allah knowing what is in the heavens and earth.
Changed from general word for deity to specific name for Allah by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the object of يَعْلَمُ - knows)
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize the ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added fatha at end to show Allah is the object of the verb 'knows' in this ayah
This is a base word meaning totality or completeness
Added damma and shadda to show this is a complete concept. The shadda doubles the ل to emphasize completeness.
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to شَىْءٍ (thing) that comes after it
Added بِ at the beginning to mean 'with'. This connects to عَلِيمٌ (All-Knowing) to show that Allah is All-Knowing with/of every thing.
Basic root letters that give the meaning of 'thing' or 'object'
Added vowels to the root letters to make it mean 'a thing' - this is a very common word
Added tanwin kasra to show this describes the word كُلِّ (every/all) in the phrase 'of all things'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding ي makes this describe someone who has complete knowledge as their core nature
The tanween (double damma) shows this is describing Allah's absolute knowledge without any limitations
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb
Changed from 'he knew' to 'know!' by adding alif at start and changing the middle vowel to sukoon - this makes it a command
Changed to address multiple people by adding وا at the end and ُ before it. The hamza changed to ٱ due to connection with previous word.
This is the basic form of the word that connects parts of a sentence, like saying 'Know that...' in English

steps.m: that indeed (emphasizes what comes after it)
steps.n: Adding the shadda (doubled letter) makes the meaning stronger, emphasizing what comes after it. Here it emphasizes that 'Allah is severe in punishment'
Changed the root into the basic noun form for 'a deity'
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific deity rather than any deity
Added fatha to show this word is the object in the sentence
Added vowels (fat-ha and kasra) between the root letters to make a word meaning strong/intense
Changed middle letter to ي to make it an adjective meaning 'very strong'
Added ُ to show this describes the punishment (العِقَابِ) mentioned next
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
This form turns the verb into a noun meaning the result of the action. The word is in the genitive case because it is possessed by شديد (severe/strong), forming the phrase 'severe in punishment'.
Adding ال makes it specific. This word is being described as شَدِيدُ (severe) in this ayah.
The word gets a kasra (i) ending because it comes after a word that needs this ending, showing it belongs to or connects with another word
This is a basic connecting word that introduces what comes after it. Here it connects to the word شَدِيدُ (severe/strong) to tell us what we should know.
Added و at the beginning to connect this part to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'

steps.m: Allah (the one true God who is being worshipped)
steps.n: This is the unique name for the One God in Islam. The ال at the beginning and the shadda on ل make it specific and emphasize its uniqueness. The َ at the end shows this word is being acted upon in this sentence.

steps.m: to forgive or cover up faults

steps.n: Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he forgave'
Changed the pattern to show someone who forgives a lot. This describes Allah in this ayah.
Added two small dashes (tanween) to show this word describes Allah
Added vowels and ي to make it into an intensive adjective showing strong quality
Added shadda to emphasize the quality even more
Added two dots on top (tanween) to show this is a complete quality standing on its own
This is the basic form of the word ما which can mean 'what' or 'that which' depending on context.
The shadda (double م) adds emphasis. In this ayah, it's the object of يَعْلَمُ (He knows), so it means 'what' - specifically referring to what people reveal or conceal.
This is a basic preposition showing something is on top of, required of, or incumbent upon something else
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation. In this ayah, it connects to 'the messenger' to show what is required of him.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (sent) to a noun (messenger) by adding و

steps.m: the messenger (referring to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
steps.n: The word gets 'the' (ال) in front, and when 'the' comes before ر, the ل joins with ر to make a stronger ر sound (that's why we see ّ)

steps.m: upon the messenger (referring to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ in مَّا عَلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ)
steps.n: The kasra (ِ) is added because this word comes after عَلَى (upon) in the ayah

steps.m: except (connects ٱلْبَلٰغُ with what came before)
steps.n: This is an exception particle that excludes what comes after it (ٱلْبَلٰغُ - the conveying) from what came before. In this ayah, it shows that the messenger's only duty is conveying the message.

steps.m: to deliver or convey a message

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this is a past tense verb
Changes the verb into a noun that means the act of delivering itself
Added the definite article 'the' to specify this particular act of delivering

steps.m: the conveying (of the message) (subject of إِلَّا)
steps.n: Changed spelling slightly but means the same thing - 'delivering the message'. The word is the main subject after 'except'
This is the proper name of God in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning of divinity and worship, but has become a unique proper noun.
Added shadda on ل to show it is doubled in pronunciation

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of يَعْلَمُ - knows)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in يَعْلَمُ (knows)

steps.m: And Allah (as the subject of يَعْلَمُ - knows)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this statement with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, putting sukun on first root letter, and adding ُ at the end. This word describes what Allah does in this ayah.
This is a relative pronoun referring to things. In this ayah, it's the object of يَعْلَمُ (He knows), referring to what people reveal and conceal.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added تُ prefix to make it 'you' and changed the middle vowel to make it present tense 'reveal' instead of past tense 'revealed'

steps.m: you (all) reveal (what is in your hearts)
steps.n: Added ون suffix to show multiple people are doing the action. This verb appears in contrast with تَكْتُمُونَ (you conceal) in the ayah.
This is a particle word that doesn't come from a root. It asks about something or refers to something general.
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it. In this ayah, it's asking about what people hide.
Basic past tense verb form with fatha vowels
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed from 'he' to 'you' by replacing يَ with تَ at start
Added ونَ to show multiple people are doing the action
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense form

steps.m: say! (command to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to speak to the people)
steps.n: Changed to a command form by adding damma on first letter and dropping the middle weak letter و. This is Allah commanding Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to speak to the people.
This is a negative particle that negates what comes after it. It is an indeclinable particle that does not derive from a root.
Added emphasis by doubling the ل with a shadda. This happens because the word before it (قُل) ends with a similar sound, making the pronunciation stronger.

steps.m: to be equal or level with something else

steps.n: Basic past tense verb showing something became equal or level
Added يَ at start to show present tense, and سْتَ between first two root letters to show seeking equality. This word shows equality between الْخَبِيثُ (the evil) and what comes after it in the ayah.
