steps.n: Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action. In this ayah, Allah is telling عِيسَى how He protected him from the Children of Israel.
When used for people, this root forms words related to sons and descendants
Changed root letters into past tense verb 'he built' by adding the vowel marks
These are different words - 'banaa' means built, while 'bani' means sons/children of. They just look similar but aren't related.
Added the long vowel marker to show this word connects to the next word (Children of Israel)

steps.m: Israel (meaning 'one who struggles/persists with God', name of Prophet Jacob)
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew (ישראל/Yisrael). It was given to Prophet Jacob (يعقوب) after he wrestled with an angel, showing his persistence in seeking God's blessings. The name combines two parts: one meaning 'to struggle/persist' and the other referring to God.

steps.m: away from (preposition related to كَفَفْتُ/I restrained)
steps.n: This preposition shows separation or moving away from something. In this ayah, it's connected to كَفَفْتُ (I restrained) to show who was being protected from harm.

steps.m: away from you (object of كَفَفْتُ/I restrained)
steps.n: The كَ at the end means 'you' (masculine singular), showing who was being protected. This pronoun is attached to the preposition عَن (away from) to show the object of the verb كَفَفْتُ (I restrained).

steps.m: when (referring to the time when Allah held back the Children of Israel)
steps.n: This is a time word that points to when something happened in the past. In this ayah, it's used to remind Prophet Isa about the time when Allah protected him from the Children of Israel.
The root means to come or arrive at a place
The middle letter ي changes to ئ with a sukun (ْ) because it's a special type of word. The first letter gets a kasra (ِ) to show it happened in the past.
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' (speaking to one male) did this action in the past
Added هُم at the end to show the action was done to 'them' (a group of people)
Added shadda (double letter) on ي to make the meaning stronger
Added ة to turn the adjective (clear) into a noun meaning 'a clear sign/proof'
Changed to feminine plural form to show multiple signs
Adding kasra for genitive case
Added ال to show these are specific clear signs that Jesus brought
Added بِ to show these signs were the means by which Jesus came to his people
The middle letter و changes to ا when making the past tense. This is a common pattern for words with و in the middle.

steps.m: then he said (following what was mentioned before about Jesus)
steps.n: Adds فَ at the beginning to show this statement follows after the previous events mentioned about Jesus
Added ن to make it refer to multiple people
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific, like saying 'the ones who' instead of just 'who'
Added shadda on ل and fatha at the end. This word connects to كَفَرُوا (they disbelieved) in this ayah, showing who did the disbelieving.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb. This word is part of what was said after فَقَالَ (then said).
Adds وا at the end to change 'he rejected' to 'they rejected'
Added sukun to show the end of the preposition part

steps.m: from them (referring to بَنِىٓ إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ - Children of Israel)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هُم (them) to show who is being referred to. In this ayah, it refers back to the Children of Israel who were mentioned earlier.
A particle used to introduce a negative statement, specifically used here to emphasize that something is nothing but/only something else. Works with إِلَّا to mean 'this is nothing but/only...'
Basic demonstrative pronoun formed from ha (for attention) and dha (for pointing). The dagger alif (ٰ) extends the ha sound.

steps.m: this (pointing to something in the ayah)
steps.n: The alif is elongated (آ) for emphasis when speaking or reciting, showing clear pointing to something specific. Here it points to what the disbelievers are calling 'magic'.
Basic form of the word that means 'except' or 'only'

steps.m: except (excluding سِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ from what came before)
steps.n: Added shadda and alif to complete the word. In this ayah, it's used to say that what the disbelievers called these signs was nothing except clear magic.
Changed the root into a noun by adding kasra (ِ) on first letter and sukun (ْ) on middle letter. This word is being called 'nothing but magic' by those who disbelieved.
Added two dots (ٌ) at the end to show this is a noun that is the subject - it means 'this is magic'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Added مُ at start to make it mean 'one who makes clear' - like changing 'explain' to 'explainer'
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning
Added the -un sound (tanween) at the end to show this word describes 'magic' in the sentence
This is a time particle that refers to past events. Here it introduces when Allah inspired the disciples.

steps.m: and when (connecting to أَوْحَيْتُ - I inspired)
steps.n: The و at the beginning connects this phrase to previous events mentioned before this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form basic past tense verb
Adding hamza prefix creates causative form IV verb. This is a past tense verb, not affected by case endings.
Changed from 'he revealed' to 'I revealed' by adding تُ at the end
This is a connecting word that shows direction. In this ayah, it shows that Allah's inspiration (أَوْحَيْتُ) was directed towards the disciples (ٱلْحَوَارِيِّۦنَ).
Connected the root letters with vowels to create the basic verb form
Added ا and ِ to make it a broken plural form meaning a group of followers
Added ي (ya) with shadda at the end to make it mean 'one disciple' instead of many disciples
Added the plural ending ون to show multiple disciples
Added the definite article ال to specify these particular disciples

steps.m: that (introduces what was told to الْحَوَارِيِّۦنَ)
steps.n: This is a particle that introduces what someone was told to do. Here it connects 'I inspired to the disciples' with what they were told: 'believe'
Added hamza with alif at start and vowels to make it mean 'he believed' (Form IV verb)
Changed from past tense 'he believed' to present command 'believe!' by changing middle and end vowels
Added وا at the end to make it a command for a group
Added kasra to the letter ب to make it readable
Added ى at the end to show this means 'me'. This word is connected to the verb ءَامِنُوا۟ (believe), showing what to believe in.
Forms the word for messenger or one who is sent

steps.m: messenger, one who delivers messages frequently
steps.n: The و makes it mean someone who does this action as their main role
Adds بِ at beginning to show relationship or connection
Adds ي at end to show it belongs to me
Adds و at beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Added vowel marks (fathas) to the root letters to make it mean 'he said' in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)

steps.m: they spoke/said (the disciples responding to Jesus)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to show multiple people (the disciples) are speaking. This word appears in response to what Jesus said to his disciples.

steps.m: they spoke/said (the disciples responding to Jesus)
steps.n: Added optional pronunciation marks (the small و above the alif and the small circle) that don't change the meaning
Added vowels to connect the letters and show it happened in the past
Adding the long ا changes the meaning from just trusting to actively believing in something

steps.m: we believed (subject of قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Added نَّا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word tells us what was said in the phrase 'they said'
These are the basic letters that give us the core meaning of witnessing or testifying
Adding marks (sukun and fatha) to the root letters to make it a command meaning 'Be a witness!'
Adding hamzat al-wasl at the beginning to make it easier to pronounce
Adding final sukun to complete the command form
Adding و at the beginning to connect this command with what came before it
This is an emphatic particle that introduces a statement with certainty
Added نَا at the end to show this is about 'we/us'

steps.m: that we are (connected to ءَامَنَّا - we believed)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to connect this word to the previous statement ءَامَنَّا (we believed). This shows what they believed.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added مُ at the start to make it mean 'one who does', and changed inside vowels to show it's about submitting to Allah rather than just being safe
Added ون at the end to show this refers to multiple people who submit to Allah
This is a time particle that shows when something happened in the past. In this ayah, it connects to when the disciples 'said' (قَالَ) something to Jesus.
Added vowel marks to show it's in past tense and the subject is 'he'
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
Root letters connected with short 'a' vowels (fatha) to form a noun meaning whiteness
Added ا after second root letter and يّ at end to show someone characterized by this quality
Added ال to make it specific
Added ون to make it plural, referring to the group of disciples speaking to Jesus in this verse
This is a proper name, likely derived from Hebrew/Aramaic origins. In Arabic, it's treated as a complete name without a typical root structure.
The يٰ (yaa) is added at the beginning to show we are calling out to Jesus. This is like saying 'Hey Jesus!' or 'O Jesus!' in English. In this ayah, the disciples are addressing Jesus directly.

steps.m: to build or create something from parts

steps.n: Changed from 'to build' into 'a building/structure' by adding vowel marks to make it a noun

The transformation described is not accurate. 'Ibn' (son) comes from a different root than 'baniy' (built). They are separate words, not a transformation of one to the other. A better note would be: These are two different words that happen to look similar but have different meanings and origins.

steps.m: son (being addressed as 'O son of Mary')
steps.n: The alif at the start changed to a special connecting alif (ٱ) and got a small mark at the end because it's being used to call out to someone as 'O son of Mary'
This is a proper name that comes from Hebrew/Aramaic 'Maryam', referring to the mother of Jesus (عِيسَى). The word has a fatha at the end because it's being addressed in this verse.

steps.m: do? does? (asks a yes/no question before يَسْتَطِيعُ)
steps.n: This is a question word that comes before يَسْتَطِيعُ (is able) to ask if something is possible. It's like adding a question mark at the start of the sentence.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: he is able to (subject of يُنَزِّلَ)
steps.n: Added يَسْتَ at the beginning to show trying or seeking ability. The ُ at the end shows this is the subject doing the action. This word connects to يُنَزِّلَ (to send down) in the ayah, showing what he is able to do.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, raise up

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter ب changes this from an action into someone who does that action intensively
The ُ shows this word is the subject (doer) of the verb يَسْتَطِيعُ (is able)
Adds possessive pronoun to show ownership
This is a connecting word that joins يَسْتَطِيعُ (is able) to يُنَزِّلَ (to send down). It's like saying 'is able TO send down' in English.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
The shadda intensifies the meaning, indicating repeated or intensive sending down

steps.m: that he sends down (object of يَسْتَطِيعُ/is able)
steps.n: Changed to present tense and added subjunctive ending because it comes after أَن (that). This word is what is being 'able to be done' in the question.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'. It's one of the most common Arabic prepositions.
When adding a pronoun after عَلَى, the ى changes to يْ to make it easier to connect the next part.
Adding نَا at the end means 'us' - it includes the speaker and others they're with. In this ayah, it refers to the disciples speaking to Jesus.

steps.m: to provide food or a feast

steps.n: Changed to show the person who gives food by adding alif after meem
Added ة to make it refer to the thing itself rather than the person who provides it

steps.m: a spread of food (being sent down)
steps.n: Added -an ending (tanween) to show this is the object receiving the action
This is a basic preposition meaning 'from'. The kasra helps connect it to the next word.
The shadda adds emphasis to show a stronger connection
The fatha at the end helps connect this word smoothly to the next word السَّمَآءِ (the sky/heaven)

steps.m: to be elevated or rise up

steps.n: Adds اء to make it a noun meaning sky or heaven
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the س letter

steps.m: of the sky/heaven (describing مَآئِدَةً - a table)
steps.n: Added kasra (i-sound) at the end to show this is 'from/of the sky', since it's describing where the table comes from
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this is something that happened in the past. This word is connected to يَسْتَطِيعُ (is able) in this ayah.
This is a common weak letter transformation where و changes to ا in hollow verbs
Changed from root letters to past tense 'he' form by adding vowels
Added reflexive pattern with ت that combines with و to form تّ. The initial ا helps pronunciation.
Changed from 'he protected' to 'you all protect!' by adding وا at the end - this makes it a command to a group
Changed to the unique name for the One True God by adding ال and merging letters
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the command 'fear' (اتَّقُوا)
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects to the next word كُنتُم (you were) to mean 'if you were'
Connected the root letters with fathas to make the basic past tense verb
The middle weak letter و drops out in certain forms, with damma added to first letter and sukun on last letter
Added تُم at the end to show 'you all' (masculine plural) did this action in the past. This word is followed by مُّؤْمِنِينَ (believers) to complete the meaning 'if you all were believers'

steps.m: to feel or give safety and trust

steps.n: Added مُ at the start to show who does the action, like changing 'trust' to 'truster'
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many believers (men)
Added shadda on م and fatha at end because it comes after كُنتُم (you were) in the ayah
Added vowels to connect the root letters together. This is the basic past tense form before the weak letter change.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
The suffix وا changes the subject from 'he' to 'they (masculine)'. This word is connected to نُرِيدُ (we want) in the next part of the ayah, showing who is speaking.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: we want (subject of قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: The word changes from past to present tense. The نُ at the start means 'we'. The middle letter changes from و to ي. This is what 'they' want in the story.
This is a connecting word that shows what they want to do. It connects نُرِيدُ (we want) with نَّأْكُلَ (we eat), like saying 'we want that we eat'
Changed root letters into a present tense verb by adding marks (sukun and damma) on top and bottom
Added نَ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action
Added a fatha at the end to show this is a possibility or wish
Added shadda on ن to add emphasis to the action
This is a basic preposition meaning 'from'. Since it's a particle, it doesn't come from a root.
Added ها at the end to show this means 'from it' where 'it' refers to something feminine (in this case, the heavenly table mentioned earlier in the conversation)
Adds vowel marks to make it a complete verb
Changes from past to present tense and adds feminine 'she' subject
Added نَّ at the end to add emphasis, like saying 'definitely' or 'surely'

steps.m: and it definitely becomes peaceful (by itself)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous phrase

steps.m: to turn over, change direction, transform

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to turn' into the noun 'heart'
Changes singular 'heart' into plural 'hearts' by adding و and damma marks

steps.m: our hearts (subject of تَطْمَئِنَّ - become assured)
steps.n: The suffix نَا is added to indicate first person plural possession (our). The ُ before نَا is added as a connecting vowel. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb تَطْمَئِنَّ (become assured/at rest).
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create past tense form
Added ن at start to show 'we' as subject, and changed vowels to show present tense subjunctive mood (after أن)

steps.m: and we know (subjunctive: and that we know)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this clause with what came before it

steps.m: that (introduces what they want to do)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that introduces what the people want to do. It connects 'we want' (نُرِيدُ) to their specific desires: to eat, for their hearts to be at peace, and to know.

steps.m: indeed/certainly (emphasizes صَدَقْتَنَا - 'you have spoken truth to us')
steps.n: This is a particle that emphasizes that something definitely happened in the past. In this ayah, it emphasizes that the disciples are certain that Jesus has spoken truth to them.
This is the basic meaning of being truthful or honest
Added fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb
Added تَ to show 'you' (referring to Allah) as the one who told truth

steps.m: you told us truth (referring to Allah telling truth to the disciples)
steps.n: Added نَا at the end to show who received the truth (us/the disciples)
Added damma to show basic verb form with weak middle letter و

steps.m: we are/exist (connected to نُرِيدُ - we want)
steps.n: The نَ prefix shows 'we' are doing the action, connecting to the earlier verb نُرِيدُ (we want)
The َ (fatha) at the end shows this is connected to 'that' - it's what we want to be/become

steps.m: and that we be/exist (connected to نُرِيدُ أَن - we want that)
steps.n: Conjunction prefix وَ adds the meaning of 'and' to connect clauses
This is a basic preposition showing position or relationship. In this ayah, it shows the relationship with the table mentioned earlier.
Changed the ending to prepare for attaching the pronoun suffix

steps.m: upon it (referring to the table they want to eat from)
steps.n: Added هَا which means 'it' (feminine) since it refers back to the table (مائدة) which is feminine in Arabic
This is a basic preposition showing origin or source
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word ٱلشَّاهِدِينَ (the witnesses)
This is the basic concept of witnessing or observing something
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra on second letter to make it mean 'one who does the action'
Added ين to make it refer to multiple witnesses

steps.m: the witnesses (of صَدَقْتَنَا - you were truthful to us)
steps.n: Added the definite article 'the' and a shadda on the first letter. This word relates to صَدَقْتَنَا (you were truthful to us) in the ayah, showing who witnessed this truth.

steps.m: he said (subject of عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ)
steps.n: Basic root ق-و-ل became past tense قَوَلَ (he said) by adding the vowel marks. Shows Jesus son of Mary was the one speaking.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
This is a proper name that entered Arabic from Aramaic/Hebrew. In this ayah, Jesus is speaking, as shown by قَالَ (he said) before his name.
The name is followed by ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ (son of Mary) which is a common way of referring to Jesus in the Quran to distinguish him and honor his mother.

steps.m: son (one who continues building the family)
steps.n: Root meaning 'to build' became the noun 'son' - like how a son builds/continues the family line
Added damma at end to show this word describes Jesus. Changed the initial alif to a special form (ٱ) because it comes after another word.
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Aramaic. In this ayah, it's part of the phrase 'Jesus son of Mary' (عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ) which shows Jesus's relationship to his mother.
The fatha (َ) at the end shows Mary is the object being referred to - in this case, she is being referred to as 'son of Mary'
Root letters أ-ل-ه were joined together and given vowels to make the word for 'god' or 'something worshipped'
The alif after ل makes the 'aa' sound longer
Adding ال makes it specific - 'the god' instead of just 'a god'
The ُ shows this word is doing the action (subject)

Changed from 'the god' (ٱلْإِلَٰهُ) to a special way of calling Allah directly by adding مَّ at the end. This مَّ replaces saying 'O' (يا) before Allah's name and is only used with Allah's name to show respect
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures and takes care of others completely

steps.m: our Lord (being called upon by عِيسَى)
steps.n: Adding نَا shows we're talking about 'our Lord'. This is part of a prayer by Jesus (عِيسَى) calling upon God.

steps.m: our Lord (being called upon by عِيسَى)
steps.n: The extra alif (ا) at the end shows this is being used to call out to God in prayer
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added أ at beginning to make it mean 'made something/someone descend' instead of 'descended'

steps.m: cause to descend!/send down! (command to one person)
steps.n: Changed from past tense 'he sent down' to a command 'send down!' by changing vowels and making the end silent
This is a preposition that doesn't come from a root
The ى changes to يْ before adding attachments. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Adding نا means 'us' when attached to a preposition

steps.m: to move back and forth (like a loaded table)

steps.n: Root letters connected with vowels (َ and ْ) to make a noun meaning 'swaying motion'
Changed to active participle form to show who/what does the swaying

steps.m: table with food (literally: that which sways with food)
steps.n: Added ة to specify one instance of a swaying thing, referring to a table laden with food that moves slightly

steps.m: a table with food (object of أَنزِلْ - send down)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the object being sent down in the phrase 'send down to us a table'
This is a preposition that shows something comes from somewhere else. The fatha at the end helps connect it to the next word السَّمَآءِ (the sky/heaven).
The shadda (ّ) is added to help pronunciation when connecting with the next word. It doesn't change the meaning.
Root meaning 'rise up' became a noun for 'sky' by adding alif and hamza at the end
Added ال to make it definite and doubled the س with shadda
Added kasra because it comes after the word مِنَ (from) which requires the following word to end in kasra
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense form
Changed from past tense 'was' to a noun meaning 'being'. The middle و stays because it's an important part of the word.
Added تَ at the beginning to show this is about 'she/it' in the present tense. In this ayah, it refers to مَآئِدَةً (a table) that will be sent down.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or purpose

steps.m: for us (connected to تَكُونُ - it will be)
steps.n: Added نا which means 'us' when attached to other words. This word connects to تَكُونُ (it will be) in the ayah, showing what something will be for.

steps.m: to return or come back repeatedly

steps.n: The kasra changes this from a verb (returning) into a noun (festival) since festivals are recurring events

steps.m: a festival (object of تَكُونُ - it will be)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha to show this is the object of the verb تَكُونُ (it will be) in this ayah

steps.m: to come first/be at the beginning

steps.n: Root letters got short vowels (fat-ha) to form the simple word for 'first'
Doubling the middle letter emphasizes the meaning
Added لِ at the beginning to show purpose or direction
The ل gets a shaddah (doubled) to show stronger connection to the word after it
Added kasra to show this word is owned by what follows
Added نَا to show it belongs to us
Adding vowels changes it to mean 'being in the state of coming last'
Added 'wa' (and) at the start and made it into 'the last one' to pair with 'the first one' mentioned before
Added kasra to show this word will be owned by something

steps.m: and our last ones (connected to أَوَّلِنَا - our first)
steps.n: Added 'our' at the end to show it belongs to us. This pairs with 'our first' (أَوَّلِنَا) earlier in the verse.
The first two root letters combine into a long آ sound, which is common with this pattern. This makes it a noun meaning 'sign' or 'miracle'
Adds ة to make it refer to one specific sign or miracle
Adds two small diagonal lines (ً) at the end to show this is receiving the action
Adds وَ to connect this to the previous phrase about it being a feast day
This is a basic preposition that shows something coming from somewhere or someone
Added كَ at the end to show this is happening to 'you'
Added shadda on م because it comes after a word ending in a vowel in the ayah (مائدةً)
Changes verb into a direct command to someone
Adds conjunction 'and' to connect with previous phrase
Added first person plural object pronoun نَا meaning 'us' to show who should receive the provision. This نَا refers to Jesus and his followers who are requesting the table of food from Allah.
This is a pronoun that doesn't come from a root. It means 'you' when talking to one being with respect. In this ayah, Prophet Isa (Jesus) is addressing Allah directly.

steps.m: and You (addressing Allah as the best of providers)
steps.n: وَ means 'and'. This connects to the previous phrase about providing sustenance, leading into 'and You are the best of providers.'
Added vowels to the root letters to make it mean 'good' as a describing word
The ُ ending makes this word mean 'best of' instead of just 'good' - it connects to 'providers' to say 'best of providers'

steps.m: to provide with what is needed

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to show who does the action
Added ين at the end to show multiple providers
Added ال at beginning to make it specific and doubled the ر with shadda. The word follows خَيْرُ (best) in the ayah, making it mean 'best of the providers'.
Added fathas to make the basic past tense form. This word is connected to what comes after it, where Allah says 'Indeed, I will send it down.'
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel patterns
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject
Adding a shadda makes this word stronger - changing it from 'if' to 'indeed'. This makes what comes after it more certain.

steps.m: indeed I (speaking before مُنَزِّلُهَا/I will send it down)
steps.n: The ي at the end shows this is about 'I/me'. The kasra (ِ) comes before ي. In this ayah, Allah is speaking about what He will do.

steps.m: to send down repeatedly (subject of قَالَ)
steps.n: The shadda shows this is done multiple times or with emphasis. This word relates to what Allah said (قَالَ).
Changes نَزَّلَ (he sent down) to mean 'one who sends down' by adding مُ at start and changing vowels - like changing 'teach' to 'teacher'

steps.m: I will send it down (object is عَلَيْكُمْ - upon you)
steps.n: Adds ُ to show this is doing the action, and هَا meaning 'it' to show what is being sent down. Works with عَلَيْكُمْ to tell us who receives it.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'upon' - it comes from the concept of being high/elevated
The ى changes to يْ when we add something to the end of the word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't change the meaning.
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to
Combines 'so' (ف) with the question word 'who' (من) to mean 'whoever'
Added fathas to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past
Adding يَ at start shows this is happening now. The vowels change to show ongoing action.

steps.m: he rejects/is ungrateful (in a conditional phrase)
steps.n: The sukun at the end shows this is part of a conditional statement (whoever/if someone)
Basic concept of distance or being after something
Connected the letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the basic word meaning 'after'
Added damma at the end to show this word is in the nominative case
Basic preposition meaning 'from'

steps.m: from you all (referring to those who disbelieve after the table is sent down)
steps.n: Added pronoun 'you all' (كم) to show who is being referred to. In this ayah, it refers to those who might disbelieve after Allah sends down the table of food.
This is the base emphatic particle that adds certainty to statements
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before
Added ي at the end to show this is about me (first person)
Added the small alif (ٓ) after ى which is just a writing convention and doesn't change the meaning
Basic meaning of causing pain or punishment
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the punishment more intense
Changes to first person singular present tense

steps.m: I punish him severely (object of عَذَابًا)
steps.n: Added هُ to show who receives the punishment. This word is the object of the verbal noun عَذَابًا (punishment) in this ayah.

steps.m: to punish severely or cause pain
steps.n: Basic meaning of causing pain or punishment
Changed the verb (to punish) into a noun (punishment) by adding alif after ذ

steps.m: punishment (object of أُعَذِّبُهُ - I will punish)
steps.n: Shows this is what's receiving the action in the sentence 'I will punish with a punishment'

steps.m: a punishment (that I will punish with)
steps.n: The ًا ending makes it 'a punishment' (undefined) instead of 'the punishment' (defined)
This is the basic negative particle that means 'no' or 'not'
The shadda (ّ) adds emphasis to show this is a strong denial or negation
The elongated alif (آ) adds extra emphasis in pronunciation and shows this is a complete and final negation
Basic meaning of causing pain or punishment
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
Adding shadda makes the punishment more intense
Changes to first person future tense
Added هُ to show who receives the punishment, with ۥٓ marks for pronunciation
Basic concept of oneness or uniqueness
Connected letters with fatha marks to form a noun meaning a single person or thing
Added -an ending (tanween) to show this is the object receiving the action - it means 'anyone' as the one being punished
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added shadda for emphasis, common when followed by ال or similar sounds
Added fatha at the end to connect with the next word العٰلَمِينَ (the worlds)
Basic concept of knowledge or awareness
Added alif after first letter and vowels to make a noun meaning 'world' - something that can be known or understood
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many worlds, not just one
Added ال at beginning to make it specific and َ at end because it comes after مِنَ (from/of). The alif is written as ٰ which is just a different way to write it.
This is a time word that helps tell stories about the past. It's like saying "Remember when..." in English

steps.m: and when (introducing what Allah said to Jesus)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this to the previous story. This word introduces an important conversation between Allah and Jesus that happened in the past.
Added fathas to show this is a past tense verb meaning 'he said'
The middle letter و changes to ا because it comes after fatha - this is a common Arabic spelling rule
Changed to the specific name for The One True God by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of قَالَ - He said)
steps.n: Added a shadda (double L sound) and a damma (u sound) to show Allah is the one doing the action (saying)
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Aramaic 'Yeshua', with no Arabic root
The يٰ with a long alif (ٰ) is added at the beginning to show we are calling out to Jesus. This word is being spoken by Allah as shown by قَالَ ٱللَّهُ (Allah said) before it.
This root gives the basic idea of building or constructing, which connects to the idea of a son being built from his parents
The root letters combine with اِ at the start to make the word for 'son' - one of the most common words in Arabic
The word 'son' gets a fatha (َ) at the end because it's receiving the action. The first letter changes from اِ to ٱ which is just a different way of writing it.
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation: fatḥa on م and sukun on ر
The fatḥa at the end shows this is the object of ابن (son of) in the phrase عيسى ابن مريم (Jesus son of Mary)
This is a pronoun with no root. It is being used to address Prophet Jesus (عيسى) in this ayah.
The hamza with fatha (ءَ) at the beginning changes this into a question. This is part of Allah asking Prophet Jesus if he told people to take him and his mother as gods.
Changed the root into a command by adding damma (u) and sukun (silent mark) - like saying 'Say it!'
The weak letter و is removed to make the command easier to say
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' did this action in the past. This word is what Allah is asking Jesus about.

steps.m: to be social, friendly with others

steps.n: Root means 'to be social' - adding ا in middle makes it mean 'people who are social beings'
Added لِ at beginning to show direction or purpose
Added ال to make it specific and shadda on ن because of combining ل with ال
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense
Changed first letter to ا and added ت with shadda to show doing something for oneself
Changed vowel to ِ to help make it present tense
Added damma (ُ) to show it's present tense
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action
Added ني ('me') at the end to show who is being taken. The ني becomes just ى when connected to the verb.

steps.m: to be a source or origin

steps.n: The damma vowel mark helps create the noun meaning
Added ي at the end to show possession (my)
Added و at beginning to mean 'and', and fatha on final ي

steps.m: to take as a god, to worship

steps.n: Changes from a root meaning 'worship' into a noun meaning 'something that is worshipped'
The alif is elongated to show a long 'aa' sound
The suffix يْنِ shows this means exactly two and is being acted upon. This word is what people are being asked to take (object) in the phrase 'Did you tell people: Take me and my mother as two gods?'
This is a preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. Here it connects with the word دُونِ to mean 'besides/other than'

steps.m: below or less than something else

steps.n: Adding damma (ُ) changes it from root to usable word
Added kasra (ِ) at the end to show this connects to the word after it (اللَّهِ)
Added shadda to show emphasis of the letter ل
Added kasra at the end to show this is the object of the word دُونِ (besides/other than)

steps.m: he said (subject of قَالَ ٱللَّهُ - Allah said)
steps.n: The root letters ق-و-ل got vowels (َ) added to make قَوَلَ meaning 'he said'. This is how we show it happened in the past. It's part of the phrase 'Allah said'.

steps.m: he said (subject of قَالَ ٱللَّهُ - Allah said)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change in Arabic verbs with و in the middle.

steps.m: to be free from any flaw or imperfection

steps.n: Changes the concept into a special word that declares something's absolute perfection and glory, often used to praise Allah

steps.m: Your absolute perfection and glory! (addressing Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Adds كَ to show we're addressing Allah directly, expressing amazement at His perfection. This is Jesus's response when Allah asks him about what people claimed.

steps.m: that which (object of أَقُولَ - I say)
steps.n: This is a special word that doesn't come from a root. Here it connects what is being said to the verb 'say', showing what Jesus cannot say.
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels

steps.m: he is/will be (in response to قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added يَ at start to show present/future tense, changed middle vowel to ُ for this type of verb, and added ُ at end to show it's the subject. The و stays because it's a root letter.
Shows ongoing/future action 'to be', connected to someone saying something
Added kasra to the ل to show it's connecting to something after it

steps.m: for me, belonging to me (object of لَيْسَ - is not)
steps.n: Added ى (first person pronoun 'me/my') with elongation mark to show possession. This word is the object of لَيْسَ (is not) in this ayah, showing what does not belong to the speaker.
This is a connecting word that joins with the verb أَقُولَ (I say). It shows what someone is not allowed to say, like in English when we say 'It's not for me TO say that.'
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense form
Changes from past tense 'said' (qāla) to present tense 'saying' (qūlu) by switching middle vowel from 'a' to 'u'
Added أَ at beginning to show first person singular
Added fatha at end to show subjunctive mood
In this ayah, مَا is being used with يَكُونُ (to be) to express that something is not proper or befitting. Jesus (peace be upon him) is saying 'it is not proper for me' to make such a claim.

steps.m: is not (negates يَكُونُ in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a special negation verb that only appears in past tense form but gives present meaning. In this ayah, it's negating يَكُونُ (to be), saying something 'is not' appropriate or right.
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'for' or 'to'
Added ى at the end to show this means 'me'. This word connects to يَكُونُ (it is) to say 'it is for me' or 'I have'.
Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to make it a noun meaning 'truth'
Added tanween kasratayn to show this is indefinite and affected by the preposition

steps.m: with/by a truth (describing how عِيسَى responded)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show this describes how Jesus responded - with truth

steps.m: if (introduces condition for قُلْتُهُ - I said it)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects two parts: 'if I had said it' (the condition) with 'then You would have known it' (the result).
This root represents the basic concept of existing or being in a state
Added basic past tense vowels to form the simplest past tense verb
The middle weak letter و drops out, and vowels change due to this being a hollow verb (verb with middle weak letter)
Added تُ at the end to show that 'I' did this action in the past
The middle weak letter و becomes ا in past tense Form I verbs
Changed from 'he said' to 'I said' by adding تُ at the end
Added هُ as a direct object pronoun meaning 'it'. This word appears in response to a question about what was said, referring back to the statement mentioned.
This particle adds emphasis or certainty to the past tense verb عَلِمْتَهُۥ (You knew it) that follows it
The فَ means 'then' or 'so', showing this follows from the previous statement 'if I had said it'
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' did this action in the past
Added هُۥ at the end to show what was known (like saying 'it' in English)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb

steps.m: you know (what is in نَفْسِى - myself)
steps.n: Added ت at start to show 'you' are doing it, changed vowels to show it's happening now, and added ُ at the end to complete the present tense form. This word is connected to 'ما في نفسي' (what is in myself) in the ayah.
This is a relative pronoun meaning 'what' that connects to the verb أَقُولَ (I say) in this ayah. It introduces what is being said.
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. In this ayah, it's used to talk about what is within someone's self/soul.

steps.m: in (what is in نَفْسِكَ - your self)
steps.n: The ى is added to complete the preposition. In this context, it's used with نَفْس (self/soul) to talk about what is within someone's inner self.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun meaning 'self' or 'soul'
Added ى with kasra (ِ) to show possession ('my'). This word is being acted upon by the verb تَعْلَمُ (You know) in this ayah.
Added fatha to show clear pronunciation of the negation particle
Added alif with madda to show lengthened pronunciation

steps.m: and not (connected to أَعْلَمُ - I know)
steps.n: Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase with what comes before it. This connects 'I do not know what is in Your self' with the previous statement.
Basic vowels (fat-ha) added to make past tense 'he knew'
Changed vowels to show present tense and added sukun on first letter

steps.m: I know (in response to تَعْلَمُ - you know)
steps.n: Added أَ at beginning to show 'I' am doing the action. This word appears in response to تَعْلَمُ (you know) in the conversation.
This is a question word meaning 'what'. In this ayah, it is the object (thing being spoken) in the phrase 'that I should say what is not my right'
This is a basic preposition that shows location or position. In this ayah, it's used with نَفْسِى (myself) to mean 'in myself' - showing what is contained within something.

steps.m: to breathe, to be precious, to be a self

steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this self/soul belongs to something
The كَ at the end means 'your' (when talking to one male person)
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' in English.

steps.m: indeed you (addressing Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show this is talking to someone (in this case, Allah). This connects to عَلَّامُ (All-Knower) in the next part of the ayah.

steps.m: you (subject of قُلْتَ - did you say)
steps.n: This is a standalone pronoun used to refer to one male person you're talking to. In this ayah, Allah is asking Jesus if he told people to take him and his mother as gods.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding shadda (doubling) to the middle letter changes 'know' to 'teach'
Adding alif after the doubled letter changes this from 'taught' to 'one who teaches a lot' - like changing 'taught' to 'master teacher'
The damma (u-sound) at the end shows this is Allah's name/title as the main doer in the sentence

steps.m: to be absent or hidden from sight

steps.n: Changed the root into a plural noun by adding vowels and و, making it mean many hidden things instead of just one
Added ال to show we're talking about specific hidden things, not just any hidden things

steps.m: of the hidden things (possessed by عَلَّامُ - All-Knower)
steps.n: Added ِ at the end to show this word belongs to عَلَّامُ (All-Knower), forming the phrase 'All-Knower of the hidden things'
This is a negative particle that comes before a past tense verb to negate it. Here it negates قُلْتُ (I said), making the meaning 'I did not say'.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
The middle letter و drops out and the vowels change due to it being a hollow verb (has و in middle). This is a common pattern in Arabic.
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action. This word connects to لَهُمْ (to them) in the ayah.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or purpose

steps.m: to/for them (referring to what Jesus said to his people)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هُمْ which means 'them' (for a group of males or mixed group). This connects to the previous words مَا قُلْتُ (I did not say) to show who Jesus didn't say something to.

steps.m: except (what follows is excluded from ما قُلْتُ 'what I said')
steps.n: This is a particle that shows exception - it tells us that what comes after it is not included in what was mentioned before. Here, it shows that Jesus did not say anything to his people except what Allah commanded.

steps.m: what (object of أَمَرْتَنِى - you commanded me)
steps.n: Added fatha to show this is a question word asking about what was commanded. In this ayah, it's asking about what Allah commanded Jesus to tell his people.

steps.m: what (object of أَمَرْتَنِى - you commanded me)
steps.n: Changed the spelling to use آ instead of ا. This is just a writing style used in the Quran and doesn't change the meaning or pronunciation.
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added ت with sukun on ر to show 'you' did this action
Added نِى at the end to show the command was given to 'me'
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning with/by
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'it/him'
Added small alif (ۦٓ) after ه as a pronunciation guide to show the vowel is elongated
This is a basic connecting word (particle) that introduces what someone said or commanded. It's like quotation marks in English.
Added kasra (ِ) at the end because it connects to the command that follows (اعْبُدُوا meaning 'worship'). This is like adding a colon before quoting what someone said.
Connected the root letters with fathas to make it a past tense verb
Changed from past tense 'he worshipped' to present tense 'I worship' by changing the vowel marks
Added the command prefix alif
Added plural command ending to show it's addressed to multiple people
Added emphasis by doubling the ل letter

steps.m: Allah (as the object of اعْبُدُوا - worship)
steps.n: The word 'Allah' gets a fatha mark at the end because it's receiving the action of worship

steps.m: to care for and help grow

steps.n: Doubling the ب shows intensity of caring/nurturing
Added kasra under ب to prepare for the possessive ending
Added ى at the end to show this is 'my' Lord. In this ayah, Jesus is speaking about Allah as his Lord.
Root letters combined with shadda to make 'lord' - someone who takes care of others
Added كُم at the end to show the lord belongs to multiple people being spoken to
Added وَ at beginning to connect this word to what came before it, like saying 'and'. Also added sukun at the end to show it's a complete word.

steps.m: I was (speaking about being a witness)
steps.n: The middle letter و disappeared because it's a weak letter, and تُ was added to show 'I' did this in the past. In this ayah, Jesus is speaking about being a witness over his people.
The conjunction وَ is added to connect this clause with the previous one
This is a preposition showing something being above or over something else. It doesn't come from a root.
The ى changes to ي with a sukun when adding a suffix
Added هِمْ which means 'them' (for a group of men or mixed group). This word is connected to شَهِيدًا (witness) in the ayah - it shows who is being witnessed.

steps.m: to witness or observe something directly

steps.n: Adding vowels makes this a past tense verb meaning 'to witness'
Changed from past tense verb 'witnessed' to noun 'witness' by adding ي in the middle

steps.m: a witness (describing Jesus's role watching over his people)
steps.n: Added tanween alif to show this describes Jesus's role in the sentence. This connects to كُنتُ (I was) earlier in the verse.
This is the basic form of the word that shows duration of time in this context. It connects two parts of a sentence to show one action happening during another.

steps.m: while/as long as (connecting دُمْتُ - I remained)
steps.n: The shadda emphasizes the 'm' sound. Here it connects with the verb دُمْتُ (I remained) to show how long something lasted.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he remained' to a command meaning 'you remain!' by dropping the middle letter and changing vowels

steps.m: I remained (while I was فِيهِمْ among them)
steps.n: Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this action in the past. This word connects to فِيهِمْ (among them) in the ayah to show how long Jesus remained with his people.
This is a preposition that doesn't come from a root

steps.m: among them (referring to the people Jesus was sent to)
steps.n: Added هِمْ which means 'them' (masculine plural). In this ayah, it refers to the people Jesus was sent to guide.
This is a basic particle meaning 'when' that shows something happened after another thing. In this ayah, it connects two events: Jesus being among his people, and what happened after Allah took him.

steps.m: then when (connects تَوَفَّيْتَنِى with what came before)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. It connects 'when You took me' with what was mentioned before about being a witness over them.

steps.m: to complete or take something fully

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger
Adding تَ at the beginning shows doing the action to or for oneself
Adding تَ at the end shows 'you' did this action in the past
Adding نِى at the end shows the action was done to 'me'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
The middle weak letter و drops out and changes the vowels according to regular rules for hollow verbs

steps.m: you were (speaking to Jesus in this ayah)
steps.n: Added تَ at the end to show 'you' (masculine singular) did this action in the past

steps.m: you (masculine singular, subject of كُنتَ - you were)
steps.n: This is a personal pronoun that doesn't come from a root. In this ayah, it's the subject of كُنتَ (you were) showing who was watching over them.

steps.m: to watch over carefully and protect

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed pattern to show someone who does this action as their role or quality
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ر with shadda because it comes after ال
Added fatha at end to show this is the object of كُنتَ (you were) in this ayah
This is a preposition that doesn't come from a root
The ي is added to connect the preposition عَلَى with attached pronouns

steps.m: upon them (referring to the people Jesus was sent to)
steps.n: عَلَيْ (on/upon) + هِمْ (them) = upon them. هِمْ is added to show it's happening to multiple people
This is a standalone pronoun - it doesn't come from a root. Here it emphasizes that 'you' (Allah) specifically were the watcher.

steps.m: and you (subject of كُنتَ - you were)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it. This word emphasizes that Allah specifically (not anyone else) was the watcher over them.

This is a preposition that shows something is on top of, over, or upon something else. In this ayah, it connects the words كُلِّ شَىْءٍ (every thing) to شَهِيدٌ (witness), showing that Allah is witness over all things.
This is a special word that shows totality - it doesn't come from a root
Added damma and shadda to make the basic word meaning 'every/all'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to the word after it (شَىْءٍ meaning 'thing')
Root letters put together to make the common word for 'thing'
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) to show this word is indefinite (like 'a/any thing') and is owned by the word كُلِّ (every/all) that comes before it
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed the word to show someone who does the witnessing, like changing 'witness' to 'witness-er'

steps.m: if (followed by تُعَذِّبْهُمْ - you punish them)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it starts a phrase saying 'if you punish them...' showing what would happen in that case.
Added shadda (double letter) to show the punishment is strong or repeated
ت at start shows 'you' are doing the action
The sukun at the end makes it jussive mood
Added هُمْ at end to show who receives the punishment. This word comes after إِن (if) in the ayah, making it conditional.
Starting with the emphatic particle إِنَّ which adds certainty to what follows
Adding فَ at the beginning shows this follows from what came before in the ayah (if You punish them)

steps.m: then indeed they (referring to those being punished)
steps.n: Adding هُمْ at the end refers back to the people mentioned in تُعَذِّبْهُمْ (if You punish them)
Changed the internal vowel pattern to make it plural
Added the possessive pronoun كَ (your) and nominative case marker ُ because it's the predicate of إنّ

steps.m: if (introduces the condition 'if You forgive them')
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a new possibility. In this ayah, it starts the condition 'if You forgive them'.

steps.m: and if (connects to previous statement 'if You punish them')
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with the previous statement about punishment, showing these are two possible alternatives.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start to make it 'you' in present tense instead of 'he' in past tense

steps.m: you forgive (in response to إِن 'if' before it)
steps.n: The sukun at the end shows this is affected by the word إِن (if) that comes before it in the ayah
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning
Added the basic singular masculine pronoun

steps.m: for/to them (referring to عِبَادُكَ - Your servants)
steps.n: Changed from singular to plural by adding م with sukun. This refers back to 'Your servants' mentioned earlier in the verse.
Starting with the emphatic particle إِنَّ which adds certainty to what follows
Added the pronoun ك with fatha to show this is talking about 'you'

steps.m: then indeed you (are) (connected to عِبَادُكَ - Your servants)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement about 'Your servants', showing a logical result
This is a basic personal pronoun meaning 'you' when talking to one male person. It's not built from a root - it's a complete word by itself.
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce it correctly. In this ayah, this word is connected to ٱلْعَزِيزُ (The Mighty) - it's saying 'You are The Mighty'.
Added vowels and ي to show this is someone/something that has this quality very strongly
Added ال to make it specific/definite

steps.m: the mighty, powerful one (subject of أَنتَ)
steps.n: Added ُ to show this is the subject (predicate) of أَنتَ (you are) in this ayah

steps.m: one who is perfectly wise (attribute of أَنتَ - You)
steps.n: Forms an intensive adjective meaning 'one who possesses complete wisdom'. This word describes Allah (أَنتَ) in this ayah.
Adding ال makes this a definite title referring specifically to Allah's attribute of perfect wisdom
Changed the root letters ق-و-ل into the past tense 'he said' by adding the vowels. This word introduces what Allah is saying about the Day.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' by adding vowel marks
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject
Basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby (masculine singular)
Added ه at the beginning to draw attention, like saying 'look, this' or 'here, this'
Added alif with overbar (ٰ) and fatha marks to complete the word. In this ayah, it's pointing to 'Day' (يَوْمُ) that comes right after it.
Connected the letters together with vowel marks to form the basic noun for 'day'
Added damma at the end to show this word is the subject (doer) of the verb يَنفَعُ (benefits) in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb

steps.m: he benefits/helps (subject of يَوْمُ - this day)
steps.n: Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end - shows what happens on this day
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the word to describe someone who has this quality
Made the word plural to talk about many truthful people

steps.m: the truthful ones (object of يَنفَعُ - benefits)
steps.n: Added 'the' at the beginning and a mark at the end to show this word is receiving the action of 'benefiting'

steps.m: truth (the thing that helps the truthful people يَنفَعُ ٱلصَّادِقِينَ)
steps.n: Changed the word from an action (telling the truth) to a thing (truth itself). This truth is what will benefit the truthful people on that Day.

steps.m: their truth (referring to the truthful people ٱلصَّادِقِينَ)
steps.n: Added ُهُمْ at the end to show this truth belongs to the truthful people mentioned earlier in the ayah
Added fatha to show the basic vowel of this preposition
Added هُ which means 'him/his' to show who the preposition points to

steps.m: to/for them (referring to الصَّادِقِينَ - the truthful ones)
steps.n: Added م with sukun to change 'him' to 'them', showing this refers back to the truthful ones mentioned earlier in the verse

steps.m: to cover or hide (like how a garden is covered with plants)

steps.n: Root letters got vowels and middle letter doubled (shadda) to show complete covering
Adding ة makes it into a noun for a place that is covered
Adding ات makes it plural to show many gardens
Adding ٌ shows these gardens are doing something (they have rivers flowing under them)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Changed to feminine form with ت because it refers to جَنَّاتٌ (gardens) in the ayah
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects the verb تَجْرِى (flow) with تَحْتِهَا (beneath them) to show the rivers flow from beneath.
Connected the root letters together with vowel marks to form the basic word for 'under'
The word gets an -i ending because it owns/possesses something (like saying 'under OF the gardens')

steps.m: beneath it (referring to gardens which rivers flow under)
steps.n: Added ها which refers back to gardens (جَنَّاتٌ) that the rivers flow under
Added vowels to make the basic noun for 'river'
Changed to plural by adding أ at the start and ا after ه to make it 'rivers' instead of 'river'
Added ال to make it specific rivers
Added ُ to show this is the subject of the verb تَجْرِى (flows/running) in this ayah. The ا became ٰ which is just a writing variation.
Changes from verb 'to last' to noun meaning 'lasting one'
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about many people (like adding -s in English)

steps.m: those lasting forever (describes how they are in جَنَّاتٌ/gardens)
steps.n: Added َ (fatha) at the end because it shows how they will stay in the gardens forever - like saying 'living eternally' to describe their state
Basic preposition meaning 'in'
Added ها which refers back to the gardens (جَنَّاتٌ) mentioned earlier in the verse
Extended alif (آ) is used instead of regular alif (ا) at the end of words for emphasis and proper pronunciation
Connected the letters with fatha to form the basic noun

steps.m: forever (describing how long they will stay in جَنَّاتٌ)
steps.n: Added an alif and tanween (two damma marks) to make it mean 'forever' as an adverb - tells us how long they will stay
Basic past tense verb form for feelings/emotions
The ي changes to ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger. This word connects to عَنْهُمْ (with them) in this ayah.
Added ال and merged it with the word, changing إ to ل. This special merging only happens with this word to show it refers to the One True God.
Added shadda on ل to emphasize it, changed initial alif to hamzat wasl (ٱ), and added ُ to show this is the subject doing the action of قَالَ (said)
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation of the preposition
Added the pronoun هم (them) to show who the preposition refers to. This word connects to رَضِىَ (he was pleased) to show who Allah was pleased with.
Added vowels to connect letters and show past tense. The weak letter و changes to ي in this form.
Changes from 'he' to 'they' by adding وا at the end
Adds و with fatha to connect this word with what came before it in the verse
Basic preposition showing separation or movement away from something. The sukun (ْ) is added to show where the word stops.

steps.m: about him (referring to Allah being pleased with them)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هُ (him) to show who the preposition relates to. This connects to رَضِىَ (being pleased) in the ayah.
Basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something close
The alif with bar (ٰ) extends the meaning to show distance
Adding لِ reinforces the distance meaning

steps.m: that (pointing to the great success mentioned next)
steps.n: The كَ at the end makes it specific to what's being pointed to. Here it points forward to 'the great success' (الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ) mentioned next.
Changed the verb (achieving success) into a noun (the success itself)

steps.m: the success (the ultimate achievement mentioned in this ayah)
steps.n: Added ال to show this is THE specific success being discussed - the ultimate success of entering Paradise that Allah mentions in this ayah

steps.m: to be large/great in size, power, or rank

steps.n: Added ي between ظ and م and vowels to form an adjective describing the triumph mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: the great, the mighty (describing الْفَوْزُ - the triumph)
steps.n: Adding the definite article makes the adjective definite

steps.m: the great, the mighty (describing الْفَوْزُ - the triumph)
steps.n: The ُ (damma) shows this adjective matches the case of الْفَوْزُ which it describes
This is Allah's proper name. The shadda (ّ) shows emphasis on the ل letter.
Added لِ at the beginning to show this belongs to or is for Allah. The initial ا is dropped when لِ is added.
Added ِ at the end to show possession. In this ayah, this word shows that Allah owns مُلْكُ (the dominion/kingdom) of the heavens and earth.
Changed the verb into a noun meaning the area or thing being ruled/controlled
Added ُ to show this is the subject that belongs to Allah in the phrase 'to Allah belongs the dominion'
Transforms root meaning 'rise up' into the noun 'sky' by adding اء ending
Changed سَمَاء to سَمَاوَات by adding وات at the end to make it plural (more than one heaven)
Added 'the' and doubled the س to show it's specific heavens
Added kasra at end because this word is owned by مُلْكُ (dominion/kingdom) in the phrase مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ (dominion of the heavens)
Connected the root letters together and added basic vowel marks to make it a noun
Makes the noun definite
Adding conjunction prefix و to connect with previous phrase
The kasra ending shows this belongs to Allah, mentioned at the start of the ayah (لِلَّهِ)
This is a special word used to refer to things in general
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it in the ayah

steps.m: in (referring to being inside السَّمٰوٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: A word that shows location or position
Adds هِ (meaning 'it/him') to show what we are specifically inside of

steps.m: in them (feminine plural, referring to السَّمٰوٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: Adds نَّ to show we're talking about multiple feminine things (here, the heavens and earth are treated as feminine)

steps.m: he/it (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a pronoun used instead of repeating Allah's name. The ُ and َ marks help show it's independent (not connected to another word).

steps.m: and He/it (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it, showing that this is another statement about Allah
This is one of the most common Arabic words that shows something is on top of or over something else. It's a basic word that doesn't come from a root.
Added a small alif (ٰ) at the end to show this is connected to the word قَدِيرٌۢ (capable/able) that comes later, telling us what Allah is capable over.
The doubled ل with damma makes this a noun meaning 'everything without exception'

steps.m: every (connected to شَىْءٍ to mean 'every thing')
steps.n: The kasra shows this word is connected to شَىْءٍ (thing) that comes after it, forming the phrase 'every thing'
The root letters ش-ي-أ were joined together and given vowel marks to make the common word شَيْء meaning 'a thing'. The hamza appears as ء at the end.
Added two dots (kasratain) at the end to show this thing belongs to or is connected to something else. The letter shape changes slightly but pronunciation stays the same.

steps.m: to have power or ability over something

steps.n: Root letters connected with vowels to make an active participle meaning 'one who has power'
Added ي to make it mean 'very powerful' instead of just 'powerful' - like adding 'very' or 'all' in English
Added tanween dammah to show this is describing Allah in this ayah
